Vegetable Cultivation (159) -

Blanch Celery Stems
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Blanching Celery

1. Start Blanching Early: Begin blanching 10 days to two weeks before harvesting.
2. Choose a Method: Select one of the following methods:
- Mounding Soil: Build up soil around each side of the...
Procedure for Blanching Celery

1. Start Blanching Early: Begin blanching 10 days to two weeks before harvesting.
2. Choose a Method: Select one of the following methods:
- Mounding Soil: Build up soil around each side of the celery row and mound it to the tops of the stalks.
- Use Boards: Place tilted boards over the plants to shade them.
- Wrap with Paper: Use heavy paper (freezer paper or layers of newspaper) wrapped around each plant, fastened with a rubber band.
- Milk Cartons: Cut out the top and bottom of milk cartons and place them over the plants.
- Cylinder-Shaped Tiles: Gather the stalks together and fit cylinder-shaped tiles over the tops of the plants.
3. Limit Duration: Leave blanching materials on for no more than 10 days to two weeks, or the celery may become pithy and rot.
4. Avoid Bitterness: Blanching prevents bitterness in the stalks by reducing chlorophyll production.
blanching celery, how to blanch celery, preventing bitterness in celery
Harvest Celery
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Harvesting Celery

1. Determine Maturity: Harvest celery when the head is about two to three inches in diameter at the base.
2. Time from Planting: From transplants, this takes 100 to 130 days; from seed, it takes about 20...
Procedure for Harvesting Celery

1. Determine Maturity: Harvest celery when the head is about two to three inches in diameter at the base.
2. Time from Planting: From transplants, this takes 100 to 130 days; from seed, it takes about 20 days longer.
3. Harvest Before Frost: Begin harvesting before the first hard frost.
4. Cut at Soil Level: Cut off the head at or slightly below soil level for optimal yield and quality.
5. Yield Estimation: A 10-foot row should yield about 20 heads of celery.
celery harvesting, when to harvest celery, cutting celery for storage
Blanch Cardoon Stalks for Improved Flavor and Tenderness
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Blanch cardoon when the plant is about three feet tall, which should be four to six weeks before harvesting.
2. Tie the leaves together in a bunch around the stem.
3. Wrap paper or burlap around the stems to block sunlight...
Procedure:

1. Blanch cardoon when the plant is about three feet tall, which should be four to six weeks before harvesting.
2. Tie the leaves together in a bunch around the stem.
3. Wrap paper or burlap around the stems to block sunlight and encourage blanching.
4. Alternatively, hill up the soil around the stem to achieve the same effect.
blanching, cardoon, flavor, tenderness, gardening
Caring for Mushrooms During Growth
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Fertilizing and Watering Mushrooms

1. You do not need to fertilize mushrooms.
2. Keep them moist at all times.
3. Avoid letting the mushrooms dry out, but also ensure that water does not stand on the soil.
mushrooms,caring,watering,gardening
Choose Vegetables to Grow
Standard Source: almanac start a garden
Choosing Which Vegetables to Grow

Select vegetables that are well-suited for your climate, soil type, and available space. Consider the following:

1. Climate Compatibility: Choose varieties that thrive in your local growing zone.
2. **Soil...
Choosing Which Vegetables to Grow

Select vegetables that are well-suited for your climate, soil type, and available space. Consider the following:

1. Climate Compatibility: Choose varieties that thrive in your local growing zone.
2. Soil Type: Some vegetables prefer sandy soils, while others do better in clay or loamy soils.
3. Space Requirements: Decide whether you have enough room for row planting, raised beds, or container gardening.
4. Family Preferences: Grow vegetables that your household enjoys eating to ensure a successful harvest.
5. Ease of Growing: Start with beginner-friendly vegetables such as tomatoes, lettuce, carrots, and beans.

Tips for Success:
• Research local growing conditions and consult extension services if available.
• Consider the time it takes for each vegetable to mature before planting.
• Choose disease-resistant varieties when possible.
choose vegetables, gardening tips, vegetable selection, beginner gardening
Control Weeds Around Celery Plants
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Controlling Weeds in Celery Gardens

1. Cultivate Regularly: Cultivate the soil regularly to control weeds during the slow early growth stage of celery.
2. Avoid Root Damage: Take care not to damage the roots, which are...
Procedure for Controlling Weeds in Celery Gardens

1. Cultivate Regularly: Cultivate the soil regularly to control weeds during the slow early growth stage of celery.
2. Avoid Root Damage: Take care not to damage the roots, which are close to the soil surface.
3. Maintain Soil Health: Keep the area around celery plants free from weeds to ensure optimal growth and reduce competition for nutrients.
weeding celery garden, weed control for celery, soil cultivation for celery
Dealing with Tomato Planting Issues and Environmental Factors
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedures for handling special planting issues:

1. Fruit setting problems:
- Tomato plants may not set fruit in rainy or very humid weather.
- A plant with plenty of water and fertilizer may produce foliage but no tomatoes.
2....
Procedures for handling special planting issues:

1. Fruit setting problems:
- Tomato plants may not set fruit in rainy or very humid weather.
- A plant with plenty of water and fertilizer may produce foliage but no tomatoes.
2. Resolving fruiting issues:
- As a last resort, prune the plant back and reduce watering to give it a shock; this may stimulate fruit production.
fruiting issues, environmental factors, tomato care
Establishing an Asparagus Bed
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Choose a long-lived hardy perennial site with well-drained soil.
2. Prepare the bed in advance, as it is virtually impossible to move once established.
3. Ensure the soil freezes slightly during winter for proper...
Procedure:

1. Choose a long-lived hardy perennial site with well-drained soil.
2. Prepare the bed in advance, as it is virtually impossible to move once established.
3. Ensure the soil freezes slightly during winter for proper dormancy.
4. **Plant asparagus crowns four to six weeks before your area's average date of last frost."
5. Be patient when establishing an asparagus bed, as it takes time to become productive.
6. Once established, the asparagus bed will provide harvest for many years.
asparagus bed establishment,winter dormancy,planting timing,long-term cultivation
Fertilize and Water Eggplants
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Apply a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet before planting.
2. Fertilize again at midseason using the same rate as applied initially.
3. Maintain even...
Procedure:

1. Apply a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet before planting.
2. Fertilize again at midseason using the same rate as applied initially.
3. Maintain even soil moisture to ensure consistent growth and prevent root rot.
4. Avoid overwatering, especially in soils with poor drainage.

Notes:
• Eggplants are sensitive to both excessive and insufficient moisture.
eggplant,fertilizing,watering,gardening,cultivation
Forcing Asparagus in Pits
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use pits where succession pines are kept during the summer. Ensure the bottom of the pit has a layer of leaves about eighteen inches deep, covered with an equal thickness of tan.
2. Once the pines are removed, spread asparagus...
Procedure:

1. Use pits where succession pines are kept during the summer. Ensure the bottom of the pit has a layer of leaves about eighteen inches deep, covered with an equal thickness of tan.
2. Once the pines are removed, spread asparagus roots over the entire surface of the old tan.
3. Cover the roots with six inches more of tan and apply linings of hot dung around the sides.
4. Renew the hot dung regularly to maintain a good heat.
5. This method was successfully used by Mr. William Boss in mid-December, resulting in a crop fit for use within five weeks.
6. As soon as shoots appear, remove the lights during the day and introduce as much air as possible to give them a natural color and size comparable to those grown outdoors at the usual season.
asparagus, forcing, gardening, vegetable cultivation, hot-bed
Grow Carrots in the Garden
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained soil.
2. Prepare the soil by loosening it to a depth of at least 12 inches and incorporating organic matter or compost for improved fertility.
3. Sow carrot seeds directly into...
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained soil.
2. Prepare the soil by loosening it to a depth of at least 12 inches and incorporating organic matter or compost for improved fertility.
3. Sow carrot seeds directly into the garden bed, spacing them approximately 1 inch apart in rows that are 18 to 24 inches apart.
4. Cover the seeds with a thin layer of soil (about ¼ inch deep) and keep the soil consistently moist until germination occurs.
5. Thin seedlings to 2 to 3 inches apart once they have developed their first set of true leaves.
carrots, planting, garden, soil preparation
Grow Celery in Cool Weather
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Growing Celery in Cool Conditions

1. Choose the Right Time: Grow celery in spring in the North, planting transplants two to three weeks before the average date of last frost.
2. Plant in Late Summer (South): In the South,...
Procedure for Growing Celery in Cool Conditions

1. Choose the Right Time: Grow celery in spring in the North, planting transplants two to three weeks before the average date of last frost.
2. Plant in Late Summer (South): In the South, plant celery in late summer.
3. Avoid Cold Weather: Cold weather will inhibit growth; avoid planting during excessively cold conditions.
4. Prefer Cool Nights: Celery thrives in cool nights and performs best in cool weather.
celery growing conditions, planting celery in spring, cool weather for celery
Grow Transplants at Home from Seed
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
To grow transplants at home from seed, follow these steps:

1. Determine the correct planting time:
- Use your area's average date of last frost as a reference point for when to plant each vegetable in the garden.
- Set your indoor...
To grow transplants at home from seed, follow these steps:

1. Determine the correct planting time:
- Use your area's average date of last frost as a reference point for when to plant each vegetable in the garden.
- Set your indoor planting date six to eight weeks ahead of your outdoor planting date for all vegetables.

2. Use proper planting medium:
- Avoid regular potting soil, which is too rich and can cause young plants to grow too quickly.
- Use a sterilized seed-starter medium from a reputable garden center to prevent damping-off disease.

3. Choose appropriate containers:
- Use flats (low-sided plastic trays or shallow containers with drainage holes) for planting seeds.
- For vegetables that do not transplant well, use plantable containers like peat pots or Jiffy pellets.
- Peat pots are compressed peat shaped into a pot and come in various sizes.
- Jiffy 7 pellets have a net wrapping that disintegrates over time; Jiffy 9 pellets do not have the wrapping and disintegrate faster but crumble more easily.

4. Label all containers:
- Mark each container or row with labels to avoid confusion, especially for similar-looking seedlings like cabbage family plants, peppers, and tomatoes.
- Labels also help you track when seeds were planted.
transplanting from seed, home gardening, planting medium, plantable containers, labeling
Growing Lima Beans: Varieties and Characteristics
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Varieties:
• Bush lima: Burpee Improved Bush (75 days), Fordhook 242 (75 days) — both resistant to bean mosaic; Allgreen (67 days); Thorogreen (66 days).
• Pole lima: King of the Garden (90 days), Prizetaker (90...
Varieties:
• Bush lima: Burpee Improved Bush (75 days), Fordhook 242 (75 days) — both resistant to bean mosaic; Allgreen (67 days); Thorogreen (66 days).
• Pole lima: King of the Garden (90 days), Prizetaker (90 days).

Description:
• Lima beans are tender, large-seeded annuals that grow as either a bush or vine.
• Mature seeds are eaten, not the entire pod.
• Require warmer soil and higher temperatures than snap beans for germination and growth.
• Bush lima beans are easier to handle due to shorter height and earlier maturity.
• Pole lima beans require trellises but produce more beans per plant.
lima beans, varieties, growing conditions, vegetable gardening
Growing Mushrooms at Home
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
How to Grow Mushrooms at Home

1. Mushrooms can be grown indoors and are not affected by the climate you live in.
2. They can be grown at any time of the year, but kits and trays are typically available commercially from October through...
How to Grow Mushrooms at Home

1. Mushrooms can be grown indoors and are not affected by the climate you live in.
2. They can be grown at any time of the year, but kits and trays are typically available commercially from October through April.
3. Choose a dark, humid, cool area for growing mushrooms — such as a basement or under the kitchen sink.
4. Maintain high humidity (80–85%) and a cool temperature (55°–60°F).
5. Mushrooms can be grown in two forms:
- Kits: Prepared trays with growing medium and mushroom spores are available commercially. Simply remove the tray from the package, add an inch of topsoil, and water it.
- Spawn: Purchase spawn from seed companies and plant half-inch pieces about 2 inches deep and 8–10 inches apart in well-rotted strawy horse or cow manure.
6. For kits:
- Keep them in a dark, humid place for about four weeks before harvesting mushrooms.
7. For spawn:
- Keep the planted spawn in a dark, humid room at 70°F for the first 21 days.
- Lower the temperature to about 60°F and cover the bed with a one-inch layer of sterilized topsoil.
8. If conditions are right, you should be able to start harvesting mushrooms within about four weeks.
mushrooms,growing,homesteading
Harvest and Store Cardoon Stalks
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Harvest the plants four to six weeks after blanching.
2. Cut them off at ground level using clean, sharp tools.
3. Trim off the outer leaves before storage.
4. To store fresh stalks:
- Keep them on root.
- Wrap them...
Procedure:

1. Harvest the plants four to six weeks after blanching.
2. Cut them off at ground level using clean, sharp tools.
3. Trim off the outer leaves before storage.
4. To store fresh stalks:
- Keep them on root.
- Wrap them securely in a damp cloth or paper towel.
- Refrigerate for one to two weeks.
5. For long-term storage:
- Place the plants in a cold, moist place where they can be kept for two to three months.
6. Cardoon freezes fairly well and can also be canned or dried, similar to how you would handle celery.
harvesting, storing, cardoon, preservation
Harvesting Artichokes
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Determine the time from planting to harvest:
- 50 to 100 days for artichokes grown from suckers.
- At least one year until the first bud forms when grown from seed.
2. Harvest by cutting off the globe artichoke bud...
Procedure:

1. Determine the time from planting to harvest:
- 50 to 100 days for artichokes grown from suckers.
- At least one year until the first bud forms when grown from seed.
2. Harvest by cutting off the globe artichoke bud with one to 1½ inches of stem before the bud begins to open.

Notes:
• Harvesting should be done promptly to ensure quality and flavor.
artichoke harvesting,harvest timing,stem cutting,bud removal
Harvesting Carrots
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Time from planting to harvest is 55 to 80 days, depending on the variety.
2. Small finger carrots are usually ready in 60 days or less; other varieties may take longer.
3. Check readiness by pulling a few sample carrots...
Procedure:

1. Time from planting to harvest is 55 to 80 days, depending on the variety.
2. Small finger carrots are usually ready in 60 days or less; other varieties may take longer.
3. Check readiness by pulling a few sample carrots and examining their size.
4. For regular varieties, harvest when they are at least three quarters inch thick.
5. Pull carrots up by hand or use a spading fork to lift them gently from the ground.
6. Harvest carrots when the soil is moist — pulling from hard ground may break the roots.
carrots, harvesting, timing, readiness, soil moisture
How to Plant Lima Beans
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Planting Instructions:
1. After the last frost, choose a full-sun bed (partial shade is acceptable but may reduce yield).
2. Prepare soil by mixing in one pound of 5-10-10 fertilizer.
3. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers as they promote foliage...
Planting Instructions:
1. After the last frost, choose a full-sun bed (partial shade is acceptable but may reduce yield).
2. Prepare soil by mixing in one pound of 5-10-10 fertilizer.
3. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers as they promote foliage over bean production.

Planting Techniques:
• Bush lima beans: Plant seeds an inch deep, two inches apart in rows at least 18 to 24 inches apart.
• Pole lima beans: Plant seeds four to six inches apart in rows 30 to 36 inches apart or use inverted hills with five to six seeds per hill spaced 30 inches apart.

Support Structures:
• Set up trellises or supports at the time of planting for pole varieties.

Thinning Plants:
• Thin seedlings to four to six inches apart once they are growing well.
• Use scissors to cut seedlings at ground level; avoid disturbing other plants.

Note: Beans tolerate crowding and can use each other for support.
planting lima beans, soil preparation, planting techniques
Improving Asparagus Productivity Through Plant Selection and Care
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. When forming asparagus beds, select only male plants.
2. Identify male plants by not planting them from the seed-bed until they have flowered.
3. Transplant one-year-old male plants into other beds at six-inch intervals.
4. Allow...
Procedure:

1. When forming asparagus beds, select only male plants.
2. Identify male plants by not planting them from the seed-bed until they have flowered.
3. Transplant one-year-old male plants into other beds at six-inch intervals.
4. Allow them to remain in these beds until they flower (usually in the second year).
5. Mark each male plant with a small stick and remove female plants unless you are testing the method on a small scale.
6. Towards the end of July, especially during rainy weather, cut down the stalks of asparagus.
7. Fork up the beds and rake them smooth.
8. If the soil is dry, water the beds with the draining liquid from a dunghill; shape the beds to be flat or hollow in the center for better water retention.
9. Water once or twice a week if the weather remains dry.
10. Asparagus will begin to appear after about twelve to fourteen days and can be harvested by the end of September.
11. After harvesting, hot-beds can take over, allowing for a regular succession of asparagus throughout the year.
12. Make five or six hot-beds during winter to ensure a monthly supply of asparagus.
asparagus, productivity, gardening, vegetable cultivation, bed preparation
Pest and Disease Management for Mushrooms
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Managing Pests and Diseases in Mushroom Cultivation

1. Pests: When growing mushrooms at home, pests do not present serious problems.
2. Diseases: Mushrooms grown at home have no serious disease problems.
mushrooms,disease,pests,gardening
Plant Cardoon Transplants in the Spring
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with full sun, though cardoon can tolerate partial shade.
2. Ensure the soil is well-drained and fertile.
3. Dig in a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or **10...
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with full sun, though cardoon can tolerate partial shade.
2. Ensure the soil is well-drained and fertile.
3. Dig in a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
4. Plant transplants three to four weeks after the average date of last frost in your area.
5. If starting from seed, begin six weeks ahead of your planting date.
6. Space young plants 18 to 24 inches apart, with 36 to 48 inches between rows.
cardoon, planting, transplants, spring, garden, soil preparation, fertilizer
Plant Eggplant Seedlings
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Start eggplant seedlings indoors about two months before your intended outdoor planting date.
2. Wait until two to three weeks after the average last frost date in your area before transplanting them outdoors.
3. Ensure that...
Procedure:

1. Start eggplant seedlings indoors about two months before your intended outdoor planting date.
2. Wait until two to three weeks after the average last frost date in your area before transplanting them outdoors.
3. Ensure that daytime temperatures are at least 70°F (21°C) before planting.
4. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained soil rich in organic matter.
5. Prepare the soil by incorporating one pound of complete, well-balanced fertilizer per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
6. Plant seedlings 18 to 24 inches apart in rows that are 24 to 36 inches apart.

Notes:
• Eggplants require warm temperatures and cannot tolerate cold conditions.
eggplant,planting,gardening,cultivation
Planting Asparagus Using a Flat Bed Method
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Prepare a flat bed that is five feet wide, using good soil without any dung.
2. Sow the bed with onions.
3. Plant two asparagus seeds near each other, about one inch deep, spaced twelve inches apart in all directions.
4. If the...
Procedure:

1. Prepare a flat bed that is five feet wide, using good soil without any dung.
2. Sow the bed with onions.
3. Plant two asparagus seeds near each other, about one inch deep, spaced twelve inches apart in all directions.
4. If the spring is cold and dry, allow weeds to grow until rain comes.
5. In October, cover the bed with manure or rotten hot-bed material.
6. The next spring, remove the weakest of the two plants from each pair.
7. Keep the bed free from weeds throughout the growing season.
8. To raise seed, select the thickest stems after sufficient blossoming and cut off the tops to strengthen the seed.
9. This method is also suitable for raising double ten-weeks and Brompton stocks.
10. Use six pounds of seeds for any strong plant; planting them near double ones has no benefit.
11. The excess in petal development arises from cultivation and transplanting into rich soil; wild flowers are seldom double.
12. Keep all small seeds in the pod until ready to sow.
asparagus, planting, gardening, vegetable cultivation, seed raising
Planting Carrots in Rows
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with full sun (carrots will tolerate partial shade).
2. Prepare the soil by working half a cup of low nitrogen (5-10-10) fertilizer into the soil.
3. Turn the soil thoroughly to a depth of about 10 or 12 inches...
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with full sun (carrots will tolerate partial shade).
2. Prepare the soil by working half a cup of low nitrogen (5-10-10) fertilizer into the soil.
3. Turn the soil thoroughly to a depth of about 10 or 12 inches to ensure it is loose and free from lumps, rocks, or other obstructions that could cause roots to split, fork, or become deformed.
4. Sow carrot seeds in rows spaced 12 to 24 inches apart.
5. In early spring, cover the seeds with a quarter to a half inch of soil.
6. When the soil is dryer and warmer (later in the season), plant the seeds slightly deeper.
7. Once seedlings are growing well, thin them out so that they are 2 to 4 inches apart.
carrots, planting, rows, spacing, soil preparation
Planting Chick Pea Seeds
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Plant seeds one inch deep and two inches apart in rows that are at least 18 to 24 inches apart. Avoid soaking the seeds before planting, and do not overwater immediately after planting. Ensure the soil moisture is moderate for proper germination.
planting chick peas, seed depth, gardening
Planting Lima Beans: Timing and Conditions
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
When to Plant:
• Plant two weeks after the average date of last spring frost.
• Use growing season length and days to maturity for latest planting date.
• If planting before frost, start seeds indoors in peat pots and transplant when soil warms...
When to Plant:
• Plant two weeks after the average date of last spring frost.
• Use growing season length and days to maturity for latest planting date.
• If planting before frost, start seeds indoors in peat pots and transplant when soil warms up.

Ideal Conditions:
• Soil temperature should be at least 65°F for germination.
• Plant after last frost.
• Time planting so beans mature before very hot weather (above 80°F).

Planting Strategy:
• Plant bush beans every two weeks to extend harvest.
• Start with bush beans and follow up with pole beans if needed.
planting lima beans, timing, growing conditions
Planting Okra Seeds in Warm Weather
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Determine the average date of last frost in your area.
2. Plant okra seeds four weeks after this date, as it is a warm-season crop sensitive to cold temperatures.
3. Choose well-drained, warm soil for planting and...
Procedure:

1. Determine the average date of last frost in your area.
2. Plant okra seeds four weeks after this date, as it is a warm-season crop sensitive to cold temperatures.
3. Choose well-drained, warm soil for planting and ensure full sun exposure.
4. Avoid growing okra in containers, as it does not thrive in such conditions.
5. Plant seeds ½ inch to 1 inch deep in rows spaced 24–36 inches apart.
okra planting, frost date, warm soil, container avoidance
Planting Tomato Seeds and Seedlings
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step-by-step procedure for planting tomatoes:

1. Select the right location: Plant tomatoes in full sun with warm, well-drained, fertile soil.
2. Soil preparation:
- Work in a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one...
Step-by-step procedure for planting tomatoes:

1. Select the right location: Plant tomatoes in full sun with warm, well-drained, fertile soil.
2. Soil preparation:
- Work in a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
3. Planting seeds:
- Plant seeds half an inch deep in rows spaced 24 to 48 inches apart (depending on variety size).
4. Thinning seedlings:
- When seedlings are large enough to handle, thin them to 18 to 36 inches apart.
5. Transplanting:
- Transplant on a cloudy day or in the late afternoon if the sun is very hot.
- Protect plants with hats made of newspapers if necessary.
- Disturb roots as little as possible during transplanting.
- Gently slip plants out of clay and plastic pots. If using peat pots, plant the entire container with the top below soil level to prevent moisture loss.
6. Plant depth:
- Plant tomatoes deeper than they were growing before, up to the first leaves.
- For spindly stems, lay them on a slant so only the leaves are above ground; roots will grow from the submerged stem, creating a sturdier plant.
7. Spacing: Set transplants 18 to 36 inches apart in rows spaced 24 to 48 inches apart.
tomatoes, planting, seedlings, soil preparation, transplanting, spacing
Preparing Soil and Planting Okra Seeds
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Prepare the soil by digging in a complete well-balanced fertilizer at a rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
2. Plant okra seeds ½ inch to 1 inch deep in rows spaced 24–36 inches...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the soil by digging in a complete well-balanced fertilizer at a rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
2. Plant okra seeds ½ inch to 1 inch deep in rows spaced 24–36 inches apart.
3. Thin seedlings to stand 12–18 inches apart once they are growing strongly.
okra soil preparation, fertilizer rate, thinning seedlings
Provide Special Handling for Eggplants in Cold and Hot Climates
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. In areas with unpredictable late frosts:
- Provide protection at night until all danger of frost has passed.
2. In hot climates:
- Mulch the plants about a month after transplanting to keep soil temperatures cool for root...
Procedure:

1. In areas with unpredictable late frosts:
- Provide protection at night until all danger of frost has passed.
2. In hot climates:
- Mulch the plants about a month after transplanting to keep soil temperatures cool for root health.
3. Stake heavy fruit-bearing plants to prevent them from toppling under the weight of their produce.

Notes:
• These measures help ensure healthy growth and yield.
eggplant,cold,hot,climate,gardening,cultivation
Provide the Right Environment for Transplants
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
To provide the right environment for growing transplants at home:

1. Maintain cleanliness:
- Keep your workspace and containers clean to prevent disease and promote healthy growth.

2. Control temperature:
- Maintain a consistent...
To provide the right environment for growing transplants at home:

1. Maintain cleanliness:
- Keep your workspace and containers clean to prevent disease and promote healthy growth.

2. Control temperature:
- Maintain a consistent temperature that is appropriate for the specific vegetable variety you are growing.

3. Manage moisture levels:
- Ensure proper watering without over-saturation, as excess moisture can lead to damping-off or root rot.

4. Provide adequate light:
- Use grow lights or place containers near a sunny window to ensure sufficient light for seedlings.

5. Use proper planting medium and containers:
- As outlined in the 'Grow Transplants at Home from Seed' procedure, use sterilized seed-starter medium and appropriate containers like flats, peat pots, or Jiffy pellets.
transplant care, environment for plants, temperature control, moisture management, light requirements
Select Carrot Varieties Based on Size and Growth Time
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Choose a carrot variety based on the size and growth time that best suits your needs:
- Short (2 to 4 inches): Goldinhart, Amstel, Gold Nugget, Sweet and Short (60-65 days).
- Finger (3 to 4 inches): Little Finger,...
Procedure:

1. Choose a carrot variety based on the size and growth time that best suits your needs:
- Short (2 to 4 inches): Goldinhart, Amstel, Gold Nugget, Sweet and Short (60-65 days).
- Finger (3 to 4 inches): Little Finger, Minipak, Tiny Sweet (60-65 days).
- Half-long (5 to 6 inches): Danvers Halflong (75 days), Royal Chantenay (70 days), Gold King (70 days).
- Cylindrical (6 to 7 inches): Nantes Coreless (68 days), Tuchon Pioneer (75 days), Royal Cross Hybrid (70 days).
- Standard (7 to 9 inches): Tendersweet (75 days), Spartan Bonus (77 days), Gold Pak (75 days), Imperator (75 days).
carrot varieties, gardening, vegetable selection
Selecting Vegetable Varieties Suited to Your Area
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Finding the varieties suited to your area. Because there are so many varieties, it can be very difficult to choose the right one. Part 2 of this book describes the individual vegetables and their cultural requirements and lists some of the best...
Finding the varieties suited to your area. Because there are so many varieties, it can be very difficult to choose the right one. Part 2 of this book describes the individual vegetables and their cultural requirements and lists some of the best and most widely used varieties. But in many cases the varieties listed represent only the tip of the iceberg. Where a large number of varieties are available (as with corn or tomatoes) or where success depends as much on growing conditions as on variety (as with onions), your best bet is to get in touch with your local Cooperative Extension Service. The service's experts will be able to tell you exactly which varieties will do best in the growing conditions that exist in your part of the country.
selecting, vegetable, varieties, area, cooperative extension service
Special Handling for Carrots in Hot Soil Conditions
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. In areas with high soil temperatures, apply mulch to regulate the temperature.
2. This helps prevent roots from growing short and pale due to heat stress.
3. Control weeds, especially during the first few weeks of growth,...
Procedure:

1. In areas with high soil temperatures, apply mulch to regulate the temperature.
2. This helps prevent roots from growing short and pale due to heat stress.
3. Control weeds, especially during the first few weeks of growth, using shallow cultivation to avoid damaging the roots.
carrots, mulching, weeding, shallow cultivation
Staking and Pruning Tomato Plants
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
How to stake and prune tomatoes:

1. Staking:
- Use six-foot stakes (one by two inches) or reinforcing rods.
- Set supports at the time of transplanting.
2. Pruning staked plants:
- Prune so that only one straight stem grows.
...
How to stake and prune tomatoes:

1. Staking:
- Use six-foot stakes (one by two inches) or reinforcing rods.
- Set supports at the time of transplanting.
2. Pruning staked plants:
- Prune so that only one straight stem grows.
- Remove any suckers that appear below the first fruiting cluster.
3. Caging tomatoes:
- Use a five-foot width of wire, cut to five feet long, and bend into a cylinder by locking the ends.
- Remove the bottom strand and push the cage six inches deep around the tomato plant.
- In windy areas, drive in a supporting stake.
- Alternatively, use commercially produced square cages that fold flat for storage.
4. Pruning technique:
- Allow suckers to develop two leaves above the first fruiting cluster and then pinch out the rest of the sucker.
- The extra leaves will provide shade for the fruit.
staking, pruning, tomato support, caging
Timing and Successive Planting of Carrots
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Plant carrots in spring and early summer for a continuous crop.
2. Start planting two to three weeks after the average date of last frost in your area.
3. Continue planting successive crops every two to three weeks...
Procedure:

1. Plant carrots in spring and early summer for a continuous crop.
2. Start planting two to three weeks after the average date of last frost in your area.
3. Continue planting successive crops every two to three weeks until three months before the average date of first fall frost.
carrots, timing, successive planting, frost dates
Transplant Seedlings Properly to the Garden
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
To transplant seedlings properly to the garden:

1. Prepare the planting site:
- Ensure the soil is well-prepared with proper tilling, compost addition, and drainage.

2. Lift seedlings carefully:
- Use a knife, spatula, or stick to...
To transplant seedlings properly to the garden:

1. Prepare the planting site:
- Ensure the soil is well-prepared with proper tilling, compost addition, and drainage.

2. Lift seedlings carefully:
- Use a knife, spatula, or stick to lift seedlings from their containers.
- Hold them by their leaves to avoid damaging the stems.

3. Create planting holes:
- Make a hole in the new planting medium deep enough to accommodate the roots without crowding.

4. Plant the seedling:
- Place the seedling's roots into the hole and gently press the soil around the roots.

5. Water thoroughly:
- Water newly planted seedlings carefully to remove air pockets and ensure the roots are not left dry.

6. Label the plants:
- Mark each transplanted seedling with a label indicating the type of plant and the date it was planted.
transplanting technique, planting hole preparation, watering after transplanting, labeling plants
Using All-America Selections for Reliable Vegetable Varieties
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Guaranteed varieties: The All-America Selection. Another way to find the most reliable varieties for your area is through the All-America Selections. This is a nonprofit organization of seedsmen who develop and promote new varieties of...
Guaranteed varieties: The All-America Selection. Another way to find the most reliable varieties for your area is through the All-America Selections. This is a nonprofit organization of seedsmen who develop and promote new varieties of vegetables and flowers. The organization awards gold, silver, or bronze medals to vegetable varieties that have been proven to produce reliable results in most areas of the United States. If a vegetable is listed in your seed catalog as an All-America Selection, you can be sure that it has been tested by growers all over the country and that it's a good bet for your own garden. The organization does not bestow its seal of approval lightly — only one or two vegetable varieties win a gold medal in any year.
all-america selections, reliable, vegetable, varieties
Watering and Soil Moisture Management for Cauliflower
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Maintain abundant soil moisture and cool moist air to ensure optimal growth.
2. Avoid letting the ground dry out, as this can interrupt plant growth and prevent proper head formation.
3. Keep the plants growing vigorously by...
Procedure:
1. Maintain abundant soil moisture and cool moist air to ensure optimal growth.
2. Avoid letting the ground dry out, as this can interrupt plant growth and prevent proper head formation.
3. Keep the plants growing vigorously by ensuring consistent watering and monitoring environmental conditions such as heat or cold.

Notes:
• Water regularly, especially during hot or dry periods.
• Ensure good drainage to avoid waterlogging.
watering, soil moisture, cauliflower growth, irrigation
Winter Survival for Artichoke Roots in Cooler Areas
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Cut the plant back to about 10 inches.
2. Cover with a bushel basket to protect the roots.
3. Mulch with approximately two feet of leaves to maintain an even soil temperature during winter.

Notes:
• This procedure...
Procedure:

1. Cut the plant back to about 10 inches.
2. Cover with a bushel basket to protect the roots.
3. Mulch with approximately two feet of leaves to maintain an even soil temperature during winter.

Notes:
• This procedure is specifically for cooler areas where artichokes need protection from harsh winters.
• Artichokes bear best the second year and should be started from new plants every three to four years.
artichoke,winter survival,root protection,mulching,plant care

2. Grow local variety: Choose the variety available in your area, as few varieties are generally available.
asparagus varieties,growing time,local selection
Avoiding Disease in Soybeans
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Monitor soybean plants for signs of disease.
2. Ensure proper planting and care practices to reduce the risk of disease.
3. Avoid overwatering or excessive moisture, which can promote fungal growth.
4. Rotate crops annually to...
Procedure:

1. Monitor soybean plants for signs of disease.
2. Ensure proper planting and care practices to reduce the risk of disease.
3. Avoid overwatering or excessive moisture, which can promote fungal growth.
4. Rotate crops annually to prevent soil-borne diseases from building up in the same area.
5. If any signs of disease appear (e.g., leaf spots, wilting), remove and destroy affected plants immediately.

Notes:
• Soybeans generally have few serious disease problems when grown under proper conditions.
• Good garden hygiene and care practices are essential for disease prevention.
soybean,disease prevention,gardening,crop rotation,hygiene
Blanching Cauliflower to Achieve a Perfect Head
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Blanch cauliflower when the head reaches the size of an egg.
2. Gather three or four leaves and tie them together over the head to shield it from sunlight.
3. Secure the leaves with colored rubber bands to keep track of...
Procedure:
1. Blanch cauliflower when the head reaches the size of an egg.
2. Gather three or four leaves and tie them together over the head to shield it from sunlight.
3. Secure the leaves with colored rubber bands to keep track of cauliflowers tied at different times.
4. Check the heads occasionally for pests that may be hiding inside.

Notes:
• Self-blanching varieties do not need to be tied but will not blanch in hot weather.
• Blanching is a cosmetic procedure and does not significantly improve flavor.
blanching, cauliflower head formation, pest prevention
Choosing Lettuce Varieties for Home Gardening
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Recommended Varieties:
• Butterhead and Bibb Lettuces: Easier to grow than iceberg lettuce; have loose heads and delicate crunchy leaves.
• Celtuce (Stem Lettuce): Grown similarly to regular lettuce, but you want it to bolt. Harvest the...
Recommended Varieties:
• Butterhead and Bibb Lettuces: Easier to grow than iceberg lettuce; have loose heads and delicate crunchy leaves.
• Celtuce (Stem Lettuce): Grown similarly to regular lettuce, but you want it to bolt. Harvest the thickened stem for use like celery and use the leaves as regular lettuce.
• Cos or Romaine Lettuce: Forms a loose, long head; has a flavor between butterhead and leaf lettuce.
• Leaf Lettuce: Delightfully easy to grow, fast-growing, and provides bulk and color to salads.
lettuce,varieties,gardening,vegetables
Choosing Suitable Vegetables for Transplants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Choosing Suitable Vegetables for Transplants

When selecting vegetables to buy as transplants, consider the following:

• Best Candidates: Broccoli, Brussels sprouts, cabbage, cauliflower, chard, lettuce, and tomatoes are easy to...
Choosing Suitable Vegetables for Transplants

When selecting vegetables to buy as transplants, consider the following:

• Best Candidates: Broccoli, Brussels sprouts, cabbage, cauliflower, chard, lettuce, and tomatoes are easy to transplant.
• Moderate Tolerance: Celery, eggplant, onions, and peppers require more careful handling but can still be successfully transplanted.
• Poor Candidates: Beans, corn, cucumbers, okra, peas, summer and winter squash, and watermelons do not transplant well unless your growing season is too short to grow from seed.

Note: If you purchase these poor candidates as transplants, ensure they are in individual containers that can be planted directly into the garden.
transplant selection,vegetable suitability,hardiness of plants
Compost Used in Kew Garden for Cultivating Cucumbers
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

Ingredients:
• One-third light loam from the common
• One-third well-decomposed dung
• Leaf mould and heath earth, each one-third part

Steps:
1. Mix all ingredients thoroughly to create a compost.
2. Use this mixture for...
Procedure:

Ingredients:
• One-third light loam from the common
• One-third well-decomposed dung
• Leaf mould and heath earth, each one-third part

Steps:
1. Mix all ingredients thoroughly to create a compost.
2. Use this mixture for cultivating cucumbers in hot-beds or similar environments.

Notes:
• This is the compost used at Kew Garden for cucumber cultivation.
Kew garden compost,cucumber,mixture
Cultivate Radishes for Year-Round Harvest
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To cultivate Radishes to have them at all Seasons:

1. Take seeds of the common radish and steep them in rain water for twenty-four hours.
2. Place the wet seeds into a small linen bag, well tied with packthread.
3. If you...
To cultivate Radishes to have them at all Seasons:

1. Take seeds of the common radish and steep them in rain water for twenty-four hours.
2. Place the wet seeds into a small linen bag, well tied with packthread.
3. If you have a large quantity, divide them into several bags.
4. Expose the bags to the greatest heat of the sun for about twenty-four hours until the seeds begin to grow.
5. Sow the seeds in earth well exposed to the heat of the sun in the usual manner.
6. Prepare two small tubs that cover each other exactly (can be made by sawing a cask through the middle).
7. In winter, use both tubs; in summer, one will suffice for each kind of sown earth.
8. After sowing, cover the seeds with the tub.
9. After three days, you will find radishes the size and thickness of young lettuces, with two small round leaves at their extremities.
10. These radishes can be cut or pulled up and are excellent in a salad, with a more delicate taste than common radishes.

For winter cultivation:
1. Steep the seeds in warm water, then expose them to heat as directed.
2. Warm the two tubs; fill one with earth well dunged.
3. Sow the prepared seeds in one of the tubs and cover it with the other tub.
4. Sprinkle with warm water as needed.
5. Carry the two tubs closely joined into a warm vault or cellar.
6. After fifteen days, you may gather a fine salad.
radishes,cultivate,winter,gardening,year-round
Cultivate Sea Kail
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To cultivate Sea Kail:

1. Sow the seeds in April in drills on a good light dry soil.
2. Keep the plants clean and thin them as they rise.
3. In the first winter, earth them up to protect from frost.
4. The following summer, thin...
To cultivate Sea Kail:

1. Sow the seeds in April in drills on a good light dry soil.
2. Keep the plants clean and thin them as they rise.
3. In the first winter, earth them up to protect from frost.
4. The following summer, thin them to about eighteen inches distance, leaving the best plants.
5. At Christmas, remove decayed leaves and cover each plant with a large deep pan or flowerpot.
6. Place a quantity of tree leaves on top to protect from frost and create heat for the plants.
7. Stable litter can be used instead, but it may give the plants a rank taste.
8. In April, the pots will be full of fine tender blanched shoots.
9. Cut the shoots over by the ground (but not too near) and cover the stumps again for a second crop.
10. Repeat this process two or three times during spring before leaving the plants for summer growth.
11. With proper culture, sea kail can be boiled in two waters and is equal to asparagus or broccoli.
12. It may be eaten with butter, or butter and vinegar and pepper, depending on taste.
13. Sea kail is a perennial plant that will last for any length of time with proper care.
sea kail,cultivate,perennial,vegetables,gardening
Cultivating Cauliflower to Prevent Weeds and Soil Crust Formation
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

2. Ensure that the soil's surface does not form a crust by loosening it with gentle cultivation.
3. Take care not to damage the roots during cultivation.

Notes:
-...
Procedure:
1. Cultivate cauliflower regularly to diminish weed competition.
2. Ensure that the soil's surface does not form a crust by loosening it with gentle cultivation.
3. Take care not to damage the roots during cultivation.

Notes:
• Use a shallow hoe or cultivator for this task.
• Cultivate frequently, especially after rain or irrigation.
cultivating, weed control, soil crust prevention, cauliflower
Cultivating Celeriac for Optimal Growth and Harvest
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Plant celeriac in well-drained soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0.
2. Ensure the plant receives full sun to partial shade for optimal growth.
3. Water regularly, especially during dry periods, but avoid overwatering to prevent root...
Procedure:
1. Plant celeriac in well-drained soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0.
2. Ensure the plant receives full sun to partial shade for optimal growth.
3. Water regularly, especially during dry periods, but avoid overwatering to prevent root rot.
4. Fertilize celeriac with compost or well-rotted manure at planting time and again midseason if needed.

Notes:
• Celeriac is a form of celery and shares similar growing requirements.
• The edible root should be large and swollen, like a turnip, for best results.
celeriac cultivation, root growth, soil preparation
Cultivating Cucumbers for Early Season Production
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. To produce cucumbers early in the season, use hot-beds, pits, or hot-houses.
2. Apply heat from fire, steam, or dung to the culture of cucumbers.
3. Dung is the most effective method for cultivating cucumbers at an early...
Procedure:

1. To produce cucumbers early in the season, use hot-beds, pits, or hot-houses.
2. Apply heat from fire, steam, or dung to the culture of cucumbers.
3. Dung is the most effective method for cultivating cucumbers at an early stage.

Notes:
• Cucumbers can grow in any soil as long as they receive sufficient heat, light, water, and air.
cucumber cultivation,early season,dung,heat
Cultivating Parsnips in Guernsey
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Cultivating Parsnips in Guernsey:

1. Soil Preparation:
- Choose a good light loam soil, preferably deep.
- If the loamy soil is not deep, ensure the underlying soil is opened to allow root penetration.
- Clear the...
Procedure for Cultivating Parsnips in Guernsey:

1. Soil Preparation:
- Choose a good light loam soil, preferably deep.
- If the loamy soil is not deep, ensure the underlying soil is opened to allow root penetration.
- Clear the land of weeds like coach grass using a paring-plough in October and harrow it.

2. Land Preparation for Sowing:
- In mid-February, prepare the land with two ploughs:
1. A small plough opens the furrow to a depth of four inches.
2. A large plough (drawn by four or six oxen and as many horses) deepens the furrow to ten or fourteen inches.
- Harrow the soil repeatedly until it is pulverized, resembling garden mould.

3. Sowing Parsnip Seeds:
- Use seed that is no older than one year.
- Sow the seeds broadcast on a windy day to ensure even spreading over the surface.
- Cover the seeds with a harrow.
- The quantity sown is between two and four quarts.

4. Weeding and Thinning:
- Begin hand-weeding and thinning when the plants are sufficiently strong, repeating this process at least three times during the summer.
- Maintain a distance of about nine inches between plants.
- A harrowing may be used between the first and second weedings to save labor.

5. Weeding Schedule:
- The first weeding is performed around mid-May, with subsequent weedings as needed until early July.

6. Harvesting Parsnips:
- Harvest the roots around mid-August when they are most nutritious and beneficial for fattening animals.
- Limit the quantity harvested to two or three days' consumption at this time.
- In October, the parsnips are fully ripe and can be dug up with forks and preserved dry in sheds during winter. However, in Guernsey (where frost is rare), they are usually left in the ground and harvested as needed.

7. Feeding Parsnips to Animals:
- Small parsnips can be given whole to animals.
- Large parsnips should be sliced longitudinally.
- Hogs prefer this root over others, and it produces excellent pork.
- Horses also enjoy parsnips but may choke if they eat them too quickly. To prevent this, cut the roots into pieces before feeding.
parsnip,cultivation,guernsey,farming,soil preparation,weeding,harvesting
Cultivating Sweet Potatoes
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Select a warm and moist growing location for sweet potatoes, as they are highly sensitive to frost.
2. Plant sweet potato tubers four weeks after the average date of last frost in your area or when the soil is thoroughly warm.
3....
Procedure:
1. Select a warm and moist growing location for sweet potatoes, as they are highly sensitive to frost.
2. Plant sweet potato tubers four weeks after the average date of last frost in your area or when the soil is thoroughly warm.
3. Ensure that the growing season is at least 150 days long, as sweet potatoes require a long period to mature properly.
4. In areas with shorter growing seasons, expect smaller yields and consider using varieties adapted for shorter cycles.

Notes:
• Sweet potatoes are often grown as ornamental plants due to their attractive vines.
sweet potato,growing,cultivation,warm climate
Experimenting with Different Vegetable Varieties
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Experiment with different varieties. Remember, too, that you don't always have to play by the rules. You can plant more than one variety of a vegetable and decide for yourself which one is best suited to your palate and your garden. You can also...
Experiment with different varieties. Remember, too, that you don't always have to play by the rules. You can plant more than one variety of a vegetable and decide for yourself which one is best suited to your palate and your garden. You can also extend your harvest by planting varieties that mature at different times. Experimenting is a good part of the fun of growing a vegetable garden.
experiment, variety, vegetable, harvest extension
Fertilize and Water Horseradish Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Initial fertilization:
- Apply fertilizer before planting at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
2. Midseason fertilization:
- Apply fertilizer again at midseason using the...
Procedure:

1. Initial fertilization:
- Apply fertilizer before planting at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
2. Midseason fertilization:
- Apply fertilizer again at midseason using the same rate as for the initial application.
3. Watering requirements:
- Keep the soil evenly moist to ensure tender and flavorful roots.
- Avoid letting the soil become dry, as horseradish becomes woody in dry conditions.
fertilizing horseradish, watering horseradish, soil moisture for horseradish
Fertilizing Asparagus After Harvest
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for fertilizing asparagus after harvest:

1. Apply high-nitrogen fertilizer: Use a high-nitrogen (15-10-10) fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
2. Timing: Apply this after...
Procedure for fertilizing asparagus after harvest:

1. Apply high-nitrogen fertilizer: Use a high-nitrogen (15-10-10) fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
2. Timing: Apply this after harvesting the spears.
3. Reference for detailed fertilizing information: See "Spadework: The Essential Soil" in Part I.
asparagus fertilization,nitrogen fertilizer,application rate
