Composting (101) -

Testing Composted Manure for Herbicide Contamination
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure to test composted manure before use:

1. Research and learn about testing methods for persistent herbicides in composted manure.
2. Use simple home tests or consult resources from reputable gardening experts to determine if the...
Procedure to test composted manure before use:

1. Research and learn about testing methods for persistent herbicides in composted manure.
2. Use simple home tests or consult resources from reputable gardening experts to determine if the composted manure is free of herbicide contamination.
3. If herbicides are detected, do not use the composted manure in garden beds or compost piles.
4. Follow detailed guidelines on how to deactivate persistent herbicides in compost if they have already been introduced into your pile.
5. Always read up on the topic before using horse manure in your compost or garden to avoid long-term damage to plants.
herbicide testing, persistent herbicides, compost safety, horse manure
Balance Carbon and Nitrogen in Compost Pile
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Achieve a proper balance of carbon (browns) and nitrogen (greens) to support efficient composting.

Steps:
1. Identify Carbon Sources (Browns): Use materials such as dried leaves, small twigs, yard debris, coffee grounds,...
Objective: Achieve a proper balance of carbon (browns) and nitrogen (greens) to support efficient composting.

Steps:
1. Identify Carbon Sources (Browns): Use materials such as dried leaves, small twigs, yard debris, coffee grounds, shredded paper, newspapers, paper towel rolls, and brown paper bags.
2. Identify Nitrogen Sources (Greens): Use fresh grass clippings, plant trimmings, and food scraps like vegetables and salad greens.
3. Layer Materials: Alternate layers of browns and greens in your compost pile to ensure a balanced mix.
4. Monitor the Mix: Regularly check the composition of your pile and adjust as needed by adding more browns or greens depending on what’s dominant.

Importance: A proper carbon-to-nitrogen ratio (approximately 30:1) supports microbial activity, which is essential for breaking down organic matter efficiently.
carbon nitrogen balance, compost pile, browns greens, microbial activity
Determine When Compost is Ready for Use
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Compost is ready for use when two key conditions are met: the original ingredients can no longer be recognized and the internal temperature has stabilized at ambient levels.

1. Visual inspection: The compost should look rich, earthy, and...
Compost is ready for use when two key conditions are met: the original ingredients can no longer be recognized and the internal temperature has stabilized at ambient levels.

1. Visual inspection: The compost should look rich, earthy, and not resemble any of the original materials used (e.g., twigs, paper, or food scraps).
2. Smell test: It should have a mild, earthy odor rather than an offensive or distinctive smell.
3. Temperature check: After turning the pile and ensuring sufficient moisture is present, measure the internal temperature of the compost. If it has stabilized at ambient levels after about a day, the compost is ready for use.
4. Additional stabilization (optional): For added benefits like increased disease-fighting microorganisms and humus content, allow the compost to rest for an additional month before using.
compost ready, temperature check, ambient levels, stabilization, humus
Joe’s Top 5 Tips for Quick Compost
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Follow these five tips to accelerate the composting process and achieve rich, usable compost more quickly.

1. Add all organic material available: Use both indoor and outdoor sources of organic matter (see the list at the back of this guide...
Follow these five tips to accelerate the composting process and achieve rich, usable compost more quickly.

1. Add all organic material available: Use both indoor and outdoor sources of organic matter (see the list at the back of this guide for compostable ingredients).
2. Keep ingredients small: Reduce the size of materials to increase surface area and speed up decomposition.
3. Seek extra feedstock: Obtain additional organic material from sources like coffee shop grounds, vegetable culls from produce departments, or animal bedding (e.g., chicken or rabbit bedding for carbon and nitrogen).
4. Mix ingredients frequently: Regularly mix the compost pile to ensure even decomposition and aeration.
5. Spray with water when mixing: Maintain moisture levels by spraying the pile every time you mix it.
quick compost, organic material, feedstock, mixing, moisture
Maintain Optimal Size for a Backyard Compost Pile
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
To ensure efficient and rapid decomposition in your backyard compost heap, maintain an optimal size.

1. The ideal size for a general backyard compost pile is approximately 4’ x 4’ x 4’. This size allows sufficient heat generation and microbial...
To ensure efficient and rapid decomposition in your backyard compost heap, maintain an optimal size.

1. The ideal size for a general backyard compost pile is approximately 4’ x 4’ x 4’. This size allows sufficient heat generation and microbial activity.
2. If the pile becomes too large, it may slow down the decomposition process due to increased mass that needs breaking down.
3. If the pile is too small, there may not be enough critical mass for microorganisms to function efficiently.
4. Once your compost reaches this optimal size, stop adding new ingredients and start a new pile nearby.
optimal size, backyard compost, 4x4x4, decomposition, microbial activity
Maintain Proper Moisture Level in Compost Pile
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Ensure your compost pile has the correct moisture level to support microbial activity.

Steps:
1. Assess Moisture: Think of the ideal moisture level as that of a damp sponge.
2. Test by Hand: Take a handful of compost and...
Objective: Ensure your compost pile has the correct moisture level to support microbial activity.

Steps:
1. Assess Moisture: Think of the ideal moisture level as that of a damp sponge.
2. Test by Hand: Take a handful of compost and squeeze it:
- If water drains from your hand, it's too wet.
- If it doesn't feel moist or bind together when squeezed, it's too dry.
3. Adjust Moisture: Add water if the pile is too dry, or add dry materials (like shredded paper or leaves) if it’s too wet.
4. Monitor Regularly: Check the moisture level frequently and adjust as needed to maintain optimal conditions for decomposition.

Importance: Proper moisture ensures that microorganisms can thrive, which accelerates the composting process and helps break down organic material efficiently.
moisture level, compost pile, damp sponge test, microbial activity
Properly Adding Livestock Manure to Compost
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for adding livestock manure to compost:

1. Use livestock manure in moderation due to its high nitrogen content.
2. If using excess manure, set it aside in a separate pile to compost on its own.
3. Composting manure first helps mellow...
Procedure for adding livestock manure to compost:

1. Use livestock manure in moderation due to its high nitrogen content.
2. If using excess manure, set it aside in a separate pile to compost on its own.
3. Composting manure first helps mellow the high nitrogen levels and prevents harm to plants.
4. Ensure that the compost pile reaches high temperatures during decomposition to kill off weed seeds present in the manure.
5. Avoid using horse manure that may be tainted with persistent herbicides, as these will not break down in your compost pile and can severely damage or kill garden plants.
6. Never add manure from carnivorous animals (e.g., dogs, cats, humans) due to the presence of dangerous pathogens.
manure, composting, nitrogen, weed seeds, herbicides, pathogens
Recognize Factors That Affect Composting Speed
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
The speed at which your compost breaks down depends on several factors:

• Material composition: The type of organic matter added to the pile significantly influences decomposition time. For example, woody materials take longer due to lignin...
The speed at which your compost breaks down depends on several factors:

• Material composition: The type of organic matter added to the pile significantly influences decomposition time. For example, woody materials take longer due to lignin content.
• Environmental conditions: Optimal temperatures and moisture levels accelerate microbial activity and decomposition.
• Aeration practices: Regular turning or mixing introduces oxygen, which is essential for aerobic decomposition. Lack of aeration leads to slower breakdown.
• Consistency of care: Spraying water regularly and maintaining pile structure ensures consistent microbial activity and faster composting.

Note: Even under ideal conditions, the time required can range from 8 weeks (in optimal environments) to several months or longer for absentee composters.
compost speed, decomposition factors, organic matter, lignin, aeration, moisture, composting time
Set Up a Hot Composting System for Faster Decomposition
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Create conditions that allow the compost pile to reach and maintain high temperatures (above 140°F) for rapid decomposition.

Materials Required:
• Nitrogen-rich materials (greens)
• Carbon-rich materials (browns)
• Compost...
Objective: Create conditions that allow the compost pile to reach and maintain high temperatures (above 140°F) for rapid decomposition.

Materials Required:
• Nitrogen-rich materials (greens)
• Carbon-rich materials (browns)
• Compost thermometer
• Water source
• Manual turning tool (e.g., pitchfork)

Steps:
1. Layer the compost pile with a 3:1 ratio of carbon to nitrogen material, ensuring that both types are well-mixed.
2. Maintain sufficient moisture in the pile by regularly adding water as needed.
3. Monitor temperature using a compost thermometer, aiming for temperatures above 140°F to kill pathogens and weed seeds.
4. Manually turn the pile periodically (every few days or so) to ensure even decomposition and oxygenation.

Notes:
• Hot composting requires active management, including regular turning and moisture checks.
• This method is ideal for those who want a faster composting process but are willing to invest time and effort into the process.
hot composting, temperature, nitrogen, carbon, decomposition
Set Up an Outdoor Composting Location for Indoor Compost Waste
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
If you are unable to complete the composting process indoors due to space limitations:

1. Identify a suitable outdoor location: Find a place outside your indoor living area where you can deposit your compostable ingredients. This could be your...
If you are unable to complete the composting process indoors due to space limitations:

1. Identify a suitable outdoor location: Find a place outside your indoor living area where you can deposit your compostable ingredients. This could be your own compost pile, a friend’s garden, or a community garden.
2. Use an intermediate storage container: If no outdoor composting site is available, set up an intermediate holding bucket in a location outside of your main living space to store the compostable materials temporarily.
3. Consider professional services: Research and identify companies or organizations that offer weekly pickup services for compostable waste. These services transport the waste to an outdoor composting facility where it can be completed.

Important considerations:
• Always ensure that your intermediate storage container is secure and odor-proof to prevent attracting pests or creating unpleasant smells.
• If you use a professional service, verify their reliability and schedule for regular pickups.
outdoor composting, intermediate storage bucket, professional pickup service, compost waste disposal
Shred or Break Down Compost Ingredients for Faster Decomposition
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
To speed up the decomposition process in your compost pile, it is essential to break down large pieces of organic material into smaller fragments.

1. Use a mower with a bagging attachment to shred grass and leaves.
2. Cut, chop, or shred...
To speed up the decomposition process in your compost pile, it is essential to break down large pieces of organic material into smaller fragments.

1. Use a mower with a bagging attachment to shred grass and leaves.
2. Cut, chop, or shred sticks, twigs, paper, and cardboard products before adding them to the compost bin.
3. Smaller particle sizes increase surface area, which accelerates microbial activity and decomposition.
4. While not strictly required, this step significantly improves the efficiency of your composting process.
shred, break down, compost ingredients, decomposition, surface area, compost pile
Use a Mix of Greens and Browns for Composting
Standard Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
While I don’t want to discount the importance of the C:N discussion, and its significance in the composting process, I don’t want you to get overwhelmed by it either. Frankly, I’ve never worried about trying to hit that just-right ratio. By...
While I don’t want to discount the importance of the C:N discussion, and its significance in the composting process, I don’t want you to get overwhelmed by it either. Frankly, I’ve never worried about trying to hit that just-right ratio. By providing a good mix of greens (nitrogen-rich materials) and browns (carbon-rich materials) from inside and outside the house, you and I will make beautiful compost quick enough.

Life’s too short to get bogged down in the details for me.
greens,browns,mix,compost,organic,waste
Accelerate Composting by Maintaining Optimal Conditions
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
To speed up the composting process, follow these steps:

1. Ensure a good balance of browns (carbon) and greens (nitrogen): This provides the necessary nutrients for microorganisms to thrive.
2. Turn or mix your compost pile weekly: Turning...
To speed up the composting process, follow these steps:

1. Ensure a good balance of browns (carbon) and greens (nitrogen): This provides the necessary nutrients for microorganisms to thrive.
2. Turn or mix your compost pile weekly: Turning introduces oxygen, which is essential for aerobic decomposition. More frequent turning leads to faster breakdown.
3. Maintain proper moisture levels: The pile should be as moist as a damp sponge, but not saturated. This ensures that microorganisms have enough water to function without drowning the pile.
4. Monitor and adjust conditions regularly: Check the pile's temperature and microbial activity. If temperatures drop or decomposition slows, turn the pile again to reintroduce oxygen and stimulate microbial activity.

Important notes:
• Microorganisms consume large amounts of oxygen, so regular aeration is crucial.
• Woody materials decompose more slowly due to lignin content; consider shredding them before adding to the pile for faster breakdown.
compost, composting, aeration, microorganisms, carbon, nitrogen, moisture, turning compost
Add Additional Trays to Worm Composter
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Adding additional trays helps manage the composting process efficiently and ensures optimal worm activity.

Steps:

1. Identify when to add a new tray:
- Add a new tray when the current feeding tray is full or has been active for...
Adding additional trays helps manage the composting process efficiently and ensures optimal worm activity.

Steps:

1. Identify when to add a new tray:
- Add a new tray when the current feeding tray is full or has been active for about 45-60 days.
- The material in the lower trays does not need to be fully composted before adding a new tray; worms will continue processing it.

2. Prepare the new tray:
- Add a small amount of finished vermicompost to the new tray. This introduces beneficial microbes and speeds up decomposition.
- Cover the new feeding tray with moist newspaper.
- Underneath the cover, add a good mixture of green and brown materials, ensuring at least ½ inch of browns (carbon-rich bedding) is included.

3. Position the new tray correctly:
- Ensure the grid of the new tray makes contact with the material in the lower processing tray.
- The new tray should not rest on the plastic rim of the tray below to allow worms to migrate upward as they process food.

4. Maintain a systematic rotation of trays:
- Each tray should be at a different stage of decomposition, which helps speed up the composting process and allows worms to work more efficiently.

5. Expand if needed:
The Worm Factory 360 comes with four composting trays but can accommodate up to seven. Additional trays can be ordered if your system generates more waste than it can handle.
worm composter,trays,adding trays,worm composting,vermicompost
Assembling a 3-Bin Composting System Using Pallets
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Preparation:
• Choose a level surface for assembling the composting system.

Materials Needed:
• Seven pallets: one for each outer wall, two for the dividers to separate the bins, and three across the back.

Assembly Steps:
1. Start...
Preparation:
• Choose a level surface for assembling the composting system.

Materials Needed:
• Seven pallets: one for each outer wall, two for the dividers to separate the bins, and three across the back.

Assembly Steps:
1. Start by attaching the left outer wall pallet to the back pallet using several screws.
2. Add one of the inner pallet dividers and secure it against the back pallet.
3. Continue with the remaining pallets, working your way across for the next two sections in the same manner.
4. Once all pallets are attached, you will have a basic set-up ready to begin composting.
3-bin compost system assembly, pallet bin construction, compost bin setup
Avoid Adding Animal Waste from Carnivores
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Prevent potential risks associated with adding animal waste from carnivores to your compost pile.

Steps:
1. Identify Carnivore Waste: Avoid adding any waste from carnivorous animals (e.g., cats, dogs, foxes).
2. **Dispose of...
Objective: Prevent potential risks associated with adding animal waste from carnivores to your compost pile.

Steps:
1. Identify Carnivore Waste: Avoid adding any waste from carnivorous animals (e.g., cats, dogs, foxes).
2. Dispose of Safely: If you have access to carnivore waste, dispose of it in the trash or follow local regulations for safe disposal.
3. Monitor Compost Pile: Ensure no carnivore waste is accidentally added to your compost pile.

Importance: Carnivore waste may contain pathogens that can pose a risk to human health and should not be used in home composting systems.
carnivore waste, compost pile, pathogens
Avoid Adding Chemically-Treated Plants and Grass
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Prevent the risk of chemical residues from entering your compost and potentially affecting your garden.

Steps:
1. Identify Chemically-Treated Materials: Avoid adding any plants or grass that have been treated with chemicals...
Objective: Prevent the risk of chemical residues from entering your compost and potentially affecting your garden.

Steps:
1. Identify Chemically-Treated Materials: Avoid adding any plants or grass that have been treated with chemicals (e.g., pesticides, herbicides).
2. Dispose of Safely: If you have chemically-treated materials, dispose of them in the trash or follow local regulations for safe disposal.
3. Monitor Compost Pile: Ensure no chemically-treated plant material is accidentally added to your compost pile.

Importance: Some chemicals are persistent and may not break down completely during the composting process, potentially harming your garden when you use the compost.
chemically-treated plants, compost pile, chemical residues
Avoid Adding Diseased Plants to Compost
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Prevent the spread of plant diseases through your compost pile.

Steps:
1. Identify Diseased Plants: Avoid adding any plants that show signs of disease (e.g., spots, wilting, rot).
2. Dispose of Diseased Material Safely:...
Objective: Prevent the spread of plant diseases through your compost pile.

Steps:
1. Identify Diseased Plants: Avoid adding any plants that show signs of disease (e.g., spots, wilting, rot).
2. Dispose of Diseased Material Safely: If you have diseased plants, dispose of them in the trash or burn them if local regulations allow.
3. Monitor Compost Pile: Ensure no diseased plant material is accidentally added to your compost pile.

Importance: Diseased plant material can introduce pathogens into your compost, which may survive and infect your garden when you use the compost.
diseased plants, compost pile, plant pathogens
Avoid Adding Weeds Going to Seed
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Prevent weed seeds from surviving in the compost and sprouting in your garden.

Steps:
1. Identify Weeds Going to Seed: Avoid adding any weeds that are showing signs of going to seed (e.g., visible flower heads or seed...
Objective: Prevent weed seeds from surviving in the compost and sprouting in your garden.

Steps:
1. Identify Weeds Going to Seed: Avoid adding any weeds that are showing signs of going to seed (e.g., visible flower heads or seed pods).
2. Remove Seeds Before Composting: If you have already collected some weed seeds, ensure they are fully dried and separated from the plant material before composting.
3. Monitor Compost Pile: Keep an eye on your compost pile to ensure no weeds going to seed are accidentally added.

Importance: Weeds that go to seed can survive the composting process and sprout in your garden, leading to unwanted growth.
weed seeds, compost pile, avoid weeds
Choosing Quality Compost When Purchasing in Bulk
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
When purchasing compost in bulk, it is essential to ensure you are getting a high-quality product. Here’s how to do that:

1. Understand compost quality factors: Finished compost should be uniform, stable, and free from undesirable...
When purchasing compost in bulk, it is essential to ensure you are getting a high-quality product. Here’s how to do that:

1. Understand compost quality factors: Finished compost should be uniform, stable, and free from undesirable components such as heavy metals, weed seeds, phytotoxic compounds, and foreign objects.
2. Check for certifications: Look for suppliers offering _Certified Compost_ as designated by the U.S. Composting Council*. This certification ensures that the compost has undergone quality testing and meets safety and effectiveness standards.
3. Be aware of chemical residues: Compost can contain chemicals from the original feedstock, so choose products that have been tested for soluble salts, pH levels, and overall stability.
4. Avoid low-quality sources: Be cautious with unverified suppliers who may offer compost that is not fully decomposed or contains harmful substances.
5. Use certified compost for best results: Certified compost provides the most reliable assurance of quality when you cannot produce it yourself.
certified compost, compost quality, U.S. Composting Council, chemical residues, certified compost benefits
Create an Indoor Composting Setup
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
To set up an indoor composting system:

1. Choose a container: Use a large stainless steel bowl with a tight-fitting lid to store compostable ingredients indoors. Stainless steel is ideal because it does not absorb odors and is easy to clean.
2....
To set up an indoor composting system:

1. Choose a container: Use a large stainless steel bowl with a tight-fitting lid to store compostable ingredients indoors. Stainless steel is ideal because it does not absorb odors and is easy to clean.
2. Ensure the lid fits perfectly: A lid that fits snugly over the top of the bowl is essential to prevent odors from escaping and to keep pests out.
3. Consider additional options: You can also purchase composting crocks or countertop devices designed for indoor use. Some models include a charcoal filter to help absorb potential odors.
4. Use compostable bag liners: These make the disposal and cleanup process easier, especially if you need to transfer the compostable materials frequently.

Important considerations:
• If your indoor compost bin starts to smell, it is time to take the contents outside for further processing, even with a charcoal filter.
• Ensure that the lid has no gaps to maintain freshness and prevent fruit flies or other pests from entering.
indoor composting, stainless steel bowl, lid, composting crocks, charcoal filter, compostable bag liners
Creating Winter Compost Storage Bins Using Straw or Hay Bales
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure:

1. Stack old straw or hay bales two high to create a frame.
2. Cover the stacked bales with plywood to form a storage bin.
3. Ensure the structure is stable and can support the weight of additional organic materials.
4. Lift the wood...
Procedure:

1. Stack old straw or hay bales two high to create a frame.
2. Cover the stacked bales with plywood to form a storage bin.
3. Ensure the structure is stable and can support the weight of additional organic materials.
4. Lift the wood cover periodically to add more compostable ingredients during winter.
5. Optionally, leave an opening in one side for easy insertion of material.
6. Cover the front opening if necessary to protect the contents from weather elements.

Details:
• The straw or hay bales provide additional brown/carbon material that can be used later in the composting process.
• This method allows you to continue collecting and storing organic matter even during cold months.
straw hay compost bin, winter storage solution, composting during cold weather
Creating Winter Compost Storage Bins Using Tomato Cages
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure:

1. Identify tomato cages that are temporarily out of service.
2. Use these cages as storage bins for collecting fall leaves or other organic materials during winter.
3. Ensure the cage is sturdy enough to hold the collected material...
Procedure:

1. Identify tomato cages that are temporarily out of service.
2. Use these cages as storage bins for collecting fall leaves or other organic materials during winter.
3. Ensure the cage is sturdy enough to hold the collected material without collapsing.
4. Continue adding organic matter to the cage throughout the winter season.

Details:
• This method allows you to store and manage compostable materials even when your main compost pile is inactive due to cold weather.
• The tomato cage provides a temporary, elevated storage solution that keeps materials dry and accessible.
winter composting, tomato cage storage, organic material storage, compost bin alternatives
Determine Carbon to Nitrogen (C:N) Ratio for Organic Materials
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Understanding the C:N ratio is essential for maintaining a healthy worm composting system.

• A C:N ratio greater than 30:1 indicates Browns, which are high in carbon and low in nitrogen.
• A C:N ratio less than 30:1 indicates...
Understanding the C:N ratio is essential for maintaining a healthy worm composting system.

• A C:N ratio greater than 30:1 indicates Browns, which are high in carbon and low in nitrogen.
• A C:N ratio less than 30:1 indicates Greens, which are high in nitrogen and low in carbon.

The ideal C:N ratio for worm composting is 30:1. To achieve this balance:

• Feed the worms at least 50% Browns (carbon-rich materials) and 50% Greens (nitrogen-rich materials) by volume.
• Most household waste will naturally fall within a balanced range if you follow this 50/50 guideline.

Common C:N ratios of household items:

| Material | C:N Ratio |
|----------|-----------|
| Cardboard | 350:1 |
| Fruit Waste | 20 - 50:1 |
| Paper, Newspaper | 170 - 200:1 |
| Peanut Shells | 35:1 |
| Coffee Grounds | 20:1 |
| Vegetable Scraps | 10 - 25:1 |
| Garden Waste | 30:1 |
| Pasta, Breads, Cereals | 20:1 |
| Cocoanut Coir | 100:1 |
| Peat Moss | 58:1 |
| Tea Bags | 70:1 |
| Leaves (ash, black elder, elm & alder) | 21 - 28:1 |
| Leaves (maple, oak, birch, aspen, beech) | 30 - 80:1 |
| Sawdust | 200-500:1 |

Notes:
• Grass clippings are high in nitrogen but generate excessive heat and should not be used.
• Deciduous leaves are preferred over evergreen needles or sawdust, which can be acidic and harmful to worms.
carbon,nitrogen,ratio,composting,worms,browns,greens
Maintaining Composting During Winter with a Large Pile
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure:

1. Build a compost pile that is at least 4’ x 4’ x 4’ in size.
2. Ensure the pile is well-insulated to minimize heat loss during cold weather.
3. Add organic materials regularly, even during winter months.
4. Monitor the internal...
Procedure:

1. Build a compost pile that is at least 4’ x 4’ x 4’ in size.
2. Ensure the pile is well-insulated to minimize heat loss during cold weather.
3. Add organic materials regularly, even during winter months.
4. Monitor the internal temperature of the pile to ensure it remains above freezing if possible.
5. If the pile freezes, understand that decomposition will temporarily halt until temperatures rise again.

Details:
• A larger compost pile generates more heat internally, which helps maintain some level of decomposition even in cold weather.
• This method allows you to continue making progress toward finished compost by the time spring arrives.
winter compost pile size, insulation for composting, cold weather composting
Manage Pot Worm Populations in a Worm Composter
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Pot worms, also known as terrestrial enchytraeids, are small white worms that can develop into large populations in compost piles or worm bins.

### Characteristics of Pot Worms:
• Size: About a quarter of an inch long
• Color: White...
Pot worms, also known as terrestrial enchytraeids, are small white worms that can develop into large populations in compost piles or worm bins.

### Characteristics of Pot Worms:
• Size: About a quarter of an inch long
• Color: White (unlike baby red wigglers, which are reddish)
• Habitat: Prefer moist, acidic environments rich in organic matter
• Feeding Habits: Feed on bacteria, fungi, and dead organic matter. They do not digest complex carbohydrates but may ingest small mineral particles.

### Managing Pot Worm Populations:
1. Avoid feeding large quantities of acidic or starchy foods to the bin.
2. Use bread soaked in milk to attract pot worms. This can be used to remove them in large batches if needed.
3. Pot worms are harmless to earthworms and do not harm living plants. They may even help by aerating soil and breaking down organic material.

### When to Be Concerned:
• If the bin becomes too dry, pot worms will die.
• Large populations can occur when acidic or starchy materials are added and allowed to ferment.
potworms, enchytraeids, composting, worm bin management
Maximize Compost Use with Limited Supply
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
When you have a limited amount of compost available, follow these steps to make the most effective use of it:

1. Focus on applying compost where plant roots will be.
2. Apply about 1 inch of compost around the area where you plan to add...
When you have a limited amount of compost available, follow these steps to make the most effective use of it:

1. Focus on applying compost where plant roots will be.
2. Apply about 1 inch of compost around the area where you plan to add plants or seeds.
3. Work the compost into the soil to a depth of approximately 4 inches.
4. If you have more compost available, increase both the volume applied and the area covered, extending out from where your plants will grow.
5. Avoid spreading compost too thinly over large areas when supplies are limited; instead, concentrate it around plant root zones for maximum benefit.

By focusing on the immediate planting area, you ensure that your plants have access to nutrient-rich compost where they need it most.
limited compost use, compost application, soil amendment, plant roots, composting techniques
Monitor Compost Temperature for Optimal Decomposition
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Why it matters:
Composting generates heat, and the temperature of your compost pile indicates how active the decomposition process is. Higher temperatures accelerate breakdown and help eliminate pathogens, weed seeds, and pests.

**What you...
Why it matters:
Composting generates heat, and the temperature of your compost pile indicates how active the decomposition process is. Higher temperatures accelerate breakdown and help eliminate pathogens, weed seeds, and pests.

What you need:
• A compost thermometer (optional but highly recommended)

Procedure:
1. Insert a compost thermometer into the center of the pile to measure internal temperature.
2. Ideal composting occurs in the thermophilic range, which is between 113°F and 158°F (45°C – 70°C). This range promotes rapid decomposition and kills harmful elements like pathogens, weed seeds, and fly larvae.
3. If temperatures fall below 50°F (10°C), the process will be very slow. You may need to add more nitrogen-rich materials or ensure proper aeration and moisture levels.
4. Monitor temperature regularly, especially during the first few weeks of composting.
5. Once the pile cools down to ambient temperature and the material is fully decomposed (unrecognizable), your compost is ready for use.

Notes:
• A compost thermometer provides valuable feedback on how well your pile is performing.
• If you don’t have a thermometer, observe signs like heat radiating from the pile or a strong ammonia smell, which may indicate high nitrogen content and active decomposition.
compost temperature, compost thermometer, thermophilic range, mesophilic range, decomposition process
Set Up a Forced-Air Composting System Using PVC Pipes and an Electric Leaf Blower
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Accelerate composting by introducing forced airflow into the pile.

Materials Required:
• Large diameter PVC pipes with holes drilled throughout
• Electric leaf blower
• Plumbing supplies (couplings, connectors)
• Outdoor timer...
Objective: Accelerate composting by introducing forced airflow into the pile.

Materials Required:
• Large diameter PVC pipes with holes drilled throughout
• Electric leaf blower
• Plumbing supplies (couplings, connectors)
• Outdoor timer (optional)

Steps:
1. Drill holes into the PVC pipe at regular intervals to allow air flow through the pipe.
2. Bury the PVC pipes in the compost pile so that they are positioned deep within the mass of organic material.
3. Connect one end of the PVC pipe to an electric leaf blower using plumbing supplies (e.g., couplings, connectors).
4. Turn on the leaf blower to force air through the pipes and into the compost pile.
5. Optionally install an outdoor timer to automate the process, allowing for consistent aeration without manual intervention.

Notes:
• This system is highly effective at speeding up decomposition by ensuring deep oxygenation of the pile.
• The leaf blower should be used periodically or on a schedule depending on the size and activity level of the compost pile.
forced-air composting, leaf blower, PVC pipe, aeration, compost pile
Understanding Compost Tea and Its Controversy
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Compost tea is a nonheated brew of compost or manure (usually composted manure) with the addition of sugar. The result is a bacteria-rich tea that is applied to plant foliage and soil for disease suppression and a quick-feed nutrient...
Compost tea is a nonheated brew of compost or manure (usually composted manure) with the addition of sugar. The result is a bacteria-rich tea that is applied to plant foliage and soil for disease suppression and a quick-feed nutrient boost.

Although compost tea sounds like a slam-dunk way to deliver the best soil amendment in a liquid form, it is not without controversy. A quick online search will produce a number of scientific and academic studies that show compost tea doesn’t work (even when properly mixed and brewed as proponents would argue in defense of such findings). Another issue is the risk that compost tea can promote an environment that fosters increased levels of harmful bacteria.

I know people on both sides of this argument that I greatly respect. Although I don’t make or use compost tea, I have seen the results of its beneficial effect as a foliar feed, primarily in organic lawn applications. I have also talked to many avid gardeners I know and trust who are convinced it works for them.

While there is no shortage of information online on both sides of this issue, you’ll have to go there to learn more for now. A more in-depth discussion on this subject is beyond the scope of what I’m willing and able to include here. (Perhaps a dedicated guide on this topic later might be in order.)
compost tea, controversy, bacteria-rich liquid, foliar feed, organic lawn applications
Understanding Stability in Composting and Soil Management
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Stability: The degree to which compost or other material can resist further break down or the absorption of other compounds.

This term refers to the maturity of compost and its ability to remain chemically stable, reducing the risk of nutrient...
Stability: The degree to which compost or other material can resist further break down or the absorption of other compounds.

This term refers to the maturity of compost and its ability to remain chemically stable, reducing the risk of nutrient leaching or decomposition.
stability,compost maturity,nutrient retention
Using Coir in Worm Composters
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Procedure for Using Coir in Worm Composting:

1. Add coir to new trays: Place a handful or two of coir into each new tray of your worm composter.
2. Monitor composting process: The addition of coir can speed up the composting process and...
Procedure for Using Coir in Worm Composting:

1. Add coir to new trays: Place a handful or two of coir into each new tray of your worm composter.
2. Monitor composting process: The addition of coir can speed up the composting process and improve the quality of finished worm compost.

Benefits of Coir in Worm Composting:
• Improved soil structure: Coir enhances sandy soils by increasing texture, water retention, and nutrient storage capacity.
• Not essential but beneficial: While not required for operating a worm composter, coir is highly recommended if available due to its positive impact on compost quality.

Gardening Results: Many gardeners report excellent results when using coir in their soil mixes and container plant growing. Its properties make it an ideal amendment for both home gardens and commercial farming.
worm composter, coir, composting, soil structure, water retention, nutrient storage
Utilize Liquid Effluent from Vermicomposting
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Overview: The liquid effluent (often called worm tea or liquid gold) produced during vermicomposting is a nutrient-rich organic fertilizer that can be used to boost plant growth.

Steps:
1. Collect the Liquid: Use a spout or siphon to...
Overview: The liquid effluent (often called worm tea or liquid gold) produced during vermicomposting is a nutrient-rich organic fertilizer that can be used to boost plant growth.

Steps:
1. Collect the Liquid: Use a spout or siphon to collect the liquid from the bottom of the worm bin. This occurs naturally as the worms process food scraps and excrete waste.
2. Dilute the Tea: Dilute the collected liquid with water in a 1:10 ratio (1 part worm tea to 10 parts water) to avoid burning plants.
3. Apply to Plants: Use the diluted solution as a foliar spray or pour it directly into the soil around your plants. Apply every few weeks for best results.
4. Store Unused Tea: If not using immediately, store the undiluted worm tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 2-3 days.

Notes: The liquid effluent is rich in nutrients and beneficial microbes that enhance soil health and plant growth. Always ensure it is properly diluted before application.
worm tea, liquid effluent, vermicompost fertilizer, organic liquid fertilizer
Alternative Methods to Prevent Compost Loss from Pallet Bins
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Using Hardware Cloth:
• Line the interior of each bin with one-inch hardware cloth or similar material.
• This reduces the amount of compost falling through gaps in the pallets.

Extra Pallet for Parts and Slats:
• Use an **extra...
Using Hardware Cloth:
• Line the interior of each bin with one-inch hardware cloth or similar material.
• This reduces the amount of compost falling through gaps in the pallets.

Extra Pallet for Parts and Slats:
• Use an extra pallet to create additional slats or parts for your composting system.
• Remove the slats from the extra pallet and use them to close any gaps in the main bins.
hardware cloth for compost bins, compost loss prevention, extra pallet usage
Assess Risks of Using Plastic Bins and Tires for Composting
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure:

1. Research the type of plastic used in compost bins to determine if it is stable and less likely to leach harmful chemicals.
2. Contact the manufacturer of the specific product to obtain detailed information about potential...
Procedure:

1. Research the type of plastic used in compost bins to determine if it is stable and less likely to leach harmful chemicals.
2. Contact the manufacturer of the specific product to obtain detailed information about potential risks or safety guarantees.
3. For old tires, be aware that they may leach zinc and carcinogenic compounds over time as they degrade.
4. Avoid using old tires in composting if possible due to the risk of chemical leaching into the soil.
plastic bins, tire composting, chemical leaching, safety, manufacturer contact
Batch Composting Process
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Batch Composting Process

The most efficient way to compost is in batches. Each new pile or tray should be filled to capacity and not receive any additional material until the process is complete.

• **Adding new materials continuously results...
Batch Composting Process

The most efficient way to compost is in batches. Each new pile or tray should be filled to capacity and not receive any additional material until the process is complete.

• Adding new materials continuously results in a never-ending process, which can reduce efficiency and quality of the final compost product.

Worm composting is special! Worms produce a fantastic “biologically-enhanced humus-rich” product with several advantages over traditional garden compost:

• Full of beneficial soil microorganisms
• Very high humus content
• Contains slow-release natural fertilizers
• Higher in nutrients than traditional compost
• Concentrated water-soluble nutrients that plants can use immediately
• Helps bind together soil particles
• Enhances disease resistance
• Encourages healthy/strong root systems
batch composting,worm composting,batch process,compost efficiency
Build a Simple Compost Sifter
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Supplies Needed:
• 2x4s cut to size
• 3-inch galvanized screws
• Drill
• Hardware cloth
• 3/4-inch galvanized staples (or similar)
• Wire cutter

Steps:
1. Make the Frame:
- Cut 2x4 lumber into pieces to make a frame large enough to...
Supplies Needed:
• 2x4s cut to size
• 3-inch galvanized screws
• Drill
• Hardware cloth
• 3/4-inch galvanized staples (or similar)
• Wire cutter

Steps:
1. Make the Frame:
- Cut 2x4 lumber into pieces to make a frame large enough to rest solidly on top of a wheelbarrow, cart, or collection tub.
- A good uniform frame size is 24" x 48". This size works well for handling and storage.
- Ensure the width of the frame matches (or is narrower than) the width of the hardware cloth you will use later.
- Secure the frame together using 3-inch decking or weather-resistant screws, placing two screws per joint.

2. Select the Right Hardware Cloth:
- Purchase hardware cloth from a box or hardware store. It comes in rolls with different widths (common sizes: 24", 30", and 36") and opening spacings (common options: 1/2-inch and 1-inch).
- Choose a roll that is as wide or wider than your frame; you can cut it down to size later.
- Select the wire with 1-inch spacing, as this is sufficient for compost sifting purposes.

3. Secure the Hardware Cloth to the Frame:
- Place the frame on a solid surface and align the hardware cloth so its edges match the frame's edges.
- Cut the length of the cloth to fit the frame, or slightly beyond it for easier handling.
- Use 3/4-inch galvanized u-shaped, nailable staples to secure the wire to the frame every few inches around the entire perimeter. Ensure the screen is tightly-fitting and well-secured.
- Finally, cut any exposed edges of the hardware cloth so there is no wire beyond the frame edge.

Result: You now have a compost sifter that will last for years and can be used frequently.
compost sifter, hardware cloth, composting tools, DIY compost bin, compost frame
Build a Three-Bay Pallet Composting Bin
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Supplies Needed:
• Used shipping pallets (preferably hardwood and uniform in size)
• All-weather screws
• Drill
• Optional: Hinges for roof and access panels

Steps:
1. Select Pallets:
- Choose used shipping pallets, ideally made...
Supplies Needed:
• Used shipping pallets (preferably hardwood and uniform in size)
• All-weather screws
• Drill
• Optional: Hinges for roof and access panels

Steps:
1. Select Pallets:
- Choose used shipping pallets, ideally made of hardwood and all the same size for consistency.

2. Assemble the Frame:
- Use a drill and all-weather screws to assemble the pallets into a three-bin system.
- The design is simple, low-cost, and allows for easy repurposing of pallets that are commonly found in abundance.
- With help from someone to hold the pallets steady while you screw them together, the bin can be assembled in minutes.

3. Enhance Functionality (Optional):
- Add a hinged roof to the section of the bin that will store finished compost for protection from rain and pests.
- Attach additional hinges to the outer pallet wall of the finished compost section for easier access when harvesting compost.

Result: A durable, functional three-bin composting system made entirely from repurposed materials. This setup has been used successfully for over 10 years.
pallet compost bin, DIY composting system, three-bin compost, repurposed materials
Composting Bread and Cereal
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Bread and Cereal:

1. Collect stale or unused bread and cereal (both hot or cold) from your kitchen.
2. Chop or break the bread and cereal into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the chopped material to...
Procedure for Composting Bread and Cereal:

1. Collect stale or unused bread and cereal (both hot or cold) from your kitchen.
2. Chop or break the bread and cereal into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the chopped material to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) and carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) for optimal decomposition.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Bread and cereal are high in carbon (brown material) and can be composted effectively if managed properly.
• Avoid adding large, whole pieces of bread directly into the pile without chopping them first.
bread,cereal,compost,brown
Composting Cardboard and Paper Products
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Cardboard and Paper Products:

1. Collect cardboard (such as boxes, egg cartons, and paper towels) from your home or office.
2. Shred the cardboard into small pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Avoid using glossy or...
Procedure for Composting Cardboard and Paper Products:

1. Collect cardboard (such as boxes, egg cartons, and paper towels) from your home or office.
2. Shred the cardboard into small pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Avoid using glossy or coated paper products, as they may not break down effectively in compost.
4. Add the shredded cardboard to your compost pile or bin.
5. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) for optimal decomposition.
6. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Cardboard is high in carbon (brown material) and can be composted effectively if managed properly.
• Shredding cardboard helps it break down more quickly in the compost pile.
cardboard,paper,products,compost,brown

Procedure for Composting Citrus and Fruit Waste:

1. Collect citrus peels, fruit rinds, seeds, and peelings from citrus fruits.
2. Chop or cut the waste into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the chopped waste to your compost pile...
Procedure for Composting Citrus and Fruit Waste:

1. Collect citrus peels, fruit rinds, seeds, and peelings from citrus fruits.
2. Chop or cut the waste into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the chopped waste to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) and carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) for optimal decomposition.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Citrus and fruit waste are high in carbon (brown material) and can be composted effectively if managed properly.
• Avoid adding large, whole citrus fruits directly into the pile without chopping them first.
citrus,fruit,waste,compost,brown
Composting Coffee Grounds and Filters
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Coffee Grounds and Filters:

1. Collect used coffee grounds and filters from your kitchen or office.
2. Ensure the filters are made of natural materials (such as paper) and not synthetic fibers.
3. Add the coffee grounds...
Procedure for Composting Coffee Grounds and Filters:

1. Collect used coffee grounds and filters from your kitchen or office.
2. Ensure the filters are made of natural materials (such as paper) and not synthetic fibers.
3. Add the coffee grounds and filters to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) for optimal decomposition.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Coffee grounds are high in nitrogen (green material) and can be a valuable addition to your compost.
• Paper filters should be composted separately from synthetic ones, as they may not break down effectively.
coffee,grounds,filters,compost,green
Composting Corncobs
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Corncobs:

1. Collect corncobs from your kitchen or garden.
2. Chop or cut the corncobs into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the chopped corncobs to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with...
Procedure for Composting Corncobs:

1. Collect corncobs from your kitchen or garden.
2. Chop or cut the corncobs into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the chopped corncobs to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) and carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) for optimal decomposition.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Corncobs are high in carbon (brown material) but break down slowly due to their fibrous structure.
• Chopping them into smaller pieces can significantly speed up the composting process.
corncobs,compost,brown
Composting Eggshells
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Eggshells:

1. Collect used eggshells from your kitchen.
2. Rinse the shells to remove any residual egg white or yolk.
3. Crush the shells into small pieces using a mortar and pestle, blender, or by hand.
4. Add the...
Procedure for Composting Eggshells:

1. Collect used eggshells from your kitchen.
2. Rinse the shells to remove any residual egg white or yolk.
3. Crush the shells into small pieces using a mortar and pestle, blender, or by hand.
4. Add the crushed shells to your compost pile or bin.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile for efficient decomposition.

Notes:
• Eggshells are high in calcium and can be beneficial for plant growth when composted properly.
• Crushing them first helps speed up their breakdown in the compost pile.
eggshells,compost,brown
Composting Grass Clippings
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Grass Clippings:

1. Collect fresh grass clippings.
2. If the clippings are saturated, spread them out to dry slightly before adding to the compost pile.
3. Add the clippings to your compost bin or pile.
4. Monitor the...
Procedure for Composting Grass Clippings:

1. Collect fresh grass clippings.
2. If the clippings are saturated, spread them out to dry slightly before adding to the compost pile.
3. Add the clippings to your compost bin or pile.
4. Monitor the pile for any unpleasant odors, which may occur temporarily due to the saturation of the clippings.
5. Ensure proper aeration and moisture balance in the compost pile to prevent odor issues.

Notes:
• Fresh grass clippings are high in nitrogen (green material) and can contribute to a balanced compost if managed properly.
• Avoid adding overly wet clippings directly into the pile without drying them first.
grass,clippings,compost,brown,green
Composting Hay or Straw
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Hay or Straw:

1. Collect hay or straw for composting.
2. Check the source of the hay or straw to ensure it does not contain persistent herbicides, which may survive the composting process.
3. Add the hay or straw to...
Procedure for Composting Hay or Straw:

1. Collect hay or straw for composting.
2. Check the source of the hay or straw to ensure it does not contain persistent herbicides, which may survive the composting process.
3. Add the hay or straw to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) to maintain a healthy carbon-to-nitrogen ratio.
5. Monitor the compost pile for any signs of herbicide contamination and use caution when applying it to sensitive plants.

Notes:
• Hay and straw are high in carbon (brown material) but may contain herbicides that can affect plant growth.
• Use with caution, especially if using horse manure or other materials that may carry herbicides.
hay,straw,compost,brown
Composting Leaves
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Leaves:

1. Collect fallen leaves from your yard.
2. Shred or chop the leaves using a lawnmower, shredder, or scissors to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the shredded leaves to your compost pile or bin.
4. Mix the leaves...
Procedure for Composting Leaves:

1. Collect fallen leaves from your yard.
2. Shred or chop the leaves using a lawnmower, shredder, or scissors to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the shredded leaves to your compost pile or bin.
4. Mix the leaves with green materials (such as vegetable scraps) to maintain a balanced carbon-to-nitrogen ratio.
5. Ensure proper moisture levels and aeration in the compost pile for efficient breakdown.

Notes:
• Oak leaves are acidic and break down slowly; they may require more time or additional nitrogen-rich materials to decompose effectively.
• Black walnut leaves contain natural toxins that can be harmful to some plants, but these toxins are neutralized during the composting process.
leaves,compost,brown,green
Composting Manure
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Manure:

1. Collect animal manure (such as horse, cow, or chicken manure) from a reliable source.
2. Check the source of the manure to ensure it does not contain persistent herbicides that may survive the composting...
Procedure for Composting Manure:

1. Collect animal manure (such as horse, cow, or chicken manure) from a reliable source.
2. Check the source of the manure to ensure it does not contain persistent herbicides that may survive the composting process.
3. Add the manure to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) to maintain a healthy carbon-to-nitrogen ratio.
5. Monitor the compost pile for temperature and moisture levels, ensuring proper aeration.

Notes:
• Manure is high in nitrogen (green material) and can be a valuable addition to your compost.
• Horse manure may contain herbicides; use caution when sourcing it.
manure,compost,green
Composting Pet Bedding

1. Collect bedding material from small herbivorous pets such as hamsters, rabbits, or guinea pigs.
2. Ensure the bedding is free of inorganic materials (such as plastic or synthetic fibers) before...
Procedure for Composting Pet Bedding:

1. Collect bedding material from small herbivorous pets such as hamsters, rabbits, or guinea pigs.
2. Ensure the bedding is free of inorganic materials (such as plastic or synthetic fibers) before composting.
3. Add the bedding to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) and carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) for optimal decomposition.
5. Monitor moisture levels and aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Pet bedding from herbivorous animals is high in nitrogen (green material).
• Avoid using bedding from carnivorous pets, as it may contain harmful substances.
pet,bedding,compost,green
Composting Pet Food (Dry Only)

Procedure for Composting Dry Pet Food:

1. Collect unused dry pet food from your home or office.
2. Ensure the pet food is free of inorganic materials and synthetic additives that may not break down effectively in compost.
3. Add the dry pet...
Procedure for Composting Dry Pet Food:

1. Collect unused dry pet food from your home or office.
2. Ensure the pet food is free of inorganic materials and synthetic additives that may not break down effectively in compost.
3. Add the dry pet food to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) and carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) for optimal decomposition.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Dry pet food is high in carbon (brown material) and can be composted effectively if managed properly.
• Avoid adding wet or spoiled pet food to your compost, as it may attract pests or cause odor issues.
pet,food,dry,compost,brown
Composting Pine Needles
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Pine Needles:

1. Collect pine needles from your yard or garden.
2. Add the pine needles to your compost pile or bin.
3. Monitor the pH of the compost, as pine needles are acidic and may lower the overall pH of the...
Procedure for Composting Pine Needles:

1. Collect pine needles from your yard or garden.
2. Add the pine needles to your compost pile or bin.
3. Monitor the pH of the compost, as pine needles are acidic and may lower the overall pH of the pile.
4. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) to maintain a healthy carbon-to-nitrogen ratio.
5. Ensure proper moisture levels and aeration in the pile for efficient decomposition.

Notes:
• Pine needles break down slowly due to their waxy coating.
• They are high in carbon (brown material) but may require additional nitrogen-rich materials to speed up the composting process.
pine,needles,compost,brown
Composting Popcorn and Pasta
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Popcorn and Pasta:

1. Collect unused popcorn (both cooked or uncooked) and pasta from your kitchen.
2. Chop or break the popcorn and pasta into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the chopped material to...
Procedure for Composting Popcorn and Pasta:

1. Collect unused popcorn (both cooked or uncooked) and pasta from your kitchen.
2. Chop or break the popcorn and pasta into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the chopped material to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) and carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) for optimal decomposition.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Popcorn and pasta are high in carbon (brown material) and can be composted effectively if managed properly.
• Avoid adding large, whole pieces of popcorn or pasta directly into the pile without chopping them first.
popcorn,pasta,compost,brown
Composting Stages and Disruption
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Composting Stages and Disruption

• Compost passes through distinct cycles or stages employing many different microorganisms.
• This process is disrupted when new food is added to a tray before the process is complete.

Disruptions can lead...
Composting Stages and Disruption

• Compost passes through distinct cycles or stages employing many different microorganisms.
• This process is disrupted when new food is added to a tray before the process is complete.

Disruptions can lead to:

• Uneven decomposition of materials
• Poor quality compost
• Imbalanced microbial activity

To ensure optimal results, wait until one batch is fully processed before adding new material.
compost stages,microbial activity,disruption,new food addition
Composting Tea Leaves and Bags
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Tea Leaves and Bags:

1. Collect used tea leaves and tea bags from your kitchen or office.
2. Ensure the tea bags are made of natural materials (such as paper) and not synthetic fibers.
3. Add the tea leaves and bags to...
Procedure for Composting Tea Leaves and Bags:

1. Collect used tea leaves and tea bags from your kitchen or office.
2. Ensure the tea bags are made of natural materials (such as paper) and not synthetic fibers.
3. Add the tea leaves and bags to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) for optimal decomposition.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Tea leaves are high in nitrogen (green material) and can be a valuable addition to your compost.
• Paper tea bags should be composted separately from synthetic ones, as they may not break down effectively.
tea,leaves,bags,compost,green
Composting Vegetable Scraps
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure for Composting Vegetable Scraps:

1. Collect vegetable scraps such as peels, cores, and trimmings.
2. Chop or cut the scraps into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the scraps to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with...
Procedure for Composting Vegetable Scraps:

1. Collect vegetable scraps such as peels, cores, and trimmings.
2. Chop or cut the scraps into smaller pieces to speed up decomposition.
3. Add the scraps to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance with carbon-rich materials (such as leaves or straw) to maintain a healthy carbon-to-nitrogen ratio.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Vegetable scraps are high in nitrogen (green material) and can be a valuable addition to your compost.
• Avoid adding large, whole vegetables directly into the pile without chopping them first.

1. Collect yard debris such as branches, twigs, and small plant trimmings.
2. Cut or chop the debris into smaller pieces to accelerate decomposition.
3. Add the cut debris to your compost pile or bin.
4....
Procedure for Composting Yard Debris:

1. Collect yard debris such as branches, twigs, and small plant trimmings.
2. Cut or chop the debris into smaller pieces to accelerate decomposition.
3. Add the cut debris to your compost pile or bin.
4. Balance the carbon-rich material with nitrogen-rich materials (such as food scraps) for optimal composting.
5. Monitor moisture levels and ensure proper aeration in the pile.

Notes:
• Yard debris is typically brown material, high in carbon.
• Smaller pieces decompose faster than larger ones.
yard,debris,compost,brown
Create Passive Airflow in Compost Pile Using PVC Pipes
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Improve aeration in compost pile without manual turning.

Materials Required:
• Large diameter PVC pipes
• Drill with hole saw or drill bit

Steps:
1. Drill holes into the PVC pipe at regular intervals (e.g., every 6...
Objective: Improve aeration in compost pile without manual turning.

Materials Required:
• Large diameter PVC pipes
• Drill with hole saw or drill bit

Steps:
1. Drill holes into the PVC pipe at regular intervals (e.g., every 6 inches) to allow air flow through the pipe.
2. Insert the PVC pipe horizontally and/or vertically into the compost pile, ensuring that it reaches deep into the center of the pile.
3. Repeat as needed with additional pipes depending on the size of your compost pile.

Notes:
• The holes should be large enough to allow air to pass through but not so large that they compromise the structural integrity of the pipe.
• This method helps speed up decomposition by ensuring oxygen reaches the core of the pile, even without manual turning.
passive composting, aeration, PVC pipe, compost pile, oxygen flow
Create Passive Airflow in Compost Pile Using Twigs or Branches
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Improve aeration in compost pile without manual turning.

Materials Required:
• Twigs, branches, or other woody materials of varying sizes

Steps:
1. Layer the compost pile with twigs and branches of different sizes to...
Objective: Improve aeration in compost pile without manual turning.

Materials Required:
• Twigs, branches, or other woody materials of varying sizes

Steps:
1. Layer the compost pile with twigs and branches of different sizes to create open pockets within the pile.
2. Ensure that these materials are evenly distributed throughout the pile to allow for maximum airflow.
3. Allow time for the twigs to break down, though they will not decompose as quickly as other organic matter in the pile.

Notes:
• This method is a simple and low-effort way to improve aeration, but it may result in some twigs remaining in the finished compost, which can be a nuisance when harvesting.
• The open pockets created by the twigs help oxygen reach deeper into the pile, promoting faster decomposition.
passive composting, aeration, twigs, branches, compost pile
Create a Compost Heap for Soil Improvement
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Objective: Create a compost heap that improves soil structure, fertility, and plant growth.

Steps:
1. Gather organic materials: Collect a mix of carbon-based (brown) materials like dried leaves, straw, and paper, and...
Objective: Create a compost heap that improves soil structure, fertility, and plant growth.

Steps:
1. Gather organic materials: Collect a mix of carbon-based (brown) materials like dried leaves, straw, and paper, and nitrogen-based (green) materials like grass clippings, vegetable scraps, and coffee grounds.
2. Layer the materials: Alternate layers of browns and greens to ensure a balanced decomposition process. Aim for a rough carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of 25:1 if possible, but this is not strictly necessary for successful composting.
3. Add moisture: Keep the pile moist, like a wrung-out sponge, to help microorganisms break down the organic matter efficiently.
4. Aerate the pile: Turn the heap every few weeks with a pitchfork or compost aerator to introduce oxygen and speed up decomposition.
5. Monitor temperature: A well-maintained compost pile can reach temperatures of 149°F (65°C), which helps break down materials faster and kill pathogens.
6. Wait for maturation: Allow the pile to decompose over several months until it becomes dark, crumbly, and has an earthy smell.

Benefits:
• Enhances soil tilth and structure.
• Increases moisture infiltration and reduces erosion.
• Supplies organic matter and nutrients to plants.
• Reduces greenhouse gas emissions by diverting waste from landfills.
• Encourages healthy root growth and nutrient uptake in plants.
compost heap, soil improvement, carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, composting process, organic matter
Create a Compost Pile Using the Four-Ingredient Method
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Why it matters:
Composting is a simple and effective way to recycle organic waste into nutrient-rich soil amendment. The four-ingredient method ensures you have all the necessary components for successful composting.

What you need:
-...
Why it matters:
Composting is a simple and effective way to recycle organic waste into nutrient-rich soil amendment. The four-ingredient method ensures you have all the necessary components for successful composting.

What you need:
• Air: Ensure proper aeration by turning the pile regularly.
• Water: Keep the pile moist, like a damp sponge.
• Carbon (Browns): Materials such as dried leaves, straw, cardboard, and wood chips.
• Nitrogen (Greens): Materials such as grass clippings, vegetable scraps, coffee grounds, and plant trimmings.

Procedure:
1. Start by combining generous portions of carbon-rich (browns) and nitrogen-rich (greens) materials in a pile.
2. Continue adding ingredients until the pile reaches approximately 4’ x 4’ x 4’ in size.
3. Mix the pile thoroughly every week to ensure even decomposition and aeration.
4. Add water as needed to keep the pile moist, but not soggy (like a damp sponge).
5. Allow the pile to decompose outdoors for several months.
6. Check regularly by turning the pile and monitoring its temperature with a compost thermometer if available.
7. The compost is ready when:
- The ingredients are unrecognizable.
- The internal temperature has cooled down to ambient levels.
- The material smells rich and earthy, not sour or foul.
8. Once ready, add the compost to your garden soil at approximately 30% by volume and mix it into the top four inches of any garden bed.

Notes:
• You can substitute ingredients as needed based on availability.
• No precise measurements are required; just ensure a good balance between browns and greens.
• A well-maintained compost pile will provide you with rich, organic soil amendment for your plants.
four-ingredient composting method, compost pile creation, carbon and nitrogen balance, compost ingredients
Determine Amount of Compost Needed for Soil Amendment
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure:

1. According to the U.S. Composting Council, aim for 5% organic matter by dry weight in your plantable area.
2. Apply one inch of compost worked into the top four inches of soil for optimal results and to reach the target...
Procedure:

1. According to the U.S. Composting Council, aim for 5% organic matter by dry weight in your plantable area.
2. Apply one inch of compost worked into the top four inches of soil for optimal results and to reach the target organic matter level.
3. Use a free online calculator or app to determine how much compost is needed for any given area:
- Input the depth in inches you want to cover with compost.
- Enter the dimensions (length, width) of the area you wish to amend.
4. The calculator will provide an estimate of the amount of compost required.
compost calculation, soil amendment, organic matter, U.S. Composting Council, online calculator
Deterring Fire Ants in Compost Pile
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Procedure:

1. Turn the compost pile more frequently to disrupt ant colonies.
2. Increase moisture levels in the pile by watering it regularly.
3. Avoid over-drying the pile, as fire ants prefer drier environments.
4. Be cautious not to...
Procedure:

1. Turn the compost pile more frequently to disrupt ant colonies.
2. Increase moisture levels in the pile by watering it regularly.
3. Avoid over-drying the pile, as fire ants prefer drier environments.
4. Be cautious not to discourage all ants—black ants are beneficial and help break down material and improve airflow within the compost pile.
fire ants, ant control, composting, moisture, turning compost
Enhance Compost Nutrient Levels with Organic Supplements
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
To enhance nutrient levels in your compost:

• Use a diverse array of organic materials to create a balanced, nutrient-rich amendment.
• If additional nutrients are needed, add them in moderation using organic inputs only.

### Common Organic...
To enhance nutrient levels in your compost:

• Use a diverse array of organic materials to create a balanced, nutrient-rich amendment.
• If additional nutrients are needed, add them in moderation using organic inputs only.

### Common Organic Primary Nutrients to Add:

1. Nitrogen:
• Dried blood
• Blood meal
• Cottonseed meal
• Fish emulsion
• Seaweed extract

2. Phosphorus:
• Bone meal
• Rock phosphate

3. Potassium:
• Greensand
• Sulfate of potash

### Notes:
• Avoid synthetic, salt-based fertilizers as they can harm soil quality and do not support long-term soil health.
• Organic liquid fish emulsion can be applied to newly planted seedlings or used for a mid-season boost in vegetable gardens.
• A light application of slow-release organic nitrogen fertilizer (e.g., Milorganite) can help boost nutrient capacity in the soil and assist new seedlings.
compost,nutrients,organic,fertilizer,blood meal,cottonseed meal,fish emulsion,greensand,milorganite
Enhancing the Pallet Compost Bin with Additional Features
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting

• Use extra slats from an additional pallet to fill gaps between the pallets.
• This helps prevent compost from falling out of the bin.

Optional Cover for Finished Compost:
• Create a cover using a...
Adding Slats to Fill Gaps:
• Use extra slats from an additional pallet to fill gaps between the pallets.
• This helps prevent compost from falling out of the bin.

Optional Cover for Finished Compost:
• Create a cover using a cut-to-fit sheet of corrugated plastic and attach it to a wooden frame made of 2x2 pine.
• Secure the cover with hinges attached to the back pallet.

Reinforcement Option:
• Add a long, treated 2x4 across the top edge of the back side of the pallet wall for extra stability.

Fix Critters Active in Compost Due to Possible Inclusion of Animal-Based Products

Problem: Critters active in compost
Cause: Possible inclusion of animal-based products (meat, grease, dairy)
Other Symptoms: Small animals, rodents frequently observed. (Occasional visits by squirrels, chipmunks, turtles, snakes, etc. are normal and should be tolerated in an open composting system.)
Solution:
1. Eliminate use of animal-based products.
2. Turn pile more frequently.
3. Maintain even moisture level.
4. The elimination of meat, grease, and dairy, combined with periodic turning and moisture addition will minimize unwelcome visitors to compost.
