Creating Collars to Protect Plants from Cutworms
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to create collars for cutworm protection:

1. Materials needed: Thin cardboard or a Styrofoam cup with the bottom removed.
2. Cutting the collar: Ensure the collar is at least 1 inch deep and extends 1.5 to 3 inches outward from...
Steps to create collars for cutworm protection:

1. Materials needed: Thin cardboard or a Styrofoam cup with the bottom removed.
2. Cutting the collar: Ensure the collar is at least 1 inch deep and extends 1.5 to 3 inches outward from the base of the plant on all sides.
3. Installation: Place the collar around each transplanted plant, ensuring it is buried into the soil at least an inch deep and stands away from the plant by 1.5 to 3 inches on all sides.

Purpose: Prevent cutworms from accessing the base of plants and cutting them off at ground level.
cutworm prevention, plant collars, gardening tips
Destroy Insects in Pines Using Soap and Sulphur Decoction
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- Soft soap: 1 pound
- Flowers of sulphur: 1 pound
- Tobacco: Half a pound
- Nux vomica: 1 ounce
- Soft water: 4 gallons

2. Preparation:
- Boil all the ingredients together until the liquid...
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- Soft soap: 1 pound
- Flowers of sulphur: 1 pound
- Tobacco: Half a pound
- Nux vomica: 1 ounce
- Soft water: 4 gallons

2. Preparation:
- Boil all the ingredients together until the liquid is reduced to three gallons.
- Allow the mixture to cool completely.

3. Application:
- Trim the roots and leaves of the plant for potting.
- Immerse the entire plant in the cooled decoction.
- For plants not in optimal condition, dilute the decoction by adding a third part water before applying it as a spray over the plant.

4. Post-Application Care:
- The medicated water will settle in the angles of the leaves where insects tend to reside.
- After the intended effects have been achieved (typically after some time), rinse off any remaining liquid from the leaves with clean water to avoid making the plants look dirty.

Notes:
• This method is effective for all species of coccus and most other insects due to its strength and glutinous nature.
• Avoid using this decoction on fruiting plants as it may be harmful.
insect control,pine,soap,sulphur,decoction
Destroy Insects in Pines Using Sulphur and Water Treatment
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Preparation of Solution:
- Prepare a solution by mixing one pound of flowers of sulphur with enough water to fill a tub (approximately one garden potful per tub).

2. Application:
- Immerse the plants in the tub of...
Procedure:

1. Preparation of Solution:
- Prepare a solution by mixing one pound of flowers of sulphur with enough water to fill a tub (approximately one garden potful per tub).

2. Application:
- Immerse the plants in the tub of water containing the sulphur solution for 24 hours.

3. Drying and Repotting:
- After immersion, lay the plants with their tops downward to dry.
- Once fully dried, repot them in the usual manner.
insect control,pine,sulphur,washing
Destroy Insects in Pines Using Tobacco Infusion
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Preparation:
- Remove the plants from their pots and clean the roots thoroughly.
- Immerse the plants in water that has been infused with tobacco stalks for 24 hours.

2. Insect Removal:
- After immersion, use a...
Procedure:

1. Preparation:
- Remove the plants from their pots and clean the roots thoroughly.
- Immerse the plants in water that has been infused with tobacco stalks for 24 hours.

2. Insect Removal:
- After immersion, use a sponge to rub off any visible insects from the plant.

3. Rinsing and Repotting:
- Rinse the plants in clean water.
- Dip them briefly in the tobacco-infused water again if needed.
- Repot the plants in their usual manner after ensuring they are free of pests.
insect control,pine,tobacco,infusion
Destroy Insects in Pines Using Water and Squirt Method
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Application:
- Use a squirt to water the plants with clean water during the summer months, specifically in the evening.

2. Frequency and Care:
- This method should be done regularly but only during the evening...
Procedure:

1. Application:
- Use a squirt to water the plants with clean water during the summer months, specifically in the evening.

2. Frequency and Care:
- This method should be done regularly but only during the evening hours.
- Ensure that the plants are not exposed to severe frost or excessive moisture after watering.

Notes:
• This technique is safe for the plants and has been successfully used by experienced gardeners like Hay in Scotland without causing harm to the plants or attracting pests.
insect control,pine,watering,squirt
Guard Cabbages from Caterpillars Using Hemp
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To guard Cabbages from the Depredations of Caterpillars:

1. Bow with hemp all the borders of the ground where cabbages are planted.
2. This method will keep the area free from caterpillars, even if the surrounding area is infested.
caterpillars,cabbages,hemp,gardening,pest control
Guard Seeds from Mice
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To guard the seeds from mice:

1. Lay a pane of glass over the pot or pan until the seeds have come up.
2. Afterwards, at night, cover with a pot of equal size until the seed-leaves have expanded and the husks have dropped.
3. The cover should...
To guard the seeds from mice:

1. Lay a pane of glass over the pot or pan until the seeds have come up.
2. Afterwards, at night, cover with a pot of equal size until the seed-leaves have expanded and the husks have dropped.
3. The cover should always be removed by sunrise and replaced in the evening.
4. No air need be admitted until the heat begins to rise and steam begins to appear.
5. After that, tilt the light a little every day, in whatever state the weather may be, until the plants break ground.
6. Admit air with more care after this point.
7. If frosty or very chill, hang the end of a mat over the opening so the air may sift through it and not immediately strike the plants.
mice, seed protection, pest control, gardening
Identifying and Removing Garden Pests Early
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to Identify and Remove Garden Pests:

1. Early Identification of Pests:
- It is essential to identify pests in the early stages of their attack.
- For example, it's not a big deal to pick a couple of dozen aphids off your...
Steps to Identify and Remove Garden Pests:

1. Early Identification of Pests:
- It is essential to identify pests in the early stages of their attack.
- For example, it's not a big deal to pick a couple of dozen aphids off your broccoli; but when the attack is well under way and your plant is covered with aphids, hand-picking will be ineffective.

2. Physical Removal of Pests:
- If you're going to remove pests by hand, do so early in the infestation.

3. Using Water for Pest Control:
- If a pest problem gets past the early stages, try a good blast of water from the garden hose to knock the insects off the plant.
- Do this on a dry day to avoid wetting leaves for too long; wet leaves make plants more susceptible to disease.
pest identification,physical removal,gardening,vegetables,water control
Managing Pest Problems in Vegetable Gardens
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Monitor your garden regularly for signs of pests: Look for symptoms such as plants keeling over (indicative of cutworms) or tiny holes in leaves (flea beetles).
2. Identify the pest type: Insects and animals are common...
Procedure:

1. Monitor your garden regularly for signs of pests: Look for symptoms such as plants keeling over (indicative of cutworms) or tiny holes in leaves (flea beetles).
2. Identify the pest type: Insects and animals are common culprits, with insects being more prevalent.
3. Take action promptly: Address pest issues early to prevent widespread damage.
4. Use preventive measures:
- Plant disease-resistant varieties of crops.
- Rotate crops annually where possible to disrupt pest cycles.
5. Remove infected plants immediately (culling): This prevents the spread of plant diseases to healthy plants.

Notes:
• Pest problems are generally easier to control than plant diseases.
• Diseases can be identified by yellowing leaves, stunted growth, or mildew on stems and leaves.
pest management,garden pests,disease prevention,culling,gardening
Managing Pests on Mustard Plants
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Monitor mustard plants regularly for signs of pests such as flea beetles and aphids.
2. Hand-pick or hose off pests from the plant to reduce infestation.
3. Pinch out aphid-infested foliage to prevent spread.
4. **Use...
Procedure:

1. Monitor mustard plants regularly for signs of pests such as flea beetles and aphids.
2. Hand-pick or hose off pests from the plant to reduce infestation.
3. Pinch out aphid-infested foliage to prevent spread.
4. Use chemical sprays like Malathion or Diazinon if pest problems persist, following label instructions carefully.
5. Note that mustard is not ideal for organic gardens due to its susceptibility to pests.
6. For more detailed information on pest control, refer to the section "Keeping Your Garden Healthy" in Part 1 of the source text.
mustard pest control, flea beetles, aphids, chemical sprays, organic gardening
Managing Pumpkin Pests (Squash Vine Borers)
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Managing Squash Vine Borer Infestations:

1. Prevention: Apply carbaryl to the crown of the plant at weekly intervals if borers are suspected.
2. Identify infestation: If the vine wilts from a definite point onward, look...
Procedure for Managing Squash Vine Borer Infestations:

1. Prevention: Apply carbaryl to the crown of the plant at weekly intervals if borers are suspected.
2. Identify infestation: If the vine wilts from a definite point onward, look for a very thin wall or hole near the point where the wilting starts.
3. Remove borer:
- Slit the stem at the affected area.
- Remove the borer and dispose of it.
4. Encourage new growth: Cover the damaged stem with soil to encourage rooting at that point.
5. Monitor regularly: Check plants weekly for signs of infestation, especially during the growing season.
pumpkin pests, squash vine borer control, gardening techniques
Managing Tomato Pests and Insects
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedures for managing pests on tomato plants:

1. Common pests:
- Major pests include aphids, tomato hornworms, cutworms, tomato fruitworms, and whiteflies.
2. Control methods:
- Use collars around the base of plants at...
Procedures for managing pests on tomato plants:

1. Common pests:
- Major pests include aphids, tomato hornworms, cutworms, tomato fruitworms, and whiteflies.
2. Control methods:
- Use collars around the base of plants at transplanting to discourage cutworms.
- Hand-pick hornworms off the plants.
- Hose aphids and whiteflies off the plants or pinch out infested foliage.
- Apply Malathion or Diazinon for chemical control of aphids and whiteflies.
3. Additional resources:
- Detailed information on pest control is provided in "Keeping Your Garden Healthy" in Part 1.
tomato pests, aphids, hornworms, cutworms, pest control
Pest Control for Chinese Cabbage Without Chemicals
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to Control Pests on Chinese Cabbage Organically:
1. Aphids: Hand-pick them or hose them off the plants.
2. Cabbage worms: Spray with bacillus thuringiensis, an organic product.
3. Flea beetles and cabbage loopers: Use carbaryl...
Steps to Control Pests on Chinese Cabbage Organically:
1. Aphids: Hand-pick them or hose them off the plants.
2. Cabbage worms: Spray with bacillus thuringiensis, an organic product.
3. Flea beetles and cabbage loopers: Use carbaryl for chemical control if necessary.
4. For detailed information on pest control, refer to "Keeping Your Garden Healthy" in Part 1 of the source text.
chinese cabbage, pest control, organic methods
Preventing Root Maggot Infestations with Plastic Shields
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to prevent root maggots using plastic shields:

1. Materials needed: Plastic shields that are 3 to 4 inches square.
2. Installation: Place each shield around the base of seedlings or young plants, ensuring it does not cover the...
Steps to prevent root maggots using plastic shields:

1. Materials needed: Plastic shields that are 3 to 4 inches square.
2. Installation: Place each shield around the base of seedlings or young plants, ensuring it does not cover the soil when cultivating.
3. Monitor regularly: Check for signs of root maggot damage such as poor plant growth or wilting.

Important Notes:
• Root maggots attack a variety of vegetables including beans, broccoli, Brussels sprouts, cabbage, carrots, cauliflower, radishes, spinach, squash, and turnips.
• Avoid covering the shields with soil to prevent root maggots from laying eggs near the plants.
root maggot prevention, plastic shields, vegetable gardening
Proper Use of Insecticides for Vegetable Gardening
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to Safely and Effectively Apply Insecticides:

1. Read the Label Carefully:
- Read the whole label; observe all the precautions and follow all the directions exactly.
- Check the time period that must elapse between application...
Steps to Safely and Effectively Apply Insecticides:

1. Read the Label Carefully:
- Read the whole label; observe all the precautions and follow all the directions exactly.
- Check the time period that must elapse between application of the insecticide and harvesting the plant, and observe it strictly.
- Note all restrictions carefully — often products must be applied at a certain stage in the plant's development.

2. Protect Yourself During Application:
- Wear rubber gloves while handling insecticide concentrates.
- Don't smoke while you're handling them.
- Take care not to breathe the spray or dust.

3. Mix Sprays Correctly:
- Sprays usually have to be mixed before each use.
- Follow the directions, and use _only_ the exact proportions indicated on the label.
- If it's not used exactly as indicated, an insecticide may be harmful to people, animals, or plants.

4. Use Dedicated Equipment:
- Use equipment that you keep specifically for use with insecticides.
- Don't use equipment that has been used for herbicides.

5. Apply Insecticides Safely:
- Do not apply an insecticide near fish ponds, dug wells, or cisterns.
- Do not leave puddles of pesticides on solid surfaces.
- Use a spray or dust-type insecticide only when the air is still.
- Wind will carry the product away from your garden and, possibly, be a nuisance to someone else.
- Don't spray or dust within 12 to 24 hours of an expected rain — the insecticide must dry on the plants to be effective; rain will wash it off.

6. Clean Up After Use:
- After using an insecticide, wash your clothes and equipment thoroughly.
insecticides,gardening,vegetables,application,safety,pest control
Protecting Bees When Using Carbaryl (Sevin)
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Carbaryl can be toxic to bees, which are essential for pollinating fruiting vegetable crops. To protect bees, apply carbaryl in the late evening when flowers are closed, ensuring that bees are not active during application. This helps...
Carbaryl can be toxic to bees, which are essential for pollinating fruiting vegetable crops. To protect bees, apply carbaryl in the late evening when flowers are closed, ensuring that bees are not active during application. This helps eliminate pests without harming pollinators.
carbaryl, sevin, bees, pollination, late evening application, toxicity
Selecting and Applying Chemical Insecticides for Pest Control
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Objective: Properly select and apply chemical insecticides to control harmful pests in the vegetable garden.

Procedure:
1. Understand Terminology:
- Pesticide: Any form of chemical used in the garden (includes insecticides,...
Objective: Properly select and apply chemical insecticides to control harmful pests in the vegetable garden.

Procedure:
1. Understand Terminology:
- Pesticide: Any form of chemical used in the garden (includes insecticides, herbicides, etc.).
- Insecticide: A specific type of pesticide that targets insects only.
- Herbicide: A pesticide that targets weeds and should not be confused with insecticides.
2. Choose the Right Insecticide:
- Select an insecticide based on the type of pest you are targeting (e.g., Malathion is effective for many common pests).
3. Application Methods:
- Spraying: Apply liquid insecticides using a hand sprayer or a spray attachment on your garden hose.
- Ensure thorough coverage, including both the tops and undersides of leaves.
- Dusting: Use dust-on insecticides by pumping the powder directly onto affected plants.
- Soil Drenching: Apply liquid insecticides directly to the soil surface to target pests living beneath it.
4. Follow Safety Guidelines:
- Wear protective clothing (gloves, long sleeves, etc.) when applying insecticides.
- Avoid spraying on windy days to prevent drift.
5. Residual Effects and Toxicity:
- Insecticides used in home gardens typically break down quickly and have low toxicity.
- Most common insecticides remain on plants for about 14 days before breaking down (e.g., Malathion has a toxicity level similar to Scotch whiskey).
6. Storage and Equipment Separation:
- Store insecticides separately from herbicides and other pesticides.
- Use dedicated equipment for each type of pesticide to avoid cross-contamination.
insecticide application, chemical pest control, spraying techniques, soil drenching, pesticide terminology
Selecting and Using Diazinon Insecticide
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Diazinon is an organic phosphate insecticide effective for general use in home vegetable gardens. It acts as a contact poison with low toxicity, making it suitable for controlling underground insects that attack the roots of cabbage family...
Diazinon is an organic phosphate insecticide effective for general use in home vegetable gardens. It acts as a contact poison with low toxicity, making it suitable for controlling underground insects that attack the roots of cabbage family plants, onions, and radishes. It can be obtained in wettable powder or liquid form. Always handle insecticides with care, as they are poisons.
diazinon, organic phosphate, contact poison, root pests, cabbage family, onions, radishes
Selecting and Using Sevin (Carbaryl) Insecticide
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Sevin, also known as carbaryl, is a safe insecticide for use in home vegetable gardens. It is effective against many leaf-eating caterpillars and leafhoppers, and can be obtained as a wettable powder or dust. Note that it must be...
Sevin, also known as carbaryl, is a safe insecticide for use in home vegetable gardens. It is effective against many leaf-eating caterpillars and leafhoppers, and can be obtained as a wettable powder or dust. Note that it must be ingested by the insect to take effect, so it may not work well on pests like aphids whose mouthparts are inside the plant.
sevin, carbaryl, leaf-eating caterpillars, leafhoppers, wettable powder, dust
Understanding Insecticide Residual Effects in Vegetable Gardens
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Insecticides used in vegetable gardens have residual effects, meaning they can remain on plants for some time after application. These residues may be poisonous if ingested, so it is important to consider the duration of the residual...
Insecticides used in vegetable gardens have residual effects, meaning they can remain on plants for some time after application. These residues may be poisonous if ingested, so it is important to consider the duration of the residual effect before applying any insecticide. Residual effects vary depending on the type of crop and the specific insecticide used, and because these effects are generally short-lived, preventive spraying is not recommended due to uncertainty about which pests may attack.
insecticide residual effect, vegetable garden safety, poison residue, crop-specific effects, preventive spraying
Using Bacillus thuringiensis Insecticide
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is an organic insecticide that targets caterpillars and other pests without harming beneficial insects or humans. It is available under brand names such as Dipel, Thuricide, or Bactur in wettable powder or...
Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is an organic insecticide that targets caterpillars and other pests without harming beneficial insects or humans. It is available under brand names such as Dipel, Thuricide, or Bactur in wettable powder or liquid form. This is a preferred choice for gardeners who want to avoid chemical insecticides.
bacillus thuringiensis, organic insecticide, caterpillars, dipel,thuricide,bactur,wettable powder,liquid
Using Organic Insecticides for Pest Control in the Cabbage Family
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Organic Control of Pests on Cabbage Family Plants:

• Use Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt), an organic product that is harmless to humans and animals.
• This method can effectively control caterpillars attacking cabbage family plants such as:
-...
Organic Control of Pests on Cabbage Family Plants:

• Use Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt), an organic product that is harmless to humans and animals.
• This method can effectively control caterpillars attacking cabbage family plants such as:
- Broccoli
- Brussels sprouts
- Cabbage
- Cauliflower
- Collards
- Kale
- Kohlrabi
• Other insects that commonly attack the cabbage family plants can usually be controlled by natural and physical methods.
organic pest control,cabbage family,Bacillus thuringiensis,gardening,vegetables

Note: These vegetables are generally not attacked by pests in large enough numbers to cause significant problems or require nonorganic methods of control.

Control Ants in the Garden
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Ants, except for the leaf-cutting varieties found in the South and West, generally do not create much of a problem for the home gardener. If they do nest in your garden, they can be **controlled physically by digging up and destroying their...
Ants, except for the leaf-cutting varieties found in the South and West, generally do not create much of a problem for the home gardener. If they do nest in your garden, they can be controlled physically by digging up and destroying their nest. They can also be controlled chemically by drenching their nests with Diazinon.
ants,nest destruction,Diazinon,physical control
Control Aphids and Beetles on Black-Eyed Peas
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Physically remove aphids and beetles by hand-picking them from the plants.
2. Hose off the plants to dislodge aphids and beetles.
3. Pinch out aphid-infested vegetation to eliminate infestations.
4. **Use a chemical...
Procedure:

1. Physically remove aphids and beetles by hand-picking them from the plants.
2. Hose off the plants to dislodge aphids and beetles.
3. Pinch out aphid-infested vegetation to eliminate infestations.
4. Use a chemical spray of Diazinon or Malathion for controlling aphids and beetles.

Details:
• This method is effective for managing pest populations on black-eyed peas.
• Ensure that the plants are dry before handling them to avoid spreading disease.
aphids, beetles, black-eyed peas, pest control, Diazinon, Malathion
Control Cabbage Loopers and Cabbage Worms with Organic Insecticide
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Cabbage loopers and cabbage worms love to feed on all members of the cabbage family; occasionally they will make do with lettuce. To control them, spray with Bacillus thuringiensis, an organic insecticide that is available under a number of...
Cabbage loopers and cabbage worms love to feed on all members of the cabbage family; occasionally they will make do with lettuce. To control them, spray with Bacillus thuringiensis, an organic insecticide that is available under a number of trade names including Dipel, Thuricide, and Bactur. This is a completely safe organic spray that will destroy the caterpillars without harming humans or animals.
cabbage looper,cabbage worm,Bacillus thuringiensis,Dipel,Thuricide,Bactur,organic insecticide
Control Chewing Pests Using Nonchemical Methods
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Chewing pests are usually easy to find, especially when they have put in a good clay's work, and they're easier to control by nonchemical methods than the nocturnal and underground pests are. Many of them can be hand-picked off the plant or...
Chewing pests are usually easy to find, especially when they have put in a good clay's work, and they're easier to control by nonchemical methods than the nocturnal and underground pests are. Many of them can be hand-picked off the plant or knocked off with a blast from the hose. Almost every chewing insect that feeds on the outside of the plant can be controlled chemically by using carbaryl. Check to make sure that you have identified the guilty party; apply insecticide when the pest is first discovered, and repeat the treatment as often as necessary according to the directions on the label.
chewing pests,nonchemical methods,carbaryl,hand-picking,hose blast
Control Grasshoppers with Hand-Picking and Carbaryl Spraying
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Grasshoppers have great appetites and will eat anything and everything. They usually appear in late summer and are more active where the winters are warm and the summers are hot. Try to control them when they are young by **hand-picking them off...
Grasshoppers have great appetites and will eat anything and everything. They usually appear in late summer and are more active where the winters are warm and the summers are hot. Try to control them when they are young by hand-picking them off the plants or by destroying the untended weedy spots near the garden where they begin life. Control them chemically when they are young by spraying untended weedy areas with carbaryl.
grasshoppers,hand-picking,untended weedy spots,carbaryl
Control Hornworms with Hand-Picking and Bacillus Thuringiensis
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Hornworms are large green caterpillars three to four inches long with a hornlike growth on their rear end. They eat the foliage and fruit of your tomato plants. Since the large hornworms do not usually invade in great numbers, **hand-pick them...
Hornworms are large green caterpillars three to four inches long with a hornlike growth on their rear end. They eat the foliage and fruit of your tomato plants. Since the large hornworms do not usually invade in great numbers, hand-pick them individually off the plants. If your garden is invaded by numerous hornworms, spray them with Bacillus thuringiensis.
hornworm,Bacillus thuringiensis,hand-picking,tomato plants
Control Leaf Miners by Removing Affected Leaves and Egg Masses
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Leaf miners are the larvae of a fly that feed on the external portions of a leaf. They will feed on cabbage and its relatives, and on chard, beets, and occasionally lettuce. The best method of controlling the leaf miner is to **remove affected...
Leaf miners are the larvae of a fly that feed on the external portions of a leaf. They will feed on cabbage and its relatives, and on chard, beets, and occasionally lettuce. The best method of controlling the leaf miner is to remove affected leaves from the plant by hand and to hand-pick egg masses that can usually be found on the backside of older leaves. Since the leaf miner is inside the leaf surface, chemical controls are ineffective.
leaf miner,affected leaves,egg masses,hand-picking
Control Parsley Caterpillars with Hand-Picking
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Parsley caterpillars feed on parsley, dill, fennel, and other members of the parsley family. They're not common enough to be a major problem, and hand-picking usually provides satisfactory control.
parsley caterpillar,hand-picking,parsley,dill,fennel
Control Pests in Corn Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Monitor for pests such as cutworms, wireworms, flea beetles, corn earworms, and corn borers.
2. Use a soil drench of Diazinon to control cutworms and wireworms.
3. Spray flea beetles with carbaryl when they first appear.
4. Watch...
Procedure:
1. Monitor for pests such as cutworms, wireworms, flea beetles, corn earworms, and corn borers.
2. Use a soil drench of Diazinon to control cutworms and wireworms.
3. Spray flea beetles with carbaryl when they first appear.
4. Watch for corn earworms; spray with carbaryl before the caterpillars enter the ears, as no effective control is possible once inside.
5. For corn borers, spray with carbaryl every five days starting from the first appearance of eggs.
6. Remove raccoon and rodent habitats and fence the garden to deter them.
corn pests,insecticide application,cutworm control,wireworm treatment,flea beetle spray
Control Slugs and Snails with Physical Methods and Beer Traps
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Slugs and snails — snails have shells and slugs don't. Both are more closely related to oysters and clams than they are to insects. You can detect their presence by the slimy trail they leave from the scene of their activity. They don't like to...
Slugs and snails — snails have shells and slugs don't. Both are more closely related to oysters and clams than they are to insects. You can detect their presence by the slimy trail they leave from the scene of their activity. They don't like to be out in the heat of the day; they eat and run and can be hard to control. They like to feed on cabbage and all its relatives, and on carrots, lettuce, tomatoes, and turnips. To control them:

1. Remove their hiding places — old boards, cans, bricks, and other garbage.
2. Lay scratchy sand or cinders around each plant to discourage them.
3. Set a saucer in the soil with the rim flush with the soil surface, and fill it with stale beer. The slugs and snails will be attracted to the beer and fall in and drown. Then you can dispose of them in the morning.
slugs,snails,sand,cinders,stale beer,traps,hiding places
Control Spider Mites on Black-Eyed Peas
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Remove affected plants before spider mites can spread to healthy plants.
2. Spray the undersides of the foliage with Diazinon to control spider mite infestations.

Details:
• Spider mites are difficult to manage even...
Procedure:

1. Remove affected plants before spider mites can spread to healthy plants.
2. Spray the undersides of the foliage with Diazinon to control spider mite infestations.

Details:
• Spider mites are difficult to manage even with proper chemicals.
• Focus on spraying the undersides of leaves where spider mites tend to congregate.
spider mites, black-eyed peas, pest control, Diazinon
Control Squash Bugs and Beetles
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
**Procedure for Controlling Squash Bugs and Beetles:

1.** Inspect squash plants regularly for the presence of pests such as squash bugs, cucumber beetles, and squash borers.

2. Hand-pick any visible pests from the plants and dispose of them in...
**Procedure for Controlling Squash Bugs and Beetles:

1.** Inspect squash plants regularly for the presence of pests such as squash bugs, cucumber beetles, and squash borers.

2. Hand-pick any visible pests from the plants and dispose of them in a sealed container or by drowning them in soapy water.

3. Use a hose to wash off beetles from the leaves and stems of the plant.

4. Apply carbaryl (a chemical insecticide) to control beetles, following the manufacturer's instructions for safe application.

5. For squash borers, apply carbaryl directly to the crowns of the plants on a weekly basis as soon as there is any suspicion of damage.

6. If you notice a small hole in the stem (indicating that borers have entered), slit the stem carefully with a clean knife, remove the borers, and dispose of them.

7. After removing the borers, cover the slit area with soil to encourage root development at that point.

Note: Chemical controls are ineffective once borers have entered the plant, so early detection is critical.
squash bugs,cucumber beetles,squash borers,carbaryl,pest control,gardening
Controlling Aphids on Artichokes
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Identify aphid infestation by inspecting the foliage.
2. Control chemically by spraying the foliage with Malathion or Diazinon according to label instructions.
3. Control nonchemically by:
- Hand-picking aphids from...
Procedure:

1. Identify aphid infestation by inspecting the foliage.
2. Control chemically by spraying the foliage with Malathion or Diazinon according to label instructions.
3. Control nonchemically by:
- Hand-picking aphids from the plants.
- Hosing them off the plants with water.

Notes:
• Aphids are a common pest on artichokes.
• Plume moths are not a serious problem except in specific artichoke-growing areas.
aphid control,artichoke pests,organic pest control,chemical pest control

Common Pests:
• Cutworms
• Slugs and snails
• Aphids

Control Methods:
1. Put a collar around each plant to discourage cutworms.
2. Trap slugs and snails with a saucer of stale beer set flush to the soil.
3. Control aphids by:
- Pinching out infested foliage.
- Hosing aphids off the plants.
- Using chemical controls like Malathion or Diazinon, ensuring to spray the undersides of leaves.

• Hand-pick or hose them off, or control them chemically by spraying with Malathion or Diazinon.
• Detailed information on pest control is given in "Keeping Your Garden...
### Controlling Pests on Chayote

• Aphids may visit your chayote vines.
• Hand-pick or hose them off, or control them chemically by spraying with Malathion or Diazinon.
• Detailed information on pest control is given in "Keeping Your Garden Healthy" in Parti.

Controlling Pests on Chick Peas
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Chick peas may be attacked by aphids, bean beetles, flea beetles, leafhoppers, and mites. Use chemical sprays such as Malathion or Diazinon for aphids, leafhoppers, and mites. For bean beetles and flea beetles, use carbaryl. Organic methods are less...
Chick peas may be attacked by aphids, bean beetles, flea beetles, leafhoppers, and mites. Use chemical sprays such as Malathion or Diazinon for aphids, leafhoppers, and mites. For bean beetles and flea beetles, use carbaryl. Organic methods are less effective but can still be used with lower yields.
pest control, chick peas, gardening
Controlling Pests on Squash Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Identify common pests such as squash bugs, squash borers, and cucumber beetles.
2. Hand-pick or hose off visible beetles from the plants to reduce their population.
3. Apply carbaryl insecticide to the crowns of the plants weekly...
Procedure:
1. Identify common pests such as squash bugs, squash borers, and cucumber beetles.
2. Hand-pick or hose off visible beetles from the plants to reduce their population.
3. Apply carbaryl insecticide to the crowns of the plants weekly if there is any suspicion of borer infestation.
4. If a small hole in the stem indicates that borers are inside, slit the stem and remove the borers manually.
5. Cover the damaged area with soil to encourage root development.

Notes:
• Chemical controls (like carbaryl) become ineffective once borers have entered the plant.
• Regular monitoring is essential for early detection of pest infestations.
pest control,squash,borers,carbaryl
Identify and Control Beetles in the Garden
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Beetles come in many sizes and shapes. Some prefer one or two special vegetables; others chew on whatever looks appetizing at the time. Some get their names from their favorite delicacy. Asparagus beetles feed on asparagus; **Mexican bean...
Beetles come in many sizes and shapes. Some prefer one or two special vegetables; others chew on whatever looks appetizing at the time. Some get their names from their favorite delicacy. Asparagus beetles feed on asparagus; Mexican bean beetles love beans. Blister beetles feed on beans, beets,chard, corn, potatoes, tomatoes, and squash. Cucumber beetles (spotted and striped) often attack melons, pumpkins, squash, and watermelon, as well as cucumbers. Their eating habits may not cause much damage, but they carry cucumber bacterial wilt, which will kill any of these plants when they're older. Flea beetles will eat almost any garden crop. They're very small, and it's difficult to spot them, but you'll know they're there when tiny black dots jump from the plants when you come near, and when you notice that the leaves of your vegetable plants are suddenly full of small holes scattered over the entire leaf surface. Potato beetles chew on eggplants, potatoes, and tomatoes. If there are too many small beetles to hand-pick, try to hose them off. All these beetles can be controlled chemically with carbaryl, used according to the label directions.
beetles,asparagus beetle,Mexican bean beetle,blister beetle,cucumber beetle,flea beetle,potato beetle,carbaryl
Identify and Differentiate Insect Pests in the Garden
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Objective: Correctly identify insect pests and distinguish them from non-insect pests that may harm vegetable plants.

Procedure:
1. Understand Common Misconceptions:
- Not all small crawling or flying creatures are insects. Examples...
Objective: Correctly identify insect pests and distinguish them from non-insect pests that may harm vegetable plants.

Procedure:
1. Understand Common Misconceptions:
- Not all small crawling or flying creatures are insects. Examples of non-insect pests include:
- Mites
- Slugs
- Snails
- Nematodes
- Sowbugs
- Symphylans
2. Recognize the Role of Insects:
- Some insects are beneficial and play a critical role in the garden, such as:
- Pollinators (e.g., bees, butterflies)
- Predatory insects that control harmful pests
3. Avoid Indiscriminate Pest Control:
- Be cautious with pest control methods to avoid harming beneficial insects.
4. Monitor Regularly:
- Inspect plants regularly for signs of damage or the presence of pests.
5. Use Resources:
- Consult gardening guides, local extension services, or online resources to help identify specific pests and their impact on your garden.
insect identification, pest differentiation, beneficial insects, non-insect pests, gardening monitoring
Identify and Monitor Sucking Pests in Vegetable Gardens
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Signs of Sucking Pest Infestation:
• Look for scraped or rusty-looking areas on leaves.
• Check for twisted, deformed leaves and leaf-tips.
• Inspect stems that appear unusually thick.

Action Steps:
1. Regularly inspect plants for early...
Signs of Sucking Pest Infestation:
• Look for scraped or rusty-looking areas on leaves.
• Check for twisted, deformed leaves and leaf-tips.
• Inspect stems that appear unusually thick.

Action Steps:
1. Regularly inspect plants for early signs of infestation.
2. Use magnifying glass if needed to spot small pests like aphids, mites, or thrips.
3. Monitor for honeydew excretions (a sign of aphid presence) and sooty mold growth.
4. Watch for light-colored spotting on leaves (a symptom of leafhopper damage).
5. Look for silvery webs on the undersides of leaves (an indicator of spider mites).
6. Check for white blotches or distorted leaf tips (signs of thrips).
aphids,leafhoppers,mites,thrips,sucking pests,garden monitoring
Implementing Natural Pest Control Methods in Vegetable Gardens
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Natural pest control methods involve using harmless insects to destroy harmful ones. These include:

• Ladybugs: Feed on destructive bugs.
• Lacewing flies: Consume harmful pests.
• Praying mantises: Prey on garden pests.
• **Aphid...
Natural pest control methods involve using harmless insects to destroy harmful ones. These include:

• Ladybugs: Feed on destructive bugs.
• Lacewing flies: Consume harmful pests.
• Praying mantises: Prey on garden pests.
• Aphid lions: Eat aphids and other damaging insects.

Important Considerations:
• These natural methods may not provide complete pest control. They should be used as part of a broader strategy, not relied upon entirely.
• Insects like ladybugs and praying mantises can be purchased but are unlikely to stay in your garden due to their shyness and tendency to fly away.
• Encourage these helpful insects by protecting them when they appear naturally in your garden.
natural pest control, ladybugs, lacewing flies, praying mantises, aphid lions, organic gardening
Managing Onion Thrips on Garlic Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Inspect garlic plants regularly for signs of onion thrips.
2. If onion thrips are present, hose them off the plants using a strong stream of water from a garden hose.
3. Repeat this process as needed to ensure all pests are...
Procedure:
1. Inspect garlic plants regularly for signs of onion thrips.
2. If onion thrips are present, hose them off the plants using a strong stream of water from a garden hose.
3. Repeat this process as needed to ensure all pests are removed.
Notes: Onion thrips do not typically pose a serious threat to garlic crops.
onion thrips,garlic,pest control,hosing,organic gardening

2. Insecticide application: If necessary, spray with carbaryl to control infestations.
3. **Reference for detailed pest control...
Procedure for controlling asparagus beetles:

2. Insecticide application: If necessary, spray with carbaryl to control infestations.
3. Reference for detailed pest control information: See "Keeping Your Garden Healthy" in Part I.
