Fertilize and Water Summer and Winter Savory Plants
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
To care for summer and winter savory plants:

1. Do not fertilize at midseason to avoid over-fertilization.
2. Both varieties prefer dry conditions and do better if kept on the dry side.
3. Avoid overwatering, as this can lead to root rot or...
To care for summer and winter savory plants:

1. Do not fertilize at midseason to avoid over-fertilization.
2. Both varieties prefer dry conditions and do better if kept on the dry side.
3. Avoid overwatering, as this can lead to root rot or other issues.
4. Ensure proper drainage in the soil to prevent waterlogging.

Note: Detailed information on fertilizing is given in "Spadework: The Essential Soil" in Parti.
summer savory,winter savory,fertilizing,watering,gardening
Handling and Cultivating Bean Plants
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Avoid handling bean plants when they are wet or covered with heavy dew, as this can spread fungus spores.

Cultivate thoroughly but carefully to avoid disturbing the shallow root systems of the plants.

For pole beans, set up trellises...
Avoid handling bean plants when they are wet or covered with heavy dew, as this can spread fungus spores.

Cultivate thoroughly but carefully to avoid disturbing the shallow root systems of the plants.

For pole beans, set up trellises or supports before planting or at the same time. Waiting until after the plants have established themselves risks damaging the roots when setting up supports.

Ensure that the support is tall enough for the specific variety of bean being planted.
handling beans,cultivating beans,pole bean trellis,root care
Setting Up Supports for Pole Beans
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Set the trellis or support in position before planting or at the same time as planting pole beans.
2. Do not wait until the plants are established, as this risks damaging the roots when setting up supports.
3. **Ensure the...
Procedure:

1. Set the trellis or support in position before planting or at the same time as planting pole beans.
2. Do not wait until the plants are established, as this risks damaging the roots when setting up supports.
3. Ensure the support is tall enough for the variety being grown.
pole beans,trellis setup,root damage,support height
Treatment of Pineapple Plants for Growth and Fruit Production
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Treating Pineapple Plants:

1. General Care: Keep the plants growing gently to ensure the pots are completely filled with roots by the time you want them to bloom.
2. Timing for Blooming: From mid-February to March is an...
Procedure for Treating Pineapple Plants:

1. General Care: Keep the plants growing gently to ensure the pots are completely filled with roots by the time you want them to bloom.
2. Timing for Blooming: From mid-February to March is an ideal period to have the main crop in flower, as this leads to the finest fruit season.
3. Dressing the Plants: About a month before expecting fruit, perform the following:
- Remove two inches of topsoil from the pot.
- Twist off some of the lower leaves.
- Fill the space around the stem with fresh compost up to the remaining leaves.
4. Reviving the Bark-Bed: At the same time, revive the bark-bed to make it lively; however, do not add new tan (compost or soil) until the time for the fullest heat arrives.

Controlling Suckers for Fruit Size and Multiplication:
1. To Encourage Large Fruit: Destroy suckers as they appear by twisting out their hearts with an iron-sharpened instrument designed for this purpose:
- Apply the tool to the heart of the sucker.
- Turn it around and remove the heart.
2. For Multiplication of Stock: Do not destroy the suckers; allow them to grow naturally.

Additional Techniques for Fruit Swelling:
1. Remove a few of the lower leaves from the plant.
2. Place a rim of tin or any hoop-shaped object around the top of the pot, raising the soil level by three to four inches.
3. Use high-quality soil and keep it moderately moist:
- Cover the surface with moistened moss to maintain moisture levels.
4. This method encourages rapid root growth, especially from the part of the stem just under the leaves, which benefits fruit development.
pineapple care, fruit production, sucker removal, plant treatment, gardening techniques
Treatment of Seedlings Until Transplanted to Fruiting Bed
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Treatment till removed to the Fruiting Bed:

1. After sowing, continue the glasses on the frame.
2. Give occasional vent above for steam to evaporate.
3. When plants are up in a few days:
- Admit air daily, but more guardedly at the upper...
Treatment till removed to the Fruiting Bed:

1. After sowing, continue the glasses on the frame.
2. Give occasional vent above for steam to evaporate.
3. When plants are up in a few days:
- Admit air daily, but more guardedly at the upper ends of the lights.
4. In frosty weather, hang part of a mat over the aperture.
5. When the plants are a little advanced and the seed leaves are about half an inch broad:
- Take them up and prick some in small pots of light earth previously warmed by the heat of the bed.
- Put three plants in each pot and insert them a little slopingly, quite to the seed-leaves.
6. Plunge the pots into the earth.
7. Prick some plants also into the earth of the bed.
8. Give a very little water just to the roots; the water should be previously warmed to the temperature of the bed.
9. Draw on the glasses, but admit air daily to promote growth and give vent to steam rising in the bed:
- Tilt the lights behind from half an inch to an inch or two high, in proportion to the heat of the bed and the temperature of the weather.
10. Cover the glasses every night with garden mats and remove them timely in the morning.
11. Give twice a week, once in two days or daily, according to the season, a very light watering.
12. Keep up a moderate lively heat in the bed by requisite linings of hot dung to the sides.
seedlings, transplanting, gardening, plant care
Vegetable Liquor to Hasten Blooming of Bulbous-Rooted Flowers
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Vegetable Liquor to hasten the Blowing of Bulbous-Rooted Flowers:

1. Prepare a solution by combining:
- 3 ounces of nitre (potassium nitrate)
- 1 ounce of common salt
- 1 ounce of potash (potassium carbonate)
- **½ ounce...
Vegetable Liquor to hasten the Blowing of Bulbous-Rooted Flowers:

1. Prepare a solution by combining:
- 3 ounces of nitre (potassium nitrate)
- 1 ounce of common salt
- 1 ounce of potash (potassium carbonate)
- ½ ounce of sugar
- 1 pound of rain-water or river-water
2. Dissolve the salts in a glazed earthen pot over gentle heat.
3. Once dissolved, add the sugar and filter the mixture thoroughly.
4. Add approximately eight drops of this solution to a glass jar filled with rain or river water.
5. Keep the jars always full by replacing the water every ten to twelve days, adding an equal amount of the vegetable liquor each time.
6. Place the flowers on the corner of a chimney-piece where a fire is regularly kept for optimal growth.
7. The same mixture can be used to water flowers in pots or fill dishes containing bulbs or plants to maintain moisture.
vegetable liquor, bulbous-rooted flowers, nitre solution, chimney-piece heating
Caring for Biennial and Perennial Plants in the Garden
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Caring for Biennial and Perennial Plants:

• Spring Care: Loosen the earth around plants in spring.
• Transplanting: Spring is the ideal time for transplanting these plants.
• Summer Pruning: In summer, prune away weak...
Procedure for Caring for Biennial and Perennial Plants:

• Spring Care: Loosen the earth around plants in spring.
• Transplanting: Spring is the ideal time for transplanting these plants.
• Summer Pruning: In summer, prune away weak stems to encourage healthy growth.
• Fall Covering: In fall, cover plants with coarse manure. If evergreen, shelter them with cedar or pine boughs.

Note: These care instructions apply to many biennial and perennial flowering plants.
biennial, perennial, plant care, gardening

Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia

• Repotting: Repot heliotrope plants often to ensure healthy growth.
• Growth Management: Allow the plant to grow large, trimming only for shape if necessary.
• Fragrance: The plant is known for...
Procedure for Caring for Heliotrope:

• Repotting: Repot heliotrope plants often to ensure healthy growth.
• Growth Management: Allow the plant to grow large, trimming only for shape if necessary.
• Fragrance: The plant is known for its delightful fragrance and is not hard to cultivate.

Note: Heliotrope can be managed similarly to verbenas in terms of care.
heliotrope, plant care, gardening, fragrance

• Planting Time: Plant out the lemon verbena in the spring.
• Uses: The plant is grown for its sweet scent of leaves and is a...
Procedure for Caring for Lemon Verbena:

• Winter Storage: Keep the plant in a cellar all winter.
• Planting Time: Plant out the lemon verbena in the spring.
• Uses: The plant is grown for its sweet scent of leaves and is a half-hardy shrub.

Note: This method ensures survival through cold seasons.
lemon verbena, winter care, gardening, fragrance

Procedure:

- Examples include the Four-o'clock and Scarlet Bean.

2. Overwintering:
- These plants should be kept through the winter to plant out again in the following season.

3. Planting location:

Caring for Verbenas in the Garden
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Caring for Verbenas:

• Watering: Provide moderate watering, as verbenas are native to dry, hilly ground and do not require excessive moisture.
• Fertilization: No specific fertilization is mentioned; however, general...
Procedure for Caring for Verbenas:

• Watering: Provide moderate watering, as verbenas are native to dry, hilly ground and do not require excessive moisture.
• Fertilization: No specific fertilization is mentioned; however, general care includes loosening the earth around them in spring if they are biennials or perennials.
• Pruning: In summer, prune away weak stems.
• Winter Care: In fall, cover verbenas with coarse manure. If evergreen, shelter them with cedar or pine boughs.

Note: These care instructions are also applicable to many biennial and perennial flowering plants.
verbenas, plant care, gardening, pruning, watering
Cultivating Muskmelons to Control Weeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Carefully cultivate the soil around muskmelon plants until the vines cover the ground. The roots are shallow and spread out, so be cautious not to damage them. Alternatively, plant muskmelons three feet apart on fences instead of in inverted...
Carefully cultivate the soil around muskmelon plants until the vines cover the ground. The roots are shallow and spread out, so be cautious not to damage them. Alternatively, plant muskmelons three feet apart on fences instead of in inverted hills. Provide support for melons growing on a fence using a net or bag — old pantyhose can also work. If growing in hills, place a board under each melon to keep it off the ground.
cultivating,muskmelons,weed_control,fencing

2. Fill the watering tray a few days before planting to allow water to wick up into the growing medium.

Procedure for Preparing the Wicky Grower Before Planting

1. Ensure that the soil in your Wicky Grower is well-watered.
2. Fill the watering tray a few days before planting to allow water to wick up into the growing medium.
3. Alternatively, manually moisten the soil medium before adding it to your Wicky Grower.
4. Check that the soil is evenly damp but not overly saturated.
5. If using a custom growing medium, follow the instructions below for mixing and preparation.

Restore Wilting Flowers Using Hot Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To restore Flowers:

1. Most cut flowers begin to droop and fade after being in water for 24 hours.
2. Some may be revived by substituting fresh water, but most can be fully restored with the following method:
3. Place the flowers in...
To restore Flowers:

1. Most cut flowers begin to droop and fade after being in water for 24 hours.
2. Some may be revived by substituting fresh water, but most can be fully restored with the following method:
3. Place the flowers in scalding hot water, ensuring that about one-third of the stem is submerged.
4. Allow the water to cool completely; during this time, the flowers will become erect and fresh again.
5. Once cooled, cut off the coddled ends of the stems and place them in cold water for final restoration.
flower restoration, hot water revival, wilting flowers
Separating Crowns and Suckers from Pineapple Plants
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Separating Crowns and Suckers:

1. Detaching Crowns: When the fruit is served at table, gently twist the crown off and return it to the gardener if desired for a new plant.
2. Removing Fruit Stalk Suckers: Remove these at...
Procedure for Separating Crowns and Suckers:

1. Detaching Crowns: When the fruit is served at table, gently twist the crown off and return it to the gardener if desired for a new plant.
2. Removing Fruit Stalk Suckers: Remove these at the same time as detaching crowns.
3. Base Suckers: These are typically ready for separation when the fruit is mature. If the stool (main stem) is vigorous, they may be left on for a month after the fruit is cut, with plentiful waterings provided.
4. Indicators of Readiness: A brownish tint at the lower part of the leaves indicates a sucker is ready to be removed. If you break off the lower leaf and see this tint, use your thumb to easily dislodge the sucker.

Bringing Out Suckers from Old Plants:
1. Wait until the fruit is cut.
2. Remove the old plant from its pot and take it out of the bark-bed.
3. Strip off the underleaves near the root.
4. Use a knife to cut away leaves to six inches from the bottom.
5. Remove some of the stale mould from the pot and replace it with fresh soil.
6. Give the plant a little water.
7. Place the old plant in a bed with good growing heat.
8. Follow routine care procedures.
9. Once the old plants send out new suckers, allow them to grow until they are four inches long or more.
10. When signs of readiness appear (e.g., brownish tint on leaves), detach the suckers.

Post-Detachment Care:
1. Twist off some of the leaves at the base of the crown or sucker.
2. This creates a vacancy at the bottom of the stem, which helps in root emission.
3. Pare the stump smooth.
4. Place the detached plants on a shelf in a shaded part of the stove or any dry apartment.
5. For crowns and fruit off-sets, wait until the part that adhered to the fruit is fully healed before planting.
6. For root suckers, wait until the part that was united to the old stock becomes dry and firm.
7. These plants will be ready to plant in five or six days.
separating crowns, separating suckers, pineapple care, propagation, gardening techniques
Special Handling for Collard Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. If collard plants become too heavy, stake them to provide support and prevent damage.
collards, plant care, gardening
Transplanting Plants
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Transplanting Plants

1. Prepare the soil: Stir, loosen, mix, and comminute the soil. In many cases, add manure or compost depending on the nature of the soil and plant to be inserted, whether in open ground, pots, or...
Procedure for Transplanting Plants

1. Prepare the soil: Stir, loosen, mix, and comminute the soil. In many cases, add manure or compost depending on the nature of the soil and plant to be inserted, whether in open ground, pots, or hot-houses.

2. Remove the plant:
- Loosen the earth around the plant and draw it out with care, avoiding breaking or bruising the roots.
- For small seedlings: Insert a spade into the soil and raise the portion of earth in which they grow.
- For larger plants: Dig a trench around the plant to remove it safely.

3. Insertion into prepared soil:
- In some cases, lift the plant with a ball of earth containing all its roots using a trowel.
- For large shrubs or trees, cut the roots at a certain distance from the plant one year before removal to allow for young fibres that can support the change.
- When transplanting from pots, less care is needed as the roots and ball of earth may be preserved intact.
transplanting, plant removal, soil preparation, root care, gardening techniques
Watering Muskmelons
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Water muskmelons generously while the vines are growing. Continue watering until the melons are mature, then stop watering during fruit ripening to avoid over-saturation.
watering,muskmelons,irrigation
Blanch Leeks for White Stalks
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to Blanch Leeks:

1. As the leek stalk develops, hill the soil up around the lower part of the stem.
2. This process covers the base of the plant and blanches it, resulting in a large, white, succulent leek.
3. Continue hilling as...
Steps to Blanch Leeks:

1. As the leek stalk develops, hill the soil up around the lower part of the stem.
2. This process covers the base of the plant and blanches it, resulting in a large, white, succulent leek.
3. Continue hilling as needed to maintain coverage over time.

Details:
• Blanching improves the flavor and texture of the leeks by reducing exposure to sunlight.
• Ensure that the soil is loose enough for easy hilling without damaging the roots.
blanching leeks, white stalks, gardening techniques
Care for Caraway Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Fertilize before planting and again at midseason using the same rate as the rest of the garden (one pound per 100 square feet).
2. In the second year, do not fertilize at midseason.
3. Allow caraway plants to **dry out...
Procedure:

1. Fertilize before planting and again at midseason using the same rate as the rest of the garden (one pound per 100 square feet).
2. In the second year, do not fertilize at midseason.
3. Allow caraway plants to dry out between waterings; avoid overwatering.
4. If growing in colder areas, provide winter protection to ensure flower and seed production in the second year.
caraway, care, watering, fertilizing, gardening
Care for Chervil Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Fertilize before planting and again at midseason using the same rate as the rest of the garden (one pound per 100 square feet).
2. Keep chervil plants moist, especially during dry periods.
3. To encourage thicker foliage,...
Procedure:

1. Fertilize before planting and again at midseason using the same rate as the rest of the garden (one pound per 100 square feet).
2. Keep chervil plants moist, especially during dry periods.
3. To encourage thicker foliage, cut flower stems before they bloom.
chervil, care, watering, fertilizing, gardening
Caring for Cauliflower Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Monitor temperature conditions; cauliflower does not tolerate extreme heat or cold and may bolt if exposed to severe frost or excessive heat.
2. Provide consistent moisture, especially during the head formation stage, as...
Procedure:

1. Monitor temperature conditions; cauliflower does not tolerate extreme heat or cold and may bolt if exposed to severe frost or excessive heat.
2. Provide consistent moisture, especially during the head formation stage, as dry conditions can prevent proper development of the curd.
3. Apply mulch around plants to retain soil moisture and regulate temperature.
4. Watch for signs of bolting, such as premature flowering, which may indicate stress from environmental factors or improper timing.
5. Harvest promptly once the head is fully formed but before it begins to loosen or yellow.
cauliflower, plant care, bolting, watering, harvesting
Caring for Jerusalem Artichokes
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Do not fertilize the plants at midseason; they thrive on their own.
2. Water only during extremely dry periods. The plants can survive long dry spells, but tuber development requires a regular water supply.
3. Monitor for...
Procedure:

1. Do not fertilize the plants at midseason; they thrive on their own.
2. Water only during extremely dry periods. The plants can survive long dry spells, but tuber development requires a regular water supply.
3. Monitor for aphids, which may occasionally appear but are generally not a serious problem.
4. If aphids are present:
- Pinch out infested foliage or
- Hose the aphids off the plants.
5. As a last resort, use chemical controls such as Malathion or Diazinon, following instructions carefully.
6. Ensure that the soil is well-drained to prevent tuber rot.
7. Maintain general garden cleanliness and health to reduce disease risk.
8. If a plant becomes infected, remove it immediately to prevent spreading the disease to healthy plants.
Jerusalem artichokes, care, aphids, tuber rot, watering, fertilizing
Fertilize and Water Kohlrabi Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Fertilize kohlrabi plants before planting and again at midseason.
2. Use the same rate as for the rest of the garden:
- One pound per 100 square feet, or
- 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
3. Ensure...
Procedure:

1. Fertilize kohlrabi plants before planting and again at midseason.
2. Use the same rate as for the rest of the garden:
- One pound per 100 square feet, or
- 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
3. Ensure consistent moisture to prevent the plant from becoming woody.
4. Avoid overwatering, which can lead to root rot.

Details:
• Even moisture is essential for proper growth and development.
• Detailed information on fertilizing is provided in "Spadework: The Essential Soil" in Part 1 of the source text.
kohlrabi, fertilizing, watering, plant care
Fertilize and Water Leeks
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to Fertilize and Water Leeks:

1. Apply a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet before planting.
2. Repeat fertilization at midseason using the same rate...
Steps to Fertilize and Water Leeks:

1. Apply a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet before planting.
2. Repeat fertilization at midseason using the same rate as applied initially.
3. Provide leeks with plenty of water to keep them growing strongly and consistently.

Details:
• Fertilize according to guidelines in "Spadework: The Essential Soil" for more detailed information.
• Water regularly, especially during dry periods or when the plants are actively growing.

Fertilizing and Watering Brussels Sprouts
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
### Fertilizing

• Fertilize before planting and again at midseason, using the same rate as for the rest of the garden.
• For detailed information on fertilizing, refer to "Spadework: The Essential Soil" in Part I.

### Watering

• Brussels...
### Fertilizing

• Fertilize before planting and again at midseason, using the same rate as for the rest of the garden.
• For detailed information on fertilizing, refer to "Spadework: The Essential Soil" in Part I.

### Watering

• Brussels sprouts need abundant soil moisture and cool moist air for the best growth.
• Reduce watering as they approach maturity.

Fertilizing and Watering Squash Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Initial Fertilization: Apply a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet before planting.
2. Midseason Fertilization: Repeat the same fertilization...
Procedure:

1. Initial Fertilization: Apply a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet before planting.
2. Midseason Fertilization: Repeat the same fertilization process at midseason to ensure continued nutrient supply.
3. Watering:
- Keep the soil evenly moist throughout the growing season.
- Squash plants require a lot of water, especially during hot weather.
4. Wilting Response:
- If squash vines wilt on hot days, it is normal and not a cause for concern if they receive regular watering.
- If wilting occurs in the morning, water the plants immediately to prevent stress.
fertilizing, watering, squash care, soil moisture
Pinching Basil Plants for Growth
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Pinch off the terminal shoots of basil plants to encourage branching and slow down flower production. If you do not pinch them, the plants will become tall and leggy.
basil,pinching,growth,branching
Pruning Circling Roots
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Procedure for Pruning Circling Roots

1. Identify circling roots in the plant's root system.
2. Expose the circling roots to air by carefully removing them from the soil or potting medium.
3. Use clean, sharp pruning shears or scissors to cut...
Procedure for Pruning Circling Roots

1. Identify circling roots in the plant's root system.
2. Expose the circling roots to air by carefully removing them from the soil or potting medium.
3. Use clean, sharp pruning shears or scissors to cut off the circling roots at the base where they begin to circle.
4. Allow the exposed roots to dry out slightly in the air for a short period (a few minutes) before replanting.
5. Replant the plant into fresh potting mix, ensuring that the root system is not constrained again.
6. Monitor the plant closely after pruning and provide proper care to encourage new, healthy root growth.

Note: Circling roots can cause restricted growth and poor nutrient uptake if left unaddressed. Pruning them helps the plant develop a more robust and branched root system.
pruning roots,circling roots,root care,plant growth,soil preparation
Special Handling of Brussels Sprouts Plants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
### Pruning for Cold Winters

• If you live in an area with cold winters, pick off the top terminal bud when the plant is 15 to 20 inches tall. This encourages all sprouts to mature at once.

### Leaf Removal

• Remove the lower leaves from...
### Pruning for Cold Winters

• If you live in an area with cold winters, pick off the top terminal bud when the plant is 15 to 20 inches tall. This encourages all sprouts to mature at once.

### Leaf Removal

• Remove the lower leaves from the sides of the stalk as the sprouts develop.
• A few more leaves can be removed each week, but leave the top leaves intact.
pruning, leaf removal, cold winter care, brussels sprouts

Herb Cultivation (34) -

Serving Suggestions for Rosemary
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use rosemary with caution as it can easily overpower other flavors.
2. Traditionally used with lamb or pork.
3. Combine with a little lemon juice and chopped parsley, then sprinkle on chicken before baking.
culinary use, rosemary recipes, herb pairing
Harvesting Herbs for Storage (Freezing, Drying, or Salting)
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Harvesting Herbs for Long-Term Storage

1. Choose the right time: Harvest herbs when the flowers of the plant are just beginning to open — this is when flavor is at its peak.
2. Cut on a dry day: Cut the plants on a dry,...
Procedure for Harvesting Herbs for Long-Term Storage

1. Choose the right time: Harvest herbs when the flowers of the plant are just beginning to open — this is when flavor is at its peak.
2. Cut on a dry day: Cut the plants on a dry, sunny morning after the dew has dried but before the sun gets too hot.
3. Select leaves: Use the young, tender, pungent leaves from the top six inches of the plant; strip off the tough lower leaves and remove flower clusters.
4. Clean the herbs: Rinse the herbs with cold water to remove dirt and dust, then blot them dry with paper towels.
5. Prepare for storage:
- For freezing: Wrap herbs in plastic wrap or place in airtight containers and freeze.
- For drying: Use a dehydrator or spread herbs on a rack in a well-ventilated area away from direct sunlight.
- For salting: Not commonly used, but some methods involve preserving herbs with salt.

Notes: Dried herbs will keep for up to a year; frozen herbs can be stored for several months if properly wrapped.
harvesting herbs, storing herbs, freezing herbs, drying herbs
Harvesting Sage
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Sage takes 75 days from planting to harvest.
2. Cut six to eight inches from the top of the plants at least twice during the growing season.
3. Pick leaves as desired, but avoid cutting back more than necessary.
harvesting sage, herb harvesting, pruning sage
Pest and Disease Management for Rosemary and Sage
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Rosemary has no serious pest or disease problems.
2. Sage also has no serious pest or disease problems.
3. Both herbs do well in an organic garden, minimizing the need for chemical interventions.
pest control, disease prevention, organic gardening
Plant Mint Using Root Divisions
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Select healthy root divisions from an existing mint plant.
2. Choose a container to prevent the roots from spreading uncontrollably (e.g., a two-pound coffee can with both ends removed).
3. Fill the container with soil,...
Procedure:
1. Select healthy root divisions from an existing mint plant.
2. Choose a container to prevent the roots from spreading uncontrollably (e.g., a two-pound coffee can with both ends removed).
3. Fill the container with soil, ensuring it is well-draining and slightly moist.
4. Place the root division into the soil, covering it lightly with soil.
5. Plant the container in the garden in a location that receives full sun or partial shade.
6. Space plants two to three inches apart in rows 18 to 24 inches apart for optimal growth.
mint, planting, root divisions, container gardening
Care for Mint Plants After Planting
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Water the plants regularly to keep the soil evenly moist until the root divisions are established.
2. Avoid fertilizing peppermint during midseason; it will grow abundantly without additional nutrients.
3. **Fertilize...
Procedure:
1. Water the plants regularly to keep the soil evenly moist until the root divisions are established.
2. Avoid fertilizing peppermint during midseason; it will grow abundantly without additional nutrients.
3. Fertilize spearmint with a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at a rate of one pound per 100 square feet before planting.
4. Monitor the plants for signs of disease or pests, though mint is generally resistant to serious pest problems.
5. Remove dead stems and leaves in winter to prevent diseases such as verticillium wilt and mint rust.
mint care, watering, fertilizing, disease prevention

Procedure:

2. If the weather is dry:
- Water regularly to keep the soil moist.
3. Avoid letting the roots dry out.

Harvesting Herb Seeds for Storage
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Harvesting and Storing Herb Seeds

1. Timing: Harvest seeds as soon as the seed heads turn brown but before they ripen completely and begin to fall off.
2. Choose a dry day: Harvest on a warm, dry day to avoid...
Procedure for Harvesting and Storing Herb Seeds

1. Timing: Harvest seeds as soon as the seed heads turn brown but before they ripen completely and begin to fall off.
2. Choose a dry day: Harvest on a warm, dry day to avoid moisture-related issues.
3. Dry the seeds: Dry the seeds in their pods, husks, or coverings.
4. Remove coverings: Use winnowing — rub a few seeds at a time between your palms to loosen and remove the pod or husk.
5. Store properly: Store the cleaned seeds in an airtight container in a cool, dry place.

Notes: Herb seeds should not be frozen or salted for storage.
harvesting herb seeds, storing herb seeds
Harvesting Herbs for Fresh Use
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Harvesting and Using Fresh Herbs

1. Gentle harvesting: Gently remove a few leaves at a time or pinch off sprigs to be used immediately.
2. Rinse and dry: Rinse the herbs with cold water, then pat them dry with paper...
Procedure for Harvesting and Using Fresh Herbs

1. Gentle harvesting: Gently remove a few leaves at a time or pinch off sprigs to be used immediately.
2. Rinse and dry: Rinse the herbs with cold water, then pat them dry with paper towels.
3. Chop for use: Chop the herbs finely before adding to soups, salads, sauces, or other recipes.
4. Store if not using immediately: Wrap the herbs in a damp paper towel, then place them in plastic wrap or a plastic bag and refrigerate.
5. Refrigeration limits: Fresh herbs can be stored in the refrigerator for up to 1 or 2 days — no longer than that.

Notes: Avoid over-harvesting from plants; only take what you need at one time.
harvesting herbs, using fresh herbs, herb storage
Special Handling for Tarragon in Cold Climates
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Title: Special Handling for Tarragon in Cold Climates

Steps:
1. Mulch after the first freeze if you live in an area with frequent freezing and thawing during winter.
2. This helps prevent the plant from being pushed up out of the ground...
Title: Special Handling for Tarragon in Cold Climates

Steps:
1. Mulch after the first freeze if you live in an area with frequent freezing and thawing during winter.
2. This helps prevent the plant from being pushed up out of the ground by a thaw.
3. Subdivide the plants every three to four years to maintain health and productivity.

Notes:
• Mulching also helps tarragon survive cold winters.
mulching, winter care, tarragon, subdivision
Winter Care for Rosemary and Sage

Procedure:

1. For rosemary:
- Pot a plant in fall and bring it indoors for winter use.
2. For sage:
- In northern areas, mulch around the plants to help them survive the winter.
winter care, herb overwintering, mulching
Cooking with Dried Herbs
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Cooking with Dried Herbs

When using dried herbs, first crush or chop the leaves to release the flavor and aroma. Use herbs singly or combine one particularly strong herb with several other milder ones. You can also make a bouquet garni by tying...
Cooking with Dried Herbs

When using dried herbs, first crush or chop the leaves to release the flavor and aroma. Use herbs singly or combine one particularly strong herb with several other milder ones. You can also make a bouquet garni by tying together or placing in a cheesecloth bag bunches of herbs such as celery leaves, parsley, onion, and thyme. Or, mix three or more herbs to make fine herbs. The most familiar combinations for fine herbs are chervil, chives, and parsley; and basil, sage, and savory.

You'll get the most from herbs in cooking if you add them at the right time. For example, when preparing stews or soups that must cook for several hours, add herbs during the last half hour of cooking time. The flavor and aroma of herbs can be lost if they cook too long. In foods that cook quickly, add the herbs immediately.

You'll get best results if you add herbs to the liquid portion of your recipe before mixing it with the rest of the ingredients. Moistening the herbs first with a little water, oil, or other suitable liquid and allowing them to stand for 10 minutes will bring out the flavor even more.

When substituting dried herbs for fresh in a recipe, use 1/4 amount specified. One teaspoon fresh herbs equals 1/4 _,_ teaspoon dried.
cooking with dried herbs,crushing herbs,bouquet garni,fine herbs,substitution ratio
Dry and Store Mint Leaves for Long-Term Use
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Strip the mint leaves from the stems using your hands or a knife.
2. Spread the leaves out in a single layer on a clean, dry surface in a warm, shady area.
3. Allow the leaves to air-dry completely, which may take...
Procedure:
1. Strip the mint leaves from the stems using your hands or a knife.
2. Spread the leaves out in a single layer on a clean, dry surface in a warm, shady area.
3. Allow the leaves to air-dry completely, which may take several days depending on humidity levels.
4. Store the dried mint leaves in an airtight jar or container in a cool, dark place for long-term use.
5. Label and date the container to ensure freshness and usability.
drying mint, storing herbs, herb preservation
Drying Herb Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Drying Herb Seeds

Herb seeds can be dried by the same method used to dry leaves. After drying, remove the outer covering from dried seeds. Just rub a few seeds at a time between the palms of your hands, and then shake them gently to let the...
Drying Herb Seeds

Herb seeds can be dried by the same method used to dry leaves. After drying, remove the outer covering from dried seeds. Just rub a few seeds at a time between the palms of your hands, and then shake them gently to let the outer seed covering fall away. Store seeds in labeled, airtight containers in a dark, cool, dry place.
drying herb seeds,seed covering removal,storage containers
Drying Herbs in a Conventional Oven
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Drying Herbs in a Conventional Oven

1. Remove the leaves from the stalks of the plant, and arrange them in a single, even layer on cookie sheets, racks, or trays. Use drying trays 11/2 to 2 inches smaller all around than the inside of your...
Drying Herbs in a Conventional Oven

1. Remove the leaves from the stalks of the plant, and arrange them in a single, even layer on cookie sheets, racks, or trays. Use drying trays 11/2 to 2 inches smaller all around than the inside of your oven, so air can circulate freely around them.

2. Set the trays in the oven, with at least _V/2_ inches between the layers of drying trays.

3. Dry the leaves in a 120°F oven or in a drying box until the leaves will crumble easily between your fingers. Prop the oven door open slightly for ventilation and to keep the oven temperature from rising too high. The herbs will dry in 2 to 4 hours.

4. Store the dried leaves whole in labeled, airtight containers, in a dark, cool, dry place.
conventional oven drying herbs,drying trays,temperature control,crumble test,storage containers
