Sprouting Vegetables: General Guidelines
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
General Sprouting Tips:

• Try sprouting just about any seed, grain, or legume for some of the most delicious, nutritious, and economical foods.
• Sprouts can be added to many dishes besides salads, soups, and sandwiches. They're delicious baked...
General Sprouting Tips:

• Try sprouting just about any seed, grain, or legume for some of the most delicious, nutritious, and economical foods.
• Sprouts can be added to many dishes besides salads, soups, and sandwiches. They're delicious baked into whole-grain breads or muffins, blended into juices, or added to granola or yogurt. You can sprinkle them on casseroles and on meat, fish, or fowl dishes of all kinds. You can even top sprouts with tomato sauce and eat them like spaghetti.
• Yields vary depending on the size of the seeds, temperature, and length of the sprouts when you harvest them.
• Small seeds—like chia — yield about eight times their original bulk in sprouts; large seeds — like corn — yield about three times their original bulk.
• Experiment with these wonder foods — you'll create some family favorites of your own.
sprouting, vegetables, gardening, food preservation, nutrition
Towel Sprouting Procedure
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Towel sprouting is best for larger grains and seeds.

### Steps:
1. Soak the seeds in a jar with 3 times as much water as you have seeds, for the time specified in the recipe. Rinse the seeds after soaking.
2. Arrange the rinsed seeds on a...
Towel sprouting is best for larger grains and seeds.

### Steps:
1. Soak the seeds in a jar with 3 times as much water as you have seeds, for the time specified in the recipe. Rinse the seeds after soaking.
2. Arrange the rinsed seeds on a damp towel. Cover with another damp towel and wrap in plastic wrap or place inside a plastic bag.
3. Place the sealed bag in a warm (70°F), dark location.
4. Dampen towels daily by misting them with water to maintain moisture.
5. If seeds haven’t sprouted after 2 days, replace the towels to prevent spoilage.
6. On about day four, remove the top towel and move the sprouts into sunlight so chlorophyll can develop and turn leaves green. Mist as needed.
7. Transfer sprouts from the towel to a strainer and rinse well to remove hulls if desired.
8. Use sprouts immediately in salads, sandwiches, or as per recipe instructions. To store, place in plastic bags and refrigerate.
9. Wash and dry all equipment for future use.

### Notes:
• Hulls can shorten storage life but add flavor.
• Ensure towels remain damp to prevent seeds from drying out.
towel sprouting, larger grains, seed germination, home gardening
Tray Sprouting Procedure
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Tray sprouting is a method best suited for seeds such as mung bean, chia, and lettuce.

### Steps:
1. Rinse the seeds in lukewarm water.
2. Place the seeds in a jar and add 3 times as much water as you have seeds. Cover with a plastic mesh...
Tray sprouting is a method best suited for seeds such as mung bean, chia, and lettuce.

### Steps:
1. Rinse the seeds in lukewarm water.
2. Place the seeds in a jar and add 3 times as much water as you have seeds. Cover with a plastic mesh lid, cheesecloth, or nylon net, then fasten with a rubber band or canning jar screw band. Soak for the time specified in the recipe.
3. At the end of soaking, rinse the seeds and spread them evenly in a tray. The tray should have a perforated bottom (e.g., wooden box with plastic mesh, nylon, or wire mesh bottom, or a perforated plastic tray).
4. Cover the tray with plastic wrap and then with newspaper or another light-blocking cover. Prop one end of the tray to allow drainage.
5. Place the tray in a warm (70°F), dark location.
6. Rinse and drain sprouts twice daily. In hot, dry weather, rinse 3–4 times per day. Rinse gently under a faucet, using a sprinkler attachment or by lowering the tray slightly into a sink of lukewarm water. Cover the tray after each rinsing.
7. On about day four, move the tray to sunlight so chlorophyll can develop and turn the leaves green. Continue rinsing and draining as before.
8. Transfer sprouts from the tray to a strainer and rinse well to remove hulls, if desired.
9. Use sprouts immediately in salads, sandwiches, or as per recipe instructions. To store, place in plastic bags and refrigerate.
10. Wash and dry all equipment for future use.

### Notes:
• Hulls can shorten storage life but add flavor.
• Ensure the tray is properly drained to prevent mold growth.
tray sprouting, mung bean, chia, lettuce, sprout growing, seed germination, home gardening

Food Preservation (18) -

Avoiding Scorching During Drying
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step 1: Be aware that each vegetable has a critical temperature beyond which a scorched taste will develop.

Step 2: Although scorching is unlikely at the start of the drying process, vegetables can easily scorch during the last couple of...
Step 1: Be aware that each vegetable has a critical temperature beyond which a scorched taste will develop.

Step 2: Although scorching is unlikely at the start of the drying process, vegetables can easily scorch during the last couple of hours.

Step 3: Slight scorching will ruin flavor and affect nutritive value. Be extra vigilant about maintaining proper temperature toward the end of the drying process.
scorching vegetables, critical temperature, food preservation
Conditioning Dried Vegetables for Even Moisture Distribution
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step 1: After the vegetables have cooled, place them into a large, deep crock, dishpan, jar, or coffee can.

Step 2: Store the container in a warm, dry room for a week to 10 days. Cover lightly with cheesecloth to keep out insects.

**Step...
Step 1: After the vegetables have cooled, place them into a large, deep crock, dishpan, jar, or coffee can.

Step 2: Store the container in a warm, dry room for a week to 10 days. Cover lightly with cheesecloth to keep out insects.

Step 3: Stir the dried pieces at least once a day so that moisture from any underdried pieces will be absorbed by overdried ones.

Note: This process ensures all food in a single batch is evenly dried.
conditioning vegetables, moisture distribution, food preservation
Construct a Homemade Drying Box
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Materials needed:

• A metal cookie sheet with sides or a jellyroll pan
• An empty cardboard box (same top dimensions as the cookie sheet)
• Heavy-duty or extra-wide aluminum foil
• A small can of black paint (spray or brush-on)
• A 60-watt...
Materials needed:

• A metal cookie sheet with sides or a jellyroll pan
• An empty cardboard box (same top dimensions as the cookie sheet)
• Heavy-duty or extra-wide aluminum foil
• A small can of black paint (spray or brush-on)
• A 60-watt light bulb and socket attached to a cord and plug

Steps:
1. Line the inside of the cardboard box with aluminum foil, shiny side up.
2. Cut a tiny notch in one corner of the box for the cord to run out.
3. Place the light fixture in the center of the box, resting it on a crumpled piece of foil.
4. Paint the bottom of the cookie sheet black and let it dry completely.
5. Prepare the vegetables according to your recipe.
6. Spread them in a single, even layer on the black-bottomed cookie sheet.
7. Place the sheet on top of the box.
8. Plug in the light bulb to preheat the box and begin drying until the food is done as specified by the recipe.

Notes:
• If you're drying more than one sheet of food, you'll need to make additional drying boxes.
• Do not prepare more food than you can dry at one time.
homemade drying box, food dryer, vegetable preservation, DIY food dryer
Drying Vegetables in an Oven
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Preheat oven: Set your gas or electric oven to 140°F for general vegetable drying, or 150°F for vegetables and 100°F for herbs if using a convection oven. Use an oven thermometer that can register as low as 100°F to maintain...
Procedure:

1. Preheat oven: Set your gas or electric oven to 140°F for general vegetable drying, or 150°F for vegetables and 100°F for herbs if using a convection oven. Use an oven thermometer that can register as low as 100°F to maintain the temperature.
2. Prop open oven door: Use a rolled newspaper, wood block, hot pad, or similar item to prop the oven door slightly open:
- Electric ovens: Open about one inch.
- Gas ovens: Open four to six inches.
- Convection ovens: Open one to one-and-a-half inches to allow moisture to escape.
3. Use a fan (for electric ovens only): Place an electric fan on 'low' in front of the oven door to improve air circulation. Do not use a fan for gas ovens with a pilot light, as it may blow out the pilot.
4. Monitor temperature: Insert the thermometer into the middle of the top tray of vegetables and check the temperature after 10 minutes. Adjust the oven door opening or temperature control if needed. Check every 30 minutes to ensure the temperature remains constant at the specified level.
5. Prepare drying trays: Use trays that are one to two inches smaller than the interior of your oven. If stacking multiple trays, place a tray with small holes in between for air circulation (for conventional ovens). For convection ovens, use dehydrating racks provided by the oven.
6. Set timer: Set the oven timer to 'stay on' if available. If not, set it for maximum time and reset as needed during the drying process.
7. Rotate racks and stir vegetables: Rotate the racks from front to back and top to bottom every 30 minutes. Stir the vegetables regularly to ensure even drying.

Notes:
• Drying times in a convection oven are usually shorter than in conventional ovens.
• Check foods for doneness at the lower end of the time range specified in recipes.
drying vegetables, oven drying, convection oven, gas oven, electric oven, food preservation
Ensuring Proper Ventilation During Drying
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step 1: Allow moisture from the vegetables to escape by evaporating into the surrounding air.

Step 2: If air is trapped around the food, it will quickly reach a saturation point and prevent further drying. Ensure proper ventilation in and...
Step 1: Allow moisture from the vegetables to escape by evaporating into the surrounding air.

Step 2: If air is trapped around the food, it will quickly reach a saturation point and prevent further drying. Ensure proper ventilation in and around your oven or dryer.

Step 3: Electric dryers and dehydrators automatically provide proper ventilation.

Step 4: When using an oven or homemade box dryer, leave the oven door slightly ajar and possibly use an electric fan to ensure good air circulation.

Step 5: Use cookie sheets or trays that are at least one to two inches smaller than the inside of your oven so air can circulate around the front, sides, and back of the trays.

Step 6: Ensure there is at least three inches of air space at the top of the oven.
ventilation drying, air circulation, food preservation
Final Treatment to Remove Insects or Eggs from Dried Vegetables
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step 1: After conditioning, give the vegetables a final treatment to eliminate any insects or insect eggs.

Option A (Freezing): Put the dried vegetables in the freezer for a few hours.

Option B (Heating): Heat them on a cookie sheet in...
Step 1: After conditioning, give the vegetables a final treatment to eliminate any insects or insect eggs.

Option A (Freezing): Put the dried vegetables in the freezer for a few hours.

Option B (Heating): Heat them on a cookie sheet in a closed oven at 175°F for 15 minutes.

Step 2: Ensure the food cools completely again before packaging.
insect removal, final treatment, food preservation
Maintaining Proper Drying Temperature
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step 1: Dry vegetables at low, even temperatures — just enough heat to dry the pieces without cooking them.

Step 2: For conventional ovens, use a temperature of 140°F. For convection ovens, use 180°F. Follow manufacturer's...
Step 1: Dry vegetables at low, even temperatures — just enough heat to dry the pieces without cooking them.

Step 2: For conventional ovens, use a temperature of 140°F. For convection ovens, use 180°F. Follow manufacturer's instructions for microwave ovens and other appliances.

Step 3: Ensure steady temperature with some air circulation. Electric dryers and dehydrators automatically maintain the right temperature.

Step 4: When using an oven or homemade box dryer, check your oven thermometer every half hour to ensure the temperature remains consistent at 140°F.

Step 5: Stir the vegetables every 30 minutes or so, shift trays from top to bottom, and rotate trays from front to back for even drying.

Note: Rapid drying is important, but too rapid drying in an oven can cause case hardening — the outer surface of the food hardens before internal moisture has evaporated. Prevent this by closely monitoring temperature and maintaining heat at 140°F.
drying temperature, oven drying, case hardening, food preservation
Proper Freezer Packaging of Vegetables
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Packing and sealing

The secret to successful freezer packaging is to seal the air out and keep it out. Immediately after blanching and cooling, pack vegetables loosely in proper containers. Plastic freezer bags and boxes or can-and-freeze jars...
Packing and sealing

The secret to successful freezer packaging is to seal the air out and keep it out. Immediately after blanching and cooling, pack vegetables loosely in proper containers. Plastic freezer bags and boxes or can-and-freeze jars are all excellent. Freezer containers must be airtight, moisture/vaporproof, odorless, tasteless, and greaseproof.

Head space. Since food expands as it freezes, you must allow room — or head space — for this expansion. Otherwise the lids will pop off, bags will burst, and you'll have wasted food, time, and money. Foods that are dry need no head space. Food that's packed in liquid or is mostly liquid needs 1/4 inch of head space for pints, 1/2 inch for quarts. If you pack foods in containers with narrow mouths, the food expands upward in the container even more, requiring 1/2 inch of head space for pints and 3/4 inches for quarts. We suggest you stick to wide-mouth containers. The recipes in this book give you head space needs for each particular food for wide-mouth containers only.

Sealing. How you seal food for the freezer is just as important as how you package it. After wiping the mouths of your freezer containers with a clean, damp cloth, seal rigid containers by following the manufacturer's instructions (if there are any), or by snapping, screwing, or fitting the lid tightly on the container. If the lid doesn't seem tight, seal it with freezer tape.

Seal bags or boilable pouches with a heat-sealing appliance; follow the instructions that come with the heat sealer. Or seal bags by pressing out the air, then twisting the bag close to the food. Fold the twisted section over and fasten it with a rubber band, pipe cleaner, or twist tie. To get air out of an odd-shaped bag, lower the filled bag into a sink full of water and let the water press the air out. Twist the bag top, lift it out, double the twisted area backward, and fasten.

Labeling. A good freezer label should tell what food is in the package, the amount of food or number of servings, and when it went into the freezer. Better yet, it should tell how the food was packed, and when, for example, "Sugar Pack Strawberries — June, 1976." You might want to include an "expiration" or "use-by" date. Frozen main dishes, sauces packed in boilable pouches, and other more complex items call for a label with description, number of servings, perhaps even heating and thawing instructions.
freezer packaging,head space,sealing vegetables,labeling freezer containers
Select and Prepare Basic Drying Equipment for Home Vegetable Drying
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Essential equipment:

• Scale: To weigh food before and after drying.
• Oven thermometer: That reads as low as 100°F to maintain proper temperature during drying.
• Sharp stainless steel knives: For thin-slicing, paring, or cutting...
Essential equipment:

• Scale: To weigh food before and after drying.
• Oven thermometer: That reads as low as 100°F to maintain proper temperature during drying.
• Sharp stainless steel knives: For thin-slicing, paring, or cutting vegetables without discoloration.
• Cutting board: Should be scrubbed thoroughly before and after use.
• Baking or cookie sheets: Used as drying trays. Sheets without raised edges are best for air circulation. They should be 1–2 inches smaller than the inside of your oven.
• Blancher: For pretreatment of most vegetables. Can be a ready-made unit, or made with a deep pot, cover, and colander/gasket. A rack or steamer basket is needed for steam blanching.
• Long, flexible spatula: To stir vegetable pieces during drying to ensure even results.
• Airtight storage containers: Must be moisture/vaporproof. Options include glass canning jars, coffee cans lined with plastic bags, freezer containers, refrigerator-ware, or double plastic bags sealed tightly with string, rubber bands, or twist ties.
• Electric fan: To circulate air in front of the oven if needed.
drying equipment, vegetable preservation, food drying setup, home gardening tools
Store and Preserve Corn
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. If storing corn before eating, wrap the entire ear (with husk) in damp paper towels.
2. Store in the refrigerator for four to eight days.
3. Corn can also be sprouted, frozen, canned, or dried for long-term preservation.
4. Refer...
Procedure:
1. If storing corn before eating, wrap the entire ear (with husk) in damp paper towels.
2. Store in the refrigerator for four to eight days.
3. Corn can also be sprouted, frozen, canned, or dried for long-term preservation.
4. Refer to Part 3 for detailed information on storing and preserving corn.
corn storage,damp paper towels,refrigeration,frozen corn,canning corn
Storing and Preserving Mustard Greens
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Store mustard greens in the refrigerator for up to one week.
2. Freeze, can, or dry excess mustard crop for long-term storage using recipes provided for preserving greens.
3. Sprout mustard seeds as an alternative...
Procedure:

1. Store mustard greens in the refrigerator for up to one week.
2. Freeze, can, or dry excess mustard crop for long-term storage using recipes provided for preserving greens.
3. Sprout mustard seeds as an alternative preservation method.
4. For detailed information on storing and preserving, refer to Part 3 of the source text.
mustard storage, freezing greens, canning mustard, drying preservation
Testing for Doneness in Dried Vegetables
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step 1: The times for drying vary considerably — from four hours to more than 12 — depending on the kind of vegetable, how thinly it's sliced, and how much food is being dried at one time.

Step 2: Use the drying time ranges provided in...
Step 1: The times for drying vary considerably — from four hours to more than 12 — depending on the kind of vegetable, how thinly it's sliced, and how much food is being dried at one time.

Step 2: Use the drying time ranges provided in recipes as a guide, but test sample pieces to ensure they are sufficiently dry.

Step 3: When you think the vegetables are dry, remove a few pieces from the tray and let them cool before testing — even perfectly dry food will feel soft and moist while still warm.

Step 4: Test for doneness as specified in each recipe. A general rule of thumb is that properly dried vegetables should be hard and brittle to the touch. Exceptions include mushrooms, sweet peppers, and squash, which will feel pliable and leathery when dry.

Optional Test (Hammer Test): If sufficiently dry, the vegetable pieces will shatter when struck with a hammer.
testing dried vegetables, doneness test, food preservation
Use an Electric Dryer or Dehydrator for Vegetable Drying
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Considerations:

• Electric dryers are lightweight metal boxes with drawer racks that can hold up to 14 pounds of fresh vegetables.
• These are ideal if you plan on drying large quantities of vegetables regularly, as they prevent tying up...
Considerations:

• Electric dryers are lightweight metal boxes with drawer racks that can hold up to 14 pounds of fresh vegetables.
• These are ideal if you plan on drying large quantities of vegetables regularly, as they prevent tying up your kitchen oven for extended periods.
• Electric dryers operate at lower temperatures than an electric oven and may take longer to complete the drying process.

Instructions:
1. Always follow the manufacturer's directions when using an electric dryer or dehydrator.
2. Prepare vegetables according to your recipe.
3. Place them in the dryer racks as specified by the manufacturer.
4. Set the temperature and time based on the type of vegetable being dried.
5. Monitor the drying process periodically to ensure even dehydration.
6. Once dry, remove the food and store it in airtight containers.
electric dryer, dehydrator, vegetable drying, home food preservation
Preparing Vegetables for Drying
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step 1: Spread the blanched and drained vegetable pieces in a single, even layer on the drying tray.

Step 2: Place the trays in the oven or electric dryer. Ensure there is at least one to two inches of space between the trays for proper air...
Step 1: Spread the blanched and drained vegetable pieces in a single, even layer on the drying tray.

Step 2: Place the trays in the oven or electric dryer. Ensure there is at least one to two inches of space between the trays for proper air circulation.

Note: Strong-smelling vegetables such as onions, cabbage, and carrots should be dried separately from other vegetables.
drying vegetables, food preservation, drying tray, air circulation
Store and Preserve Broad Beans Properly
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Unshelled broad beans can be stored in the refrigerator for up to one week.
2. Shelled beans can be frozen, canned, or dried for long-term storage.
3. Dried shelled broad beans should be kept in a cool, dry place for 10 to 12...
Procedure:

1. Unshelled broad beans can be stored in the refrigerator for up to one week.
2. Shelled beans can be frozen, canned, or dried for long-term storage.
3. Dried shelled broad beans should be kept in a cool, dry place for 10 to 12 months.

Notes:
• Detailed information on storing and preserving is available in Part 3 of the source material.
broad beans storage, freezing, canning, drying, long-term preservation
Storing and Preserving Sweet Potatoes
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for storing and preserving sweet potatoes:

1. Cure the tubers: Place them in crates in a dark, humid place at a temperature of 80° to 85°F for 10 days.
2. Store properly: After curing, store the sweet potatoes at 55° to 60°F...
Procedure for storing and preserving sweet potatoes:

1. Cure the tubers: Place them in crates in a dark, humid place at a temperature of 80° to 85°F for 10 days.
2. Store properly: After curing, store the sweet potatoes at 55° to 60°F in a moderately moist location for four to six months.
3. Alternative preservation methods: You can also freeze, can, or dry them for long-term storage.

Additional information: For detailed guidance on storing and preserving, refer to Part 3.
sweet potatoes, storage, curing, freezing, canning
Preparing and Serving Fresh Peas
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Cook freshly shelled peas quickly in a little water.
2. Serve with butter and chopped mint or add a sprig of mint during cooking.
3. Pair fresh peas with boiled new potatoes for a lamb roast accompaniment.
4. Toss cold, cooked peas...
Procedure:
1. Cook freshly shelled peas quickly in a little water.
2. Serve with butter and chopped mint or add a sprig of mint during cooking.
3. Pair fresh peas with boiled new potatoes for a lamb roast accompaniment.
4. Toss cold, cooked peas into a salad or add them to potato salad with diced cooked carrots for a Russian salad.
5. Simmer peas in butter with lettuce added at the end of cooking time.
6. Line the pot with lettuce leaves and cook peas briefly over low heat.
7. Add sautéed mushrooms or onions for a sophisticated vegetable dish.
8. Use edible-pod peas in stir-fry dishes to preserve their crisp texture and delicate flavor.
9. Eat them raw or use them lightly steamed as a side dish.
serving peas, cooking peas, pea recipes, vegetable dishes
Storing Fresh Shelling Peas
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Store unshelled fresh shelling peas in the refrigerator.
2. Keep them for up to one week.
3. Alternatively, you can sprout, freeze, can, or dry the peas.
4. Dried peas should be stored in a cool, dry place for 10 to 12...
Procedure:
1. Store unshelled fresh shelling peas in the refrigerator.
2. Keep them for up to one week.
3. Alternatively, you can sprout, freeze, can, or dry the peas.
4. Dried peas should be stored in a cool, dry place for 10 to 12 months.
Details:
• Edible-pod peas are often consumed raw and may not need storage.
• If storing edible-pod peas, place them in a plastic bag in the refrigerator for seven to 10 days.
• They also freeze well without losing much flavor.
storing peas, preserving vegetables, refrigerator storage, freezing peas, food preservation

1. After harvesting, dry the anise seeds thoroughly.
2. Store the dried seeds in airtight containers to preserve their flavor and quality.
3. Keep the containers in a cool, dark place to extend shelf life.

Notes:
• Anise...
Procedure:

1. After harvesting, dry the anise seeds thoroughly.
2. Store the dried seeds in airtight containers to preserve their flavor and quality.
3. Keep the containers in a cool, dark place to extend shelf life.

Notes:
• Anise seeds can be stored for months under these conditions.
• Detailed information on storing and preserving is provided in Part 3 of the source text.
anise seed storage, herb preservation techniques, storing herbs
Storing and Preserving Garlic Bulbs
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. After harvesting, allow the garlic bulbs to dry thoroughly.
2. Braid the tops of the plants together using twine.
3. Hang the braided garlic in a cool, dry location to continue drying.
4. Ensure that storage conditions remain cool...
Procedure:
1. After harvesting, allow the garlic bulbs to dry thoroughly.
2. Braid the tops of the plants together using twine.
3. Hang the braided garlic in a cool, dry location to continue drying.
4. Ensure that storage conditions remain cool and dry to prevent mold or rot.
Notes: This method is traditional in France and helps preserve the quality of garlic for extended periods.
garlic storage,braiding,drying,cool dry conditions,preserving

Unshelled lima beans can be stored in the refrigerator for up to one week.

Shelled lima beans freeze well and can also be canned or dried.

Dried shelled lima beans can be stored in a cool, dry place for **10 to 12...
Unshelled lima beans can be stored in the refrigerator for up to one week.

Shelled lima beans freeze well and can also be canned or dried.

Dried shelled lima beans can be stored in a cool, dry place for 10 to 12 months.

For more detailed information on storing and preserving, refer to Part III of the book.

2. Crumble the dried leaves for easier storage and use during winter months.
3. Store the crumbled, dry leaves in an airtight container in a cool, dark...

2. Crumble the dried leaves for easier storage and use during winter months.
3. Store the crumbled, dry leaves in an airtight container in a cool, dark place.

Storing and Preserving Winter Squashes
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Cure the winter squashes in a dark, humid place for 10 days at a temperature of 80° to 85°F.
2. After curing, store the squashes in a moderately dry, dark location with temperatures between 50° and 60°F for five to six months.
3....
Procedure:
1. Cure the winter squashes in a dark, humid place for 10 days at a temperature of 80° to 85°F.
2. After curing, store the squashes in a moderately dry, dark location with temperatures between 50° and 60°F for five to six months.
3. Alternatively, winter squashes can be frozen or dried for long-term preservation.
4. The seeds of winter squash can also be sprouted for future planting.

Notes:
• Detailed information on storing and preserving is provided in Part 3 of the source text.
storing,winter squash,preservation
Curing and Storing Potatoes for Long-Term Use
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Cure the potatoes in a dark, humid place at 60° to 65°F for 10 days.
2. After curing, store them in a cold, moderately moist place for 4 to 6 months.
3. Ensure that the storage area is dry enough to...
Procedure:

1. Cure the potatoes in a dark, humid place at 60° to 65°F for 10 days.
2. After curing, store them in a cold, moderately moist place for 4 to 6 months.
3. Ensure that the storage area is dry enough to prevent rotting but not so dry that the potatoes lose moisture.
4. Do not refrigerate the potatoes.
5. For long-term preservation, freeze or dry the potatoes as needed.

Details:
• Avoid getting the potatoes wet during storage to prevent rot.
• Freezing and drying are effective methods for preserving excess harvest.
potato storage, curing, preservation, freezing

2. For longer-term storage, you can freeze, can, or dry the pods.

• Detailed information on storing and preserving is given in Part 3...
Procedure:

2. For longer-term storage, you can freeze, can, or dry the pods.

okra storage, okra preservation, freezing okra, canning okra
Store and Preserve Tomatoes
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Storing and Preserving Tomatoes

To store and preserve tomatoes:

1. Refrigerate ripe tomatoes: Ripened tomatoes will keep up to one week in the refrigerator.
2. Freeze, can, or dry tomatoes: You can also freeze, can, or dry them whole,...
Storing and Preserving Tomatoes

To store and preserve tomatoes:

1. Refrigerate ripe tomatoes: Ripened tomatoes will keep up to one week in the refrigerator.
2. Freeze, can, or dry tomatoes: You can also freeze, can, or dry them whole, sliced, as juice, paste, relish, or pickles.
3. Store green tomatoes for ripening: Green tomatoes harvested before a frost can be held in a cool, moist place up to one month to ripen.

Note: For more detailed information on storing and preserving, refer to Part 3.
tomato,storage,preservation,canning,drying,freezing
Storing and Preserving Cauliflower
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Store unwashed cauliflower in a plastic bag or wrap it in plastic for up to one week in the refrigerator.
2. For longer storage, keep it in a cold, moist place for two to three weeks.

Notes:
• Avoid washing cauliflower before...
Procedure:
1. Store unwashed cauliflower in a plastic bag or wrap it in plastic for up to one week in the refrigerator.
2. For longer storage, keep it in a cold, moist place for two to three weeks.

Notes:
• Avoid washing cauliflower before storing to prevent excess moisture and spoilage.
• Ensure that the storage area is cool and dry to maintain quality.
cauliflower storage, preservation methods, refrigeration

Store muskmelons in the refrigerator for up to one week, or in a cool, moist place for two to three weeks if you have a large quantity. You can also freeze extras or make pickles with them. For more information on storing and preserving,...
Store muskmelons in the refrigerator for up to one week, or in a cool, moist place for two to three weeks if you have a large quantity. You can also freeze extras or make pickles with them. For more information on storing and preserving, refer to Part 3 of the book.
storing,muskmelons,preservation,refrigeration
Storing and Preserving Pumpkins
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure for Storing and Preserving Pumpkins:

1. Curing: Cure pumpkins in a dark, humid place at 80° to 85°F for 10 days.
2. Storage conditions: Store cured pumpkins in a dry place at 50° to 55°F for three to six months.
3. **Avoid...
Procedure for Storing and Preserving Pumpkins:

1. Curing: Cure pumpkins in a dark, humid place at 80° to 85°F for 10 days.
2. Storage conditions: Store cured pumpkins in a dry place at 50° to 55°F for three to six months.
3. Avoid refrigeration: Do not store pumpkins in the refrigerator.
4. Weight loss: Stored pumpkins may shrink up to 20% in weight but will still be suitable for pies and other uses.
5. Preservation methods:
- Dry or pickle pumpkin.
- Freeze or can the cooked pulp.
- Sprout pumpkin seeds for future planting.
pumpkin storage, food preservation, gardening techniques

3. Store caraway seeds in a sealed jar for months.
4. For detailed information on storage and...
Procedure:

3. Store caraway seeds in a sealed jar for months.
4. For detailed information on storage and preservation, refer to Part 3 of the source text.

3. Pickling or making relish is an option if the cucumbers are of a suitable variety for preservation.

Store and Preserve Kohlrabi

Procedure:

1. Store freshly harvested kohlrabi in a refrigerator for up to one week.
2. For longer storage, place it in a cold, moist place, where it can last one to two months.
3. Kohlrabi can also be frozen for extended...
Procedure:

1. Store freshly harvested kohlrabi in a refrigerator for up to one week.
2. For longer storage, place it in a cold, moist place, where it can last one to two months.
3. Kohlrabi can also be frozen for extended preservation.
4. Follow detailed instructions from Part 3 of the source text on specific methods for freezing and long-term storage.

Details:
• Proper storage ensures that kohlrabi remains fresh and usable for longer periods.
• Freezing is a good option if you plan to use it in cooking later.

2. Store sorrel leaves in the refrigerator for one to two weeks.
3. Alternatively, freeze or dry the leaves as herbs, though some flavor will be lost.

Details:
-...
Procedure:

1. Use sorrel leaves fresh for the best flavor.
2. Store sorrel leaves in the refrigerator for one to two weeks.
3. Alternatively, freeze or dry the leaves as herbs, though some flavor will be lost.

Details:
• Fresh sorrel is preferred for culinary use.
• Freezing and drying are suitable for long-term storage but may affect taste.
sorrel storage,refrigeration,drying,freezing
Storing and Preserving Brussels Sprouts
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
### Storage Methods

• If you have sprouts still on the stem in late fall:
- Remove all the leaves from the plant.
- Hang the plant in a cool, dry place for a late harvest.
- The plant can be kept up to one month in a cold, moist...
### Storage Methods

• If you have sprouts still on the stem in late fall:
- Remove all the leaves from the plant.
- Hang the plant in a cool, dry place for a late harvest.
- The plant can be kept up to one month in a cold, moist place.

### Refrigeration

• Sprouts will keep for about a week in the refrigerator.
• Remove loose or discolored outer leaves before storing them, but don't wash them until you're ready to use them.

### Other Preservation Methods

• You can also freeze or dry sprouts.

### Additional Resources

• For detailed information on storing and preserving, refer to the relevant sections in Parts of the book.
storing, preserving, brussels sprouts, freezing, drying

2. Place them in the refrigerator to store for one to two weeks.
3. Alternatively, sprout radish seeds if you wish to grow new plants from seed.

Notes:
• For long-term preservation,...
Procedure:

1. Harvest radishes when ready.
2. Place them in the refrigerator to store for one to two weeks.
3. Alternatively, sprout radish seeds if you wish to grow new plants from seed.

Notes:
• For long-term preservation, refer to Part 3 of the book for detailed storage methods.
radish,storage,preserving

Planting (18) -

Plant Collards
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Choose fertile, well-drained soil with a pH between 6.5 and 7.5.
2. Plant collard transplants four to six weeks before the average date of last frost, except in areas with long cool seasons.
3. If planting seeds, set them an inch...
Procedure:
1. Choose fertile, well-drained soil with a pH between 6.5 and 7.5.
2. Plant collard transplants four to six weeks before the average date of last frost, except in areas with long cool seasons.
3. If planting seeds, set them an inch deep and space them three inches apart.
4. Thin seedlings when they are big enough to lift by the true leaves.
5. Transplant thinned seedlings if desired.
6. For transplants, use four- to six-week-old plants with four or five true leaves.
7. If transplants are leggy or have crooked stems, plant them deeply (up to the first leaves).
8. Plant seedlings 12 inches apart in rows that are 18 to 24 inches apart.
collards, planting, gardening
Planting Chayote
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
### Planting Chayote

• You plant the whole vegetable so you can use the chayote you buy in the local Spanish _mercado._
• The chayote is a tender perennial vine that grows from a tuber and can climb to 30 feet.
• It's a member of the gourd...
### Planting Chayote

• You plant the whole vegetable so you can use the chayote you buy in the local Spanish _mercado._
• The chayote is a tender perennial vine that grows from a tuber and can climb to 30 feet.
• It's a member of the gourd family, and it has hairy leaves the size and shape of maple leaves; male and female flowers are borne on the same vine.
• You plant the whole fruit with the fat side placed at an angle half way down in the soil so that the stem area is level with the soil surface.
• Before planting, work a complete, well-balanced fertilizer into the soil at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet or 10 pounds per 1,000 square feet.
• The chayote likes well-drained soil with a high content of organic matter and will tolerate partial shade.
• Space the plants 24 to 30 inches apart, with four or five feet between rows.
• You don't need to provide a support for the vines unless you want to save space.
chayote,planting,gardening,vegetables
Planting Marjoram Seeds or Transplants
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Determine the average date of your last frost in your area.
2. Sow marjoram seeds a quarter inch deep in rows spaced 18 to 24 inches apart.
3. Alternatively, plant transplants that are two to three inches tall, spacing them about...
Procedure:
1. Determine the average date of your last frost in your area.
2. Sow marjoram seeds a quarter inch deep in rows spaced 18 to 24 inches apart.
3. Alternatively, plant transplants that are two to three inches tall, spacing them about six inches apart.
4. Thin seedlings to approximately six inches apart when they have grown sturdily.
5. If the weather warms quickly, apply mulch around transplants to protect their roots from excessive heat until they acclimate.
Notes: Marjoram thrives in poor soil with good drainage and does not require fertilization before planting.
marjoram planting,last frost date,seeding,transplants,soil drainage
Prepare Soil for Fennel Planting
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Choose well-drained soil high in organic matter.
2. Apply a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of:
- One pound per 100 square feet, or
- Ten pounds per 1,000 square feet.
3. Plant seeds **a quarter of...
Procedure:
1. Choose well-drained soil high in organic matter.
2. Apply a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of:
- One pound per 100 square feet, or
- Ten pounds per 1,000 square feet.
3. Plant seeds a quarter of an inch deep, in rows spaced two to three feet apart, in full sun.
4. Thin seedlings to stand 12 inches apart when they are growing strongly.

Notes:
• Fennel is a member of the parsley family and can tolerate both heat and cold.
• Detailed information on fertilizing is provided in "Spadework: The Essential Soil" in Parti.
fennel, soil preparation, fertilizer application, planting depth, spacing
Starting Seeds with Coir
Standard Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Steps to Start Seeds Using Coir:

1. Use moist coir: Ensure the coir is damp but not waterlogged.
2. Maintain moisture: Use a fine spray to keep the top surface of the coir moist as it dries out.
3. Provide sufficient light:...
Steps to Start Seeds Using Coir:

1. Use moist coir: Ensure the coir is damp but not waterlogged.
2. Maintain moisture: Use a fine spray to keep the top surface of the coir moist as it dries out.
3. Provide sufficient light: Seedlings require adequate light for healthy growth.

Transplanting Seeds:
• Once seedlings reach 3” or 4” in height and have developed true leaves, transplant them into a container with fertilizer or add fertilizer to the existing container.

Note: Coir retains moisture well, so it is not necessary to place the seeds in a pan of water as some methods suggest.
starting seeds, coir, moisture, light, transplant, true leaves, fertilizer
Pepper Varieties and Recommendations
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Variety Types:
• Bell (sweet) peppers
• Hot peppers (sometimes called chili peppers)

Bell Peppers:
• Most are sweet, but some are hot.
• Usually harvested when green, but can turn red or yellow if left on the plant.

Hot Peppers:
-...
Variety Types:
• Bell (sweet) peppers
• Hot peppers (sometimes called chili peppers)

Bell Peppers:
• Most are sweet, but some are hot.
• Usually harvested when green, but can turn red or yellow if left on the plant.

Hot Peppers:
• Intensely flavored with over 100 varieties available.
• Ask your Cooperative Extension Service for specific recommendations for your area.

Reliable Varieties (with TM indicating resistance to tobacco mosaic disease):
• Bell Boy (TM, 75)
peppers,varieties,bell peppers,hot peppers,tobacco mosaic disease
Planting Shallots from Cloves
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Plant shallot cloves four to six weeks before your average date of last frost.
2. Place the cloves six to eight inches apart in rows that are 12 inches apart.
3. Set the cloves so that their tops are even with the soil surface,...
Procedure:

1. Plant shallot cloves four to six weeks before your average date of last frost.
2. Place the cloves six to eight inches apart in rows that are 12 inches apart.
3. Set the cloves so that their tops are even with the soil surface, but not deeper than this.
4. Keep the area around the plants carefully cultivated to prevent competition from weeds.

Notes:
• Shallots are usually grown from cloves rather than seeds.
• Shallow root systems require careful weeding and cultivation.
shallot planting,gardening,cloves,vegetables
Preparing Soil for Muskmelon Planting
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained soil high in organic matter.
2. Prepare the soil by digging in a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of:
- One pound per 100 square feet, or
- **Ten...
Procedure:
1. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained soil high in organic matter.
2. Prepare the soil by digging in a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of:
- One pound per 100 square feet, or
- Ten pounds per 1,000 square feet.
3. Plant muskmelons in inverted hills spaced four to six feet apart.
4. If planting from seed, plant six to eight seeds in each hill.
5. When the seedlings have developed three or four true leaves, thin them to leave the strongest two or three seedlings per hill.
6. Use scissors to cut thinned seedlings at soil level to avoid damaging the root systems of surviving plants.
7. In areas with cucumber beetles, other insects, or weather issues, delay final selection until conditions improve.
8. If using transplants, place two or three transplants per hill.
muskmelon,soil preparation,fertilizer,hilling,transplants
Selecting Muskmelon Varieties Based on Climate
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Consult your garden center or local extension office to determine which muskmelon varieties grow best in your area.
2. In cool areas, select small-fruited varieties that are better suited for shorter growing seasons.
3....
Procedure:
1. Consult your garden center or local extension office to determine which muskmelon varieties grow best in your area.
2. In cool areas, select small-fruited varieties that are better suited for shorter growing seasons.
3. In warmer areas, choose large-fruited varieties that require longer growing periods.
4. If you live in a cool climate, use transplants grown in individual, plantable containers at least four inches in diameter to avoid disturbing the root systems when planting.
5. Plant transplants two to three weeks after your average date of last frost, once the ground is thoroughly warm.
muskmelon,varieties,climate selection,transplants,warm weather

Growing Dill: Varieties and Description
