• Common name: dill
• Botanical name: Anethum graveolens
• Origin: Southeast Europe

Varieties:

• Few varieties are available; grow the variety available in your area.
• Bouquet is a dwarf...
Dill Overview:

• Common name: dill
• Botanical name: Anethum graveolens
• Origin: Southeast Europe

Varieties:

• Few varieties are available; grow the variety available in your area.
• Bouquet is a dwarf variety.

Description:

• Dill, a member of the parsley family, is a biennial grown as an annual and grows two to four feet tall.
• It has finely cut leaves and small yellow flowers growing in a flat-topped cluster; it has a delicate feathery look and makes a good background for flowers or vegetables.
dill, varieties, description, parsley family
Plant Sorrel Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Plant sorrel seeds two to three weeks before the average date of last frost.
2. Sow the seeds half an inch deep in rows spaced 18 to 24 inches apart.
3. When the plants are six to eight weeks old, thin them to **12...
Procedure:

1. Plant sorrel seeds two to three weeks before the average date of last frost.
2. Sow the seeds half an inch deep in rows spaced 18 to 24 inches apart.
3. When the plants are six to eight weeks old, thin them to 12 to 18 inches apart.

Details:
• Ensure consistent soil moisture during germination.
• Thinning helps prevent overcrowding and promotes healthy growth.
sorrel planting,seeding,spacing
Planting Coriander Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to Plant Coriander Seeds:

1. Choose a location with at least 100 days of growing season and no hard frost.
2. Prepare the soil by digging in a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet.
3. Plant...
Steps to Plant Coriander Seeds:

1. Choose a location with at least 100 days of growing season and no hard frost.
2. Prepare the soil by digging in a complete, well-balanced fertilizer at the rate of one pound per 100 square feet.
3. Plant coriander seeds in early spring, two to three weeks after the average date of last frost.
4. Plant the seeds a quarter inch deep in rows that are eight to 12 inches apart.
5. Thin the plants so they stand 12 inches apart when the seedlings are growing strongly.
coriander, planting, seeds, gardening
Planting Corn in the Garden
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps for Planting Corn:
1. Soil preparation:
- Use well-worked, fertile soil with good drainage.
- Ensure full sun exposure.
2. Fertilization:
- Apply a third of a pound of complete, well-balanced fertilizer on each side of a...
Steps for Planting Corn:
1. Soil preparation:
- Use well-worked, fertile soil with good drainage.
- Ensure full sun exposure.
2. Fertilization:
- Apply a third of a pound of complete, well-balanced fertilizer on each side of a 10-foot row.
- Place the fertilizer an inch below and two inches away from where you plan to put the seed.
3. Planting timing:
- Plant when soil temperature reaches 60°F.
4. Spacing and planting technique:
- Plant seeds two to four inches apart in rows or inverted hills.
- Use short rows in a block rather than one long row for better pollination.
- For continuous supply, plant a dozen seeds of the same variety every two weeks (or when the previous planting shows three leaves), or plant early, midseason, and late varieties at the same time.
5. Thinning:
- When corn is about six inches tall:
- Thin short varieties to two feet apart
- Thin tall varieties to three feet apart
corn, planting, soil preparation, fertilization, spacing
Planting Dill: Location and Timing
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Where and When to Grow Dill:

• Dill can be grown pretty much anywhere and can withstand heat or cold.
• Grow it from seed sown in the spring or fall.
• Once established, dill will seed itself and return year after year.

**Soil...
Where and When to Grow Dill:

• Dill can be grown pretty much anywhere and can withstand heat or cold.
• Grow it from seed sown in the spring or fall.
• Once established, dill will seed itself and return year after year.

Soil Requirements:

• Poor, sandy soil is an advantage when growing dill — the herb will have a stronger flavor — but the soil must drain well.
• Dill will tolerate partial shade; in light shade the plants won't get as bushy as in full sun, so they can be closer together.

Planting Instructions:

1. Plant the seeds two or three weeks before your average date of last frost in rows two to three feet apart; they germinate quickly.
2. When the seedlings are growing well, thin them to 12 inches apart.
3. You can also thin dill to form a clump or mass rather than a row.
4. Make sure you know where you want the plants, because dill has a taproot and is not easy to transplant.
5. Dill is short-lived, so make successive sowings to give you a continuous crop.
dill, planting, soil, timing, spacing
Planting Fennel Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained soil that is high in organic matter.
2. Sow fennel seeds two to three weeks before your average date of last frost.
3. Plant the seeds in rows spaced two to three feet apart.
4....
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained soil that is high in organic matter.
2. Sow fennel seeds two to three weeks before your average date of last frost.
3. Plant the seeds in rows spaced two to three feet apart.
4. Once seedlings are growing strongly, thin them out so they stand 12 inches apart.

Notes:
• Fennel is difficult to transplant due to its taproot; it's best to direct sow seeds.
• Ensure the soil is loose and well-aerated for optimal germination.
fennel, planting, seeds, gardening, herbs
Planting Garlic Cloves
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Select plump garlic cloves for planting; use the less plump ones for cooking.
2. Plant cloves four to six weeks before your average date of last frost.
3. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained, organic-rich soil.
4....
Procedure:

1. Select plump garlic cloves for planting; use the less plump ones for cooking.
2. Plant cloves four to six weeks before your average date of last frost.
3. Choose a location with full sun and well-drained, organic-rich soil.
4. Plant cloves an inch or two deep, spaced four to six inches apart in rows about a foot apart.

Notes:
• Garlic is best planted in the spring in the North; fall plantings are suitable in the South.
• Avoid fertilizing the soil as it can reduce garlic flavor.
garlic, planting, cloves, gardening
Selecting Corn Varieties for Planting
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Choosing the Right Corn Variety:
• A large number of varieties are available.
• Consult your local extension service for recommendations based on your area's climate and growing conditions.
• Some recommended varieties include:
- **Polar Vee...
Choosing the Right Corn Variety:
• A large number of varieties are available.
• Consult your local extension service for recommendations based on your area's climate and growing conditions.
• Some recommended varieties include:
- Polar Vee (55 days)
- Sugar and Gold (60 days, white and yellow kernels)
- Earliking (66 days)
- Butter and Sugar (78 days, white and yellow kernels)
- Golden Cross Bantam (84 days)
- Aristogold Bantam Evergreen (90 days) for late crops
- Silver Queen (92 days) for late crops
corn, varieties, planting, growing conditions

Plant Propagation (17) -

Chinese Mode of Propagating Fruit Trees Using Ring Barking and Water Suspension Method
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Chinese Mode of Propagating Fruit Trees Using Ring Barking and Water Suspension Method:

1. In the spring, strip a ring of bark about an inch in width from a bearing branch.
2. Surround the area where the bark was stripped with a...
Procedure for Chinese Mode of Propagating Fruit Trees Using Ring Barking and Water Suspension Method:

1. In the spring, strip a ring of bark about an inch in width from a bearing branch.
2. Surround the area where the bark was stripped with a ball of fat earth or loam, secured to the branch using matting.
3. Suspend a pot or horn containing water above the ball of earth, ensuring it has a small hole at the bottom just large enough for water to drip slowly into the soil.
4. This keeps the earth constantly moist and encourages new roots to form in the area where the bark was stripped.
5. In the fall, after the leaves have fallen, cut off the branch below the ball of earth and plant it in the ground with all the surrounding soil still attached.
6. The following year, the planted branch will bear fruit.

Notes: This method is effective not only for fruit trees but also for other plants. It was successfully practiced by the editor during his time in China and later implemented in this country.
ring barking propagation, Chinese tree propagation, water suspension method
Chinese Mode of Propagating Plants Using Tin Cup Method
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Chinese Mode of Propagating Plants Using Tin Cup Method:

1. In the spring, just before the sap begins to rise, prepare a tin cup with a round hole punched in the bottom slightly larger than the stem of the branch you intend to...
Procedure for Chinese Mode of Propagating Plants Using Tin Cup Method:

1. In the spring, just before the sap begins to rise, prepare a tin cup with a round hole punched in the bottom slightly larger than the stem of the branch you intend to use.
2. Make a slit from the edge down one side and along the bottom to the central aperture so that the sides can be separated without damaging the branch.
3. Insert the branch into the cup, close it up securely, and fill it with loam mixed with chopped moss.
4. Suspend another tin cup or gourd (pierced with a small hole) above the branch to keep it filled with water.
5. In the fall, after the leaves have fallen, cut off the branch below the cup and plant it in the ground along with all the soil that has adhered to its roots.
6. The following year, the planted branch will bear fruit.

Notes: This method is effective for propagating a wide variety of plants and was successfully used by the editor during his time in China.
tin cup propagation, Chinese plant propagation, spring planting technique
Choosing Cuttings for Propagation
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To choose Cuttings:

• In respect to the choice of cuttings, those branches of trees and shrubs which are thrown out nearest the ground, especially such as recline, or nearly so, on the earth's surface, have always the most tendency to produce...
To choose Cuttings:

• In respect to the choice of cuttings, those branches of trees and shrubs which are thrown out nearest the ground, especially such as recline, or nearly so, on the earth's surface, have always the most tendency to produce roots. Even the brunches of resinous trees, which are extremely difficult to propagate by cuttings, when reclining on the ground, if accidentally or otherwise covered with earth in any part, will there throw out roots, and the extremity of the lateral shoot will assume the character of a main stem, as may be sometimes seen in the larch, spruce and silver fir.

• The choice of cuttings is to be made from the side shoots of plants rather than from their summits or main stems. The strength and health of side shoots being equal, those nearest the ground should be preferred.

• The proper time for taking cuttings from the mother plant is when the sap is in full motion, in order that, in returning by the bask, it may form a callus or protruding ring of granular substance between the bark and wood, whence the roots proceed. As this callus or ring of spongy matter is generally best formed in ripened wood, the cutting, when taken from the mother plant, should contain a part of the former year, or in plants which grow twice a year, of the wood of the former growth, or in the case of plants which are continually growing, as most evergreen exotics, such wood as has begun to ripen or assume a brownish color.

• This is the true principle of the choice of cuttings as to time; but there are many sorts of trees, as willow, elder etc., the cuttings of which will grow almost at any season, and especially if removed from the mother plant in winter, when the sap is at rest.

• These ought always to be cut across, with the smoothest and soundest section possible at an eye or joint. And as buds are in a more advanced state in wood somewhat ripened or fully formed than in forming wood, this section ought to be made in the wood of the growth of the preceding season; or as it were in the point between the two growths.

• It is a common practice to cut off the whole or a part of the leaves of cuttings, which is always attended with bad effects in evergreens, in which the leaves may be said to supply nourishment to the cutting till it can sustain itself. This is very obvious in the case of striking from buds, which, without a leaf attached, speedily rot and die.

• Leaves alone will even strike root, and form plants in some instances, and the same may be stated of certain flowers and fruits.
cutting selection, propagation techniques, plant care
Choosing Scions for Grafting
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To choose Scions:

Scions are those shoots which, united with the stock, form the graft. They should be gathered several weeks before the season for grafting arrives. It is desirable that the sap of the stock should be in brisk motion at the...
To choose Scions:

Scions are those shoots which, united with the stock, form the graft. They should be gathered several weeks before the season for grafting arrives. It is desirable that the sap of the stock should be in brisk motion at the time of grafting; but by this time the buds of the scion, if left on the parent tree, would be equally advanced, whereas the scions, being gathered early, the buds are kept buck, and ready only to swell out when placed on the stock.

• Scions of pears, plums and cherries are collected in the end of January or beginning of February. They are kept at full length sunk in dry earth, and out of the reach of frost till wanted, which is sometimes from the middle of February to the middle of March.
• Scions of apples are collected any time in February, and put in from the middle to the end of March.
• In July grafting, the scions are used as gathered.
scion selection, grafting procedure, plant propagation
Engrafting Coffee-Trees Using Triangular Incision Method
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Engrafting Coffee-Trees Using Triangular Incision Method:

1. During the rainy season, plant young coffee plants (12–18 inches high) raised by seed in small hampers.
2. Place these plants in the shade until they recover fully.
3....
Procedure for Engrafting Coffee-Trees Using Triangular Incision Method:

1. During the rainy season, plant young coffee plants (12–18 inches high) raised by seed in small hampers.
2. Place these plants in the shade until they recover fully.
3. Move the plants into the hampers to the foot of selected mother coffee trees, which should be healthy and productive.
4. Cut the mother plants down to within 3–4 inches of the ground to encourage new growth near their roots.
5. When the shoots from the mother plant are about a foot or fifteen inches long, perform the grafting procedure:
- Step 1: Draw together the stem of the plant in the hamper and one of the branches of the mother plant.
- Step 2: Remove 3–8 inches of the top of the plant in the hamper.
- Step 3: Make a triangular incision on the cut portion of the plant in the hamper.
- Step 4: Make a similar triangular incision on the branch of the mother plant.
- Step 5: Bring the two wounds together and fasten them securely with string or twine.
- Step 6: Cover the graft site with a mixture of clayey earth and cow dung to protect it.
- Step 7: Use a prop to keep the branch upright until the graft is secure.
6. Once the grafted parts are firmly knit together, cut the branch away from the mother plant, completing the engrafting process.
7. After one or two years in the nursery, transplant the grafted trees to their designated plantation.

Notes: This method is especially useful for fruit and spice trees that do not propagate well from seeds.
coffee tree grafting, triangular incision grafting, plant propagation
Inserting Cuttings for Rooting
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To insert Cuttings:

• Cuttings, if inserted in a mere mass of earth will hardly throw out roots, while, if inserted at the sides of the pots so as to touch the pot in their whole length, they seldom fail to become rooted plants.

• The art...
To insert Cuttings:

• Cuttings, if inserted in a mere mass of earth will hardly throw out roots, while, if inserted at the sides of the pots so as to touch the pot in their whole length, they seldom fail to become rooted plants.

• The art is, to place them to touch the bottom of the pot; they are then to be plunged in a bark or hot-bed and kept moist.
cutting insertion, rooting techniques
Piping as a Method of Propagation
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Piping:

This is a mode of propagation by cuttings, and is adopted with plants having jointed tubular stems, as the dianthus tribe, and several of the grasses and the arundines may be propagated in this manner.

• When the shoot has nearly done...
Piping:

This is a mode of propagation by cuttings, and is adopted with plants having jointed tubular stems, as the dianthus tribe, and several of the grasses and the arundines may be propagated in this manner.

• When the shoot has nearly done growing, its extremity is to be separated at a part of the stem where it is nearly indurated or ripened. This operation is effected by holding the root end between the finger and thumb of one hand, below a pair of leaves, and with the other pulling the top part above the pair of leaves, so as to separate it from the root part of the stem at the socket, formed by the axillae of the leaves leaving the stem to remain with a tubular termination.

• These pipings are inserted without any further preparation in finely sifted earth to the depth of the first joint or pipe.
piping propagation, plant cutting techniques
Managing Cuttings for Successful Propagation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Planting Depth:
- No cutting should be planted too deep.
- Larger cuttings should be inserted deeper than smaller ones.
- For evergreens, ensure the leaves do not touch the soil to prevent dampness or rotting.
-...
Procedure:

1. Planting Depth:
- No cutting should be planted too deep.
- Larger cuttings should be inserted deeper than smaller ones.
- For evergreens, ensure the leaves do not touch the soil to prevent dampness or rotting.
- For tubular-stalked plants, insert both ends into the soil for greater success and to produce two plants.

2. Environmental Control:
- Avoid extremes of light, air, water, heat, or cold as they are injurious.
- Use a hand glass or bell-glass over cuttings to maintain uniform stillness and moisture in the atmosphere.
- Immersing the pot in earth helps preserve steady moisture at the roots.
- Shade cuttings placed outdoors by situating them in shady areas to prevent excess light exposure.

3. Heat Regulation:
- Use double or single coverings of glass, mats, or both to regulate heat.
- A hand glass over a bell-glass preserves constant heat in shaded conditions.

4. Heat Requirements:
- The required heat level is determined by the mother plant's needs.
- Most erica, dahlia, and geranium species require more heat than their greenhouse-grown counterparts.
- Myrtle and camellia plants require less heat.
- In general, a lesser portion of heat and other conditions suitable for plums in their rooted state is the safest approach.
cuttings, propagation, planting depth, heat regulation, bell-glass, evergreens, tubular-stalked plants
Perform Crown Grafting on Thick Stocks or Branches
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Crown grafting is a method used for thick stocks, shortened branches, or headed-down trees. It involves inserting the scion between the bark and wood of the stock.

### Steps:
1. Cut or saw off the head of the stock or branch horizontally or...
Crown grafting is a method used for thick stocks, shortened branches, or headed-down trees. It involves inserting the scion between the bark and wood of the stock.

### Steps:
1. Cut or saw off the head of the stock or branch horizontally or level, then pare the top smooth.
2. Prepare the scions: Cut one side of each flat and somewhat sloping, forming a sort of shoulder at the top of the slope, to rest upon the crown of the stock.
3. Raise the rind of the stock with an ivory wedge (from the handle of the budding knife) so as to admit the scion between that and the wood two inches down.
4. Insert the scion: Place it with the cut side next to the wood, thrusting it down far enough for the shoulder to rest upon the top of the stock.
5. Repeat this process to insert three, four, five or more scions in one large stock or branch.

### Additional Notes:
• This method is best performed later than other grafting methods because the motion of the sap makes it easier to separate the bark and wood.
• A potential disadvantage in exposed situations is that the ingrafted shoots may be blown out of the stock by violent winds for two or three years. The remedy is to tie long rods to the body of the stock or branch, and tie up each scion and its shoots to one of the rods.
crown grafting, grafting techniques, plant propagation, tree grafting, horticulture
Perform Root Grafting in Nurseries for Tree Propagation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Root grafting is performed on parts of the roots of removed trees when proper stocks are scarce.

### Steps:
1. Select a root from a tree of the same genus, preferably one well furnished with fibres.
2. Place a scion on the root and...
Root grafting is performed on parts of the roots of removed trees when proper stocks are scarce.

### Steps:
1. Select a root from a tree of the same genus, preferably one well furnished with fibres.
2. Place a scion on the root and secure it using any of the ordinary methods for small stocks.
3. Plant the grafted unit deeply, covering the ball of clay and leaving only a few eyes of the scion above ground.

### Post-Grafting Care:
• After one month, observe the progress of the buds to determine if the scion has united with the stock.
• It is not safe to remove the clay for three months or more until the graft is completely cicatrized (healed).
• The clay may generally be taken off in July or August and at the same time, the ligatures should be loosened where the scion seems to require more room to expand.
• A few weeks after this, when the parts have been partially exposed to air and there is no danger of the scion being blown off by winds, the whole of the ligatures may be removed.
root grafting, nursery propagation, tree root grafting techniques
Perform Saddle Grafting for Standard Trees or Ornamental Plants
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Saddle grafting is performed by cutting the top of the stock into a wedge-like form and splitting up the end of the scion, thinning off each half to a tongue-shape.

### Steps:
1. Cut the top of the stock into a wedge-like shape.
2. **Split...
Saddle grafting is performed by cutting the top of the stock into a wedge-like form and splitting up the end of the scion, thinning off each half to a tongue-shape.

### Steps:
1. Cut the top of the stock into a wedge-like shape.
2. Split the end of the scion and thin each half to a tongue-shape.
3. Place the scion on the wedge, allowing it to be embraced by the wedge on each side.
4. Join the inner barks on one side of the stock, similar to cleft grafting.

### Additional Notes:
• This method is particularly strong and handsome for standard trees when grafted at the standard height.
• It is also desirable for orange-trees and rosestandards as it covers a part of the stock, which would otherwise remain exposed as a black sear.
• The stocks used should not be much larger than the scions or two scions may be inserted.
saddle grafting, standard tree grafting, ornamental plant propagation
Perform Shoulder or Chink Grafting for Ornamental Trees
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Shoulder or chink grafting is performed with a shoulder and sometimes also with a stay at the bottom of the slope.

### Steps:
1. Cut the stock and scion to form a shoulder, ensuring that the cut sides match properly.
2. **Insert the scion...
Shoulder or chink grafting is performed with a shoulder and sometimes also with a stay at the bottom of the slope.

### Steps:
1. Cut the stock and scion to form a shoulder, ensuring that the cut sides match properly.
2. Insert the scion into the stock, aligning it so that the cuts fit together seamlessly.
3. Secure with binding material as needed.

### Additional Notes:
• This method is primarily used for ornamental trees where the scion and stock are of the same size.
shoulder grafting, chink grafting, ornamental tree propagation
Perform Side Grafting on Wall Trees or for Fruit Variety
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Side grafting is similar to whip grafting but performed on the side of the stock without bending it down. It is used on wall trees to fill vacancies and sometimes to have a variety of fruits on the same tree.

### Steps:
1. **Identify areas on...
Side grafting is similar to whip grafting but performed on the side of the stock without bending it down. It is used on wall trees to fill vacancies and sometimes to have a variety of fruits on the same tree.

### Steps:
1. Identify areas on the branches where wood is missing or needed to complete the head or any part of the tree.
2. Slope off the bark and a little of the wood at these points.
3. Cut the lower end of the scions to fit the prepared area as closely as possible.
4. Join the scion to the branch, ensuring a proper fit.
5. Secure with bass (a type of twine or binding material) and cover them with clay.

### Additional Notes:
• This method is particularly useful for wall trees and when creating fruit variety on a single tree.
side grafting, wall tree grafting, fruit propagation, plant grafting techniques
Propagating Biennial and Perennial Plants by Division
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Propagating Biennial and Perennial Plants by Division:

• Timing: Divide the roots of biennial or perennial plants early in spring or after the summer bloom is over.
• Plant Selection: Examples include Lily of the...
Procedure for Propagating Biennial and Perennial Plants by Division:

• Timing: Divide the roots of biennial or perennial plants early in spring or after the summer bloom is over.
• Plant Selection: Examples include Lily of the Valley, Larkspur (Delphinium Formosum), Phlox (Phlox Drummondii), Canterbury Bell, Foxglove, Hemerocallis, Iris or Flag, Everlasting Pea, Spiraea, Sweet William, Alyssum.
• Care After Division: Ensure proper watering and soil conditions after division to promote healthy growth.

Note: These plants require little care beyond loosening the earth around them in spring.
biennial, perennial, plant propagation, gardening
Propagating Verbenas by Seed
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Propagating Verbenas from Seed:

• Seed Selection: Use seeds of verbenas to produce new varieties.
• Color Variability: Seeds can yield all colors except blue and a handsome yellow.
• Growing Conditions: Provide...
Procedure for Propagating Verbenas from Seed:

• Seed Selection: Use seeds of verbenas to produce new varieties.
• Color Variability: Seeds can yield all colors except blue and a handsome yellow.
• Growing Conditions: Provide well-drained soil with moderate watering, as described in the soil preparation procedure.

Note: This method is an alternative to propagation by cuttings.
verbenas, seed propagation, gardening, plant care
Propagating Verbenas from Cuttings
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Propagating Verbenas from Cuttings:

1. Take cuttings of young shoots from old plants early in February.
2. Place the cuttings in sandy loam and allow them to root for a few weeks.
3. After rooting, pot the cuttings.
4. When...
Procedure for Propagating Verbenas from Cuttings:

1. Take cuttings of young shoots from old plants early in February.
2. Place the cuttings in sandy loam and allow them to root for a few weeks.
3. After rooting, pot the cuttings.
4. When warm weather arrives, bed out the potted cuttings.
5. In September, repot the verbenas.

Alternative Method:
• Take cuttings from choice plants in August.
• Root them for two weeks.
• Pot the rooted cuttings.
• Repot them when the roots touch the sides of the pot.

Note: This method is generally applicable to any plants.
verbenas, propagation, cuttings, gardening, plant care
Raise Cucumber Plants from Cuttings
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To raise cucumber plants from cuttings:

1. Take a shoot that is ready for stopping.
2. Cut it off below the joint.
3. Cut smooth the lower end of the shoot or cutting.
4. Stick it into fine leaf or other rich mould, about an inch deep.
5....
To raise cucumber plants from cuttings:

1. Take a shoot that is ready for stopping.
2. Cut it off below the joint.
3. Cut smooth the lower end of the shoot or cutting.
4. Stick it into fine leaf or other rich mould, about an inch deep.
5. Provide plenty of heat.
6. Shade it from the rays of the sun until it is fairly struck.

By this method, cucumber plants may readily be propagated.
cucumber, cuttings, propagation, gardening

Tree Care (15) -

Renovate Old Apple Trees with Lime Solution
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Take fresh lime from the kiln.
2. Slake the lime well with water to create a solution.
3. Use a brush to apply this solution generously to the tree.
4. This treatment will destroy insects and moss, cause the outer rind of the...
Procedure:
1. Take fresh lime from the kiln.
2. Slake the lime well with water to create a solution.
3. Use a brush to apply this solution generously to the tree.
4. This treatment will destroy insects and moss, cause the outer rind of the tree to fall off, and promote the growth of a new smooth, clear, healthy rind.
5. The tree will appear healthier and produce finer fruit as a result.
apple trees,lime solution,renovation,moss removal,insect control
Treatment of Apple-Trees for Health and Maintenance
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Prune apple trees carefully and freely as soon as the leaves have fallen.
2. This opens a passage to the sun and air, contributing to the tree's health in future seasons.
3. Brush off moss and cut out cankered parts at any...
Procedure:
1. Prune apple trees carefully and freely as soon as the leaves have fallen.
2. This opens a passage to the sun and air, contributing to the tree's health in future seasons.
3. Brush off moss and cut out cankered parts at any convenient time.
4. Anoint the tree with a composition of sulphur and goose oil some feet from the ground.
5. If the orchard is not ploughed, open the soil around the roots to improve aeration and nutrient absorption.
apple trees,pruning,moss removal,canker treatment,sulphur goose oil
Applying Tar to Prevent Blight or Mildew in Orchards
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Rub tar well into the bark of apple trees.
2. Apply it about four to six inches wide around each tree and at approximately one foot from the ground.
3. This effectively prevents blight and results in abundant crops.

Notes:
-...
Procedure:

1. Rub tar well into the bark of apple trees.
2. Apply it about four to six inches wide around each tree and at approximately one foot from the ground.
3. This effectively prevents blight and results in abundant crops.

Notes:
• Tar is an effective barrier against blight and mildew.
apple tree,tar,application,blight,mildew
Clothing the Stems of Standard Trees
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Clothe the Stems of Standard Trees:

• Wrap the stems with an envelope of moss or short grass; alternatively, use litter wound round with shreds of matting.
• This helps keep the bark moist and aids sap circulation in the alburnum (inner...
To Clothe the Stems of Standard Trees:

• Wrap the stems with an envelope of moss or short grass; alternatively, use litter wound round with shreds of matting.
• This helps keep the bark moist and aids sap circulation in the alburnum (inner layer of wood).
• Perform this operation at or soon after planting.
• Leave the clothing on until it decays and drops off naturally.
• This is especially useful for very late planting or when summer-planting becomes necessary due to unforeseen circumstances.
tree wrapping, moss, matting, moisture retention, late planting
Preparing a Mildew Prevention Mixture for Fruit-Trees
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

Ingredients:
• 1 quart of whiskey
• 2 pounds of powdered sulphur
• 2 ounces of copperas (ferrous sulfate)
• A small quantity of camphor

Steps:
1. Reduce the camphor to powder and gradually dissolve it in the whiskey.
2....
Procedure:

Ingredients:
• 1 quart of whiskey
• 2 pounds of powdered sulphur
• 2 ounces of copperas (ferrous sulfate)
• A small quantity of camphor

Steps:
1. Reduce the camphor to powder and gradually dissolve it in the whiskey.
2. Dissolve the copperas in the same solution.
3. Gradually rub the powdered sulphur into the mixture until a thick consistency is achieved.
4. In the spring, after cleaning and tying up the fruit trees, completely cover their trunks and branches with this mixture using a large paint-brush.

Notes:
• This mixture is applied in early spring to prevent mildew on fruit-trees.
mildew prevention,mixture,fruit trees,sulphur,copperas,camphor
Preventing Gumming in Fruit-Trees with a Mixture of Horse Dung and Pitch-Tar
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

Ingredients:
• Horse dung (any quantity)
• Clay
• Sand
• Pitch-tar (from cart-wheels)

Steps:
1. Mix horse dung well with clay and a little sand to form a composition.
2. Add pitch-tar to create a wet, malleable mixture.
3....
Procedure:

Ingredients:
• Horse dung (any quantity)
• Clay
• Sand
• Pitch-tar (from cart-wheels)

Steps:
1. Mix horse dung well with clay and a little sand to form a composition.
2. Add pitch-tar to create a wet, malleable mixture.
3. In the spring of the year, after cleaning and tying up the fruit trees, completely cover their trunks and stems with this mixture.

Notes:
• This method prevents gumming, which can harm the growth and strength of fruit-trees.
gumming prevention,horse dung,pitch tar,mixture
Revitalizing Peach-Trees with Fresh Rotten Turf
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. In January and February, if peach trees are in a stunted or sickly state, carefully remove the old mould from their roots.
2. Replace it with fresh rotten turf from an old pasture (without any dung).
3. This will help the trees...
Procedure:

1. In January and February, if peach trees are in a stunted or sickly state, carefully remove the old mould from their roots.
2. Replace it with fresh rotten turf from an old pasture (without any dung).
3. This will help the trees recover their health and produce fine fruit.

Notes:
• This method is particularly effective for peach-trees that are not thriving.
peach tree,fresh turf,revitalization,stunted trees
Washing Apple-Tree Branches with Quick-Lime to Prevent Blight
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Wash the branches of apple trees with quick-lime.
2. This prevents blight and ensures a good crop.
3. Trees that are not washed will suffer from blight, while those that are washed will produce a good crop.

Notes:
-...
Procedure:

1. Wash the branches of apple trees with quick-lime.
2. This prevents blight and ensures a good crop.
3. Trees that are not washed will suffer from blight, while those that are washed will produce a good crop.

Notes:
• Quick-lime is an effective method for preserving apple trees from blight.
apple tree,washing,quick lime,blight
Apply Chalk and Tar Composition to Heal Tree Wounds
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Composition for healing wounds in trees:

1. Mix the following ingredients thoroughly:
- Three measures of dry pounded chalk
- One measure of common vegetable tar
2. Boil the mixture over a low heat until it reaches the consistency...
Composition for healing wounds in trees:

1. Mix the following ingredients thoroughly:
- Three measures of dry pounded chalk
- One measure of common vegetable tar
2. Boil the mixture over a low heat until it reaches the consistency of beeswax.
3. If chalk is unavailable, substitute with dry brick-dust.
4. Carefully remove any broken or decayed limbs and pare away all saw-cut material to ensure the surface is smooth.
5. Apply the hot composition over the wounded area and surrounding bark in a layer about the thickness of half a dollar using a hot trowel.
chalk,tar,beeswax,tree wounds,hot application
Apply Drying Varnish to Heal Tree Wounds
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To heal wounds in trees:

1. Prepare a varnish by boiling common linseed oil for one hour with the following ingredients:
- One ounce of litharge per pound of oil
- Calcined bones, pulverized and sifted to form an almost liquid...
To heal wounds in trees:

1. Prepare a varnish by boiling common linseed oil for one hour with the following ingredients:
- One ounce of litharge per pound of oil
- Calcined bones, pulverized and sifted to form an almost liquid paste
2. Ensure the bark and other substances around the wound are pared down so that the surface is as smooth and even as possible.
3. Apply the varnish using a brush in dry weather to ensure proper adhesion.
tree wounds,varnish,linseed oil,litharge,calcined bones
Avoid Corrosion from Iron Nails on Fruit Trees
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To avoid the bad effects of iron nails on fruit trees:

1. Avoid placing iron nails or cramps in contact with the bark of fruit trees, especially those trained against walls.
2. This corrosion can cause limbs to blight and die, a problem...
To avoid the bad effects of iron nails on fruit trees:

1. Avoid placing iron nails or cramps in contact with the bark of fruit trees, especially those trained against walls.
2. This corrosion can cause limbs to blight and die, a problem previously mistaken for lightning damage.
3. Ensure that all fastenings are placed so they do not touch the tree's bark.
iron nails,corrosion,fruit trees,bark protection
Destroy Moss on Trees Using Cow Dung and Soapsuds
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To destroy moss on trees:

1. Remove the moss using a hard scrubbing brush in February or March.
2. Wash the affected areas of the tree with a mixture of:
- Cow dung
- Urine
- Soapsuds
3. This method effectively...
To destroy moss on trees:

1. Remove the moss using a hard scrubbing brush in February or March.
2. Wash the affected areas of the tree with a mixture of:
- Cow dung
- Urine
- Soapsuds
3. This method effectively removes moss and prevents its regrowth.
moss removal,cow dung,soapsuds,scrubbing brush
Preserving Fruit Trees in Blossom from Frost
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Preserving Fruit Trees in Blossom from Frost:

1. Surround the trunk of a fruit tree in blossom with a wisp of straw or hemp.
2. Tie one end of the straw or hemp to a stone, and submerge the other end into a vessel of spring water...
Procedure for Preserving Fruit Trees in Blossom from Frost:

1. Surround the trunk of a fruit tree in blossom with a wisp of straw or hemp.
2. Tie one end of the straw or hemp to a stone, and submerge the other end into a vessel of spring water placed at a short distance from the tree.
3. One vessel can serve two trees if the cord is lengthened appropriately.
4. Ensure that the vessel containing the water is placed in an open area out of the reach of shade so that frost can act on the water through the cord.

Notes: This method helps protect fruit trees from frost damage by using a continuous flow of water to absorb and dissipate cold energy.
frost protection, fruit tree preservation, winter tree care
Recover Deformed Trees
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Assess the tree's condition: Determine if it is stunted or ill-shaped due to poor pruning, overbearing, or constitutional weakness.
2. Head down the plant: Cut back the tree within three, four, or five eyes (or inches, for...
Procedure:

1. Assess the tree's condition: Determine if it is stunted or ill-shaped due to poor pruning, overbearing, or constitutional weakness.
2. Head down the plant: Cut back the tree within three, four, or five eyes (or inches, for older trees) from the top of the stem to encourage a new head.
3. Prune roots if necessary:
- For overly luxuriant trees: Remove part of the sound, healthy roots to moderate growth.
- For stinted or sickly trees: Prune cankered or decayed roots and shorten dangling tap-roots affected by a bad subsoil.
4. Renovate the soil: Improve the quality of the soil around the tree.
5. Drain the subsoil if necessary: Address drainage issues in the subsoil to promote recovery.

Notes: This method is most effective for younger trees, and results may vary with older or more severely damaged trees.
recover deformed trees, prune roots, head down tree, renovate soil
Transplanting Trees to Prevent Blight
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Dig up peach and nectarine trees every five or six years.
2. Replant them with fresh mould.
3. This method increases the quantity and quality of fruit produced.

Notes:
• Newly transplanted trees are less likely to be attacked...
Procedure:

1. Dig up peach and nectarine trees every five or six years.
2. Replant them with fresh mould.
3. This method increases the quantity and quality of fruit produced.

Notes:
• Newly transplanted trees are less likely to be attacked by blight compared to other trees of the same kind in the same situation.
• Covering trees with mats can lead to blight due to lack of light, especially during extreme heat or cold.
transplanting,trees,blight,peach,nectarine,fresh mould

Container Gardening (15) -

Container Gardening Basics
Standard Source: almanac start a garden
Benefits of Container Gardening:
• Ideal for small spaces or individuals with limited garden area.
• Reduces time and cost compared to traditional gardening methods.

Getting Started:
1. Choose Containers: Use large pots, planters, or...
Benefits of Container Gardening:
• Ideal for small spaces or individuals with limited garden area.
• Reduces time and cost compared to traditional gardening methods.

Getting Started:
1. Choose Containers: Use large pots, planters, or repurposed containers that provide adequate drainage and space for root growth.
2. Select Soil Mix: Use a high-quality potting mix that is well-draining and nutrient-rich.
3. Plant Selection: Choose plants that thrive in containers (e.g., tomatoes, peppers, herbs).
4. Watering and Maintenance: Water regularly, as container soil dries out more quickly than garden soil. Monitor for signs of overwatering or underwatering.

Notes:
• Ensure containers have drainage holes to prevent waterlogging.
• Use supports (e.g., stakes) for tall plants like tomatoes.
container gardening, small space gardening, container plant care, raised bed alternatives
Growing Cucumbers in Containers or Hanging Baskets
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Select a container: Use a large pot or hanging basket for growing cucumbers.
2. Provide support: Train the vines to climb using a fence, trellis, or arbor if desired.
3. Ensure proper drainage: Make sure the container...
Procedure:

1. Select a container: Use a large pot or hanging basket for growing cucumbers.
2. Provide support: Train the vines to climb using a fence, trellis, or arbor if desired.
3. Ensure proper drainage: Make sure the container has adequate drainage holes.
4. Use quality soil: Fill the container with rich, well-drained soil high in organic matter.
container gardening, cucumber, hanging basket, vegetable garden
Setting Up a Container Garden for Vegetable Growing
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Choosing the Right Location:
• Ideal for areas with limited space
• Use vertical space effectively

Container Gardening Advantages:
• Soil warms up faster, allowing earlier planting of cool-season crops
• Plants can be moved indoors...
Choosing the Right Location:
• Ideal for areas with limited space
• Use vertical space effectively

Container Gardening Advantages:
• Soil warms up faster, allowing earlier planting of cool-season crops
• Plants can be moved indoors during unexpected cold snaps

Planning Your Container Garden:
1. Plan the layout similarly to a small garden plot.
2. Use trellises for vining plants, stakes and cages for tomatoes, and utilize railings as natural trellises on balconies.
3. Use vertical space in containers by planting in holes or pockets on the sides of containers.
4. Grow vining plants in hanging baskets, ensuring they don't shade other plants.

Selecting Plants for Containers:
• Choose varieties suitable for container growing
• Opt for smaller, compact growth habits to maximize space
• Be mindful that containers dry out faster, requiring more frequent watering
• Move containers to protected areas or indoors during cool nights
container vegetable garden, small space gardening, balcony gardening, container plant selection
Choosing Between Clay and Plastic Containers for Vegetable Gardening
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
Clay pots are usually more attractive than plastic ones, but plastic retains moisture better than unglazed terra-cotta. To get the best of both, slip a plastic pot into a slightly larger clay pot.
clay pots, plastic containers, moisture retention
Cooling and Moistening Container Plants During Hot Weather
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
To keep plants adequately cool and moist during hot summer days, double-pot them: Place a small pot inside a larger one and fill the space between them with sphagnum moss or crumpled newspaper. When watering the plant, also **soak the filler...
To keep plants adequately cool and moist during hot summer days, double-pot them: Place a small pot inside a larger one and fill the space between them with sphagnum moss or crumpled newspaper. When watering the plant, also soak the filler between the pots.
cooling plants, double-potting, hot weather
Feeding Container Plants with Liquid Fertilizer
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
Feed container plants at least twice a month with liquid fertilizer, following the instructions on the label. (An occasional application of fish emulsion or compost will add trace elements to container soil.)
fertilizing, liquid fertilizer, container plants
Grow Peanuts in Containers
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Start peanuts in pots if your growing season is short.
2. Ensure the pot has well-draining soil and adequate sunlight.
3. Transplant the plants outdoors when the weather warms up.

Details:
• Peanuts can be grown...
Procedure:

1. Start peanuts in pots if your growing season is short.
2. Ensure the pot has well-draining soil and adequate sunlight.
3. Transplant the plants outdoors when the weather warms up.

Details:
• Peanuts can be grown indoors as a novel houseplant.
• Container gardening is ideal for areas with limited space or shorter growing seasons.
peanuts, container gardening, indoor plants
Growing Chives in Containers
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Steps to Grow Chives in Containers:

1. If growing chives indoors, grow several pots so you can take turns clipping from them when needed for cooking and flavoring.
2. Separate the clumps of chives from time to time to maintain healthy...
Steps to Grow Chives in Containers:

1. If growing chives indoors, grow several pots so you can take turns clipping from them when needed for cooking and flavoring.
2. Separate the clumps of chives from time to time to maintain healthy growth.
3. Ensure that the soil does not dry out, as this will cause the tips of the leaves — the part you want to eat — to become brown and unappetizing.
chives, container gardening, indoor growing
Improving Drainage in Container Gardens
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
Before planting, add about 1 inch of coarse gravel in the bottom of the container to improve drainage.
drainage, coarse gravel, container gardening
Positioning Containers for Optimal Growth
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
Place containers where they will receive maximum sunlight and good air circulation.
sunlight, air circulation, container placement
Selecting Appropriate Container Sizes for Vegetable Gardening
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
Avoid small containers as they often can’t store enough water to get through hot days. Larger pots hold moisture better, and the bigger your container, the more plants you can grow.
container size, vegetable gardening, moisture retention
Selecting Vegetables Suitable for Container Gardening
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
Vegetables that can be easily transplanted, such as peppers and cherry tomatoes, are best suited for containers. Transplants can be purchased from local nurseries or started from seed at home.
transplantable vegetables, container gardening, peppers, cherry tomatoes
Using Alternative Containers for Vegetable Gardening
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
You can use barrels, buckets, baskets, boxes, tubs, and troughs—anything that holds soil. Ensure the container has drainage holes in the bottom.
alternative containers, drainage holes, vegetable gardening
Using Hanging Baskets for Vegetable Gardening
Reference Source: almanac start a garden
Hanging baskets make good use of space, and plants grown at eye level can be easily tended and harvested.
hanging baskets, space efficiency, vegetable gardening
Grow Radishes in Containers
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use six-inch pots filled with well-draining soil.
2. Place the containers in a bright, cool window to ensure adequate light and temperature conditions for growth.
3. Water regularly and fertilize as needed using liquid,...
Procedure:

1. Use six-inch pots filled with well-draining soil.
2. Place the containers in a bright, cool window to ensure adequate light and temperature conditions for growth.
3. Water regularly and fertilize as needed using liquid, all-purpose fertilizer diluted to quarter strength if growing in sand.

Details:
• Radishes can be grown successfully in containers even without garden space.
• Proper watering and fertilization are essential when using containers or sandy soil.
radish container growing, pot size, cool window placement

Sprouting (15) -

Growing Sprouts at Home
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Growing Sprouts at Home

1. Select seeds: Choose a variety of sprouting seeds such as alfalfa, wheat, rye berries, or lentils.
2. Prepare containers: Use clean jars or containers with lids that allow for some airflow.
3. **Add seeds and...
Growing Sprouts at Home

1. Select seeds: Choose a variety of sprouting seeds such as alfalfa, wheat, rye berries, or lentils.
2. Prepare containers: Use clean jars or containers with lids that allow for some airflow.
3. Add seeds and water: Add 2 tablespoons of alfalfa seeds to one jar, 1 cup of wheat or rye berries to another, and ½ cup of lentils to a third jar. Rinse the seeds thoroughly in cold water and add enough water to cover them by about an inch.
4. Soak seeds: Let the seeds soak for several hours (usually overnight) until they begin to swell.
5. Drain and rinse: Drain the soaked seeds and rinse them with fresh water. Repeat this process daily, ensuring that the seeds remain moist but not submerged in water.
6. Store in dark place: Place the jars in a cool, dark location (such as a cabinet or pantry) to encourage sprouting.
7. Monitor growth:
- Alfalfa seeds take about 5 days to reach an edible stage.
- Wheat and rye berries are ready for harvest within 2 days.
8. Harvest sprouts: When the sprouts have grown to the desired length (usually 1–3 inches), rinse them thoroughly and store in a cool, dry place or use immediately.
9. Enjoy sprouts: Add sprouts to salads, sandwiches, soups, and other dishes for crunch and nutrition.
home sprouting, growing sprouts, vegetable gardening, sprout recipes, sprout benefits
Jar Sprouting Procedure for Small Seeds
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Step-by-step instructions:
1. Rinse the seeds in lukewarm water.
2. Fill a jar with the seeds, then add 3 times as much water as the amount of seeds.
3. Cover the jar with plastic mesh, cheesecloth, or nylon net and secure with a...
Step-by-step instructions:
1. Rinse the seeds in lukewarm water.
2. Fill a jar with the seeds, then add 3 times as much water as the amount of seeds.
3. Cover the jar with plastic mesh, cheesecloth, or nylon net and secure with a rubber band or canning jar screw band.
4. Soak the seeds for the time specified in your recipe (do not remove the cover until sprouts are ready to harvest).
5. At the end of soaking time, drain off the water through the mesh covering.
6. Rinse and drain the seeds with lukewarm water.
7. Place the jar in a warm (60°F), dark place at an angle, allowing sprouts to drain.
8. Rinse and drain twice daily or as directed by your recipe. In hot, dry weather, rinse 3–4 times per day. Gently turn the jar during rinsing to prevent sprouts from breaking off.
9. If humidity is high (e.g., in a very humid kitchen), move the sprouts to a dry area such as near the stove or wrapped in a towel near a sunny window. Avoid temperatures above 80°F, which can inhibit sprouting.
10. On about day 4, move the jar into sunlight to allow chlorophyll development and green leaf growth. Continue rinsing and draining.
11. When ready to harvest, transfer sprouts to a strainer and rinse well to remove hulls (optional). Hulls add flavor but may shorten storage life.
12. Use sprouts immediately in salads or sandwiches, or store them in plastic bags in the refrigerator.
13. Clean and dry all equipment for future use.
jar sprouting, small seeds, alfalfa sprouts, radish sprouts, clover sprouts
Select Seeds for Sprouting
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Seeds to use:
• Wheat
• Rye
• Alfalfa
• Mung beans
• Chick peas
• Soybeans
• Pumpkin seeds
• Sesame seeds

Important considerations:
1. Always choose live, untreated seed — chemically treated seeds are not safe for sprouting.
2. **Avoid...
Seeds to use:
• Wheat
• Rye
• Alfalfa
• Mung beans
• Chick peas
• Soybeans
• Pumpkin seeds
• Sesame seeds

Important considerations:
1. Always choose live, untreated seed — chemically treated seeds are not safe for sprouting.
2. Avoid heat-treated seeds, as they cannot germinate even if edible.
3. Use the drying method recommended for sprouting (not blanching, chilling, or heating).

Water:
• Use lukewarm or room-temperature water.
• If using city water with high chlorine content, let it sit for 1–2 days to allow chlorine to dissipate.
sprouting seeds, untreated seed, heat-treated seed, chlorine water, sprout safety
Set Up Sprouting Equipment
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Materials needed:
• A jar
• Cheesecloth, plastic mesh, or plastic screen
• Rubber band to secure the cover

Alternative methods:
• Use a tray with damp towels
• Use a clay flowerpot saucer
• Use a thin layer of soil
• Purchase ready-made...
Materials needed:
• A jar
• Cheesecloth, plastic mesh, or plastic screen
• Rubber band to secure the cover

Alternative methods:
• Use a tray with damp towels
• Use a clay flowerpot saucer
• Use a thin layer of soil
• Purchase ready-made sprouters (e.g., mesh trays or sprouting lids for jars)

Tip: Try different methods to find what works best for you.
sprouting equipment, sprouter setup, jar sprouting, tray sprouting, clay saucer sprouting
Sprouting Lentils
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Use 3/4 cup of lentils in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 6 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 8 to 10 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 2 to 3 times daily for 2 to 3 days.
5. Harvest when...
Procedure:
1. Use 3/4 cup of lentils in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 6 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 8 to 10 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 2 to 3 times daily for 2 to 3 days.
5. Harvest when the sprouts are ¼ to ½ inch long.
6. Use in salads, sauces, dips, juices, soups, or casseroles.
lentils,sprouting,vegetables,gardening
Sprouting Lettuce Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Use 3 tablespoons of lettuce seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 2 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 8 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 2 to 3 times daily for 4 to 5 days.
5. Move...
Procedure:
1. Use 3 tablespoons of lettuce seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 2 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 8 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 2 to 3 times daily for 4 to 5 days.
5. Move the sprouts into sunlight to green them.
6. Harvest when the sprouts are 1 to 1½ inches long.
7. Use as a garnish (note: flavor is strong).
lettuce,sprouting,vegetables,gardening
Sprouting Millet Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Use 1½ cups of millet seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 2 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 8 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 3 times daily for 4 to 5 days.
5. Harvest when the...
Procedure:
1. Use 1½ cups of millet seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 2 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 8 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 3 times daily for 4 to 5 days.
5. Harvest when the sprouts are ¼ inch long.
6. Use in salads, soups, baked goods, casseroles, and juices.
millet,sprouting,vegetables,gardening
Sprouting Mustard Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Use 3 tablespoons of mustard seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 1 quart of sprouts.
3. Do not soak the seeds.
4. Rinse the seeds 2 to 3 times daily for 4 to 5 days.
5. Move the...
Procedure:
1. Use 3 tablespoons of mustard seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 1 quart of sprouts.
3. Do not soak the seeds.
4. Rinse the seeds 2 to 3 times daily for 4 to 5 days.
5. Move the sprouts into sunlight to green them.
6. Harvest when the sprouts are 1 to 1½ inches long.
7. Use in salads, juices, or as a garnish.
mustard,sprouting,vegetables,gardening
Sprouting Peas
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Use ½ cup of black-eyed or shelling peas in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 1 cup of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 12 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 2 to 3 times daily for 3 days.
5....
Procedure:
1. Use ½ cup of black-eyed or shelling peas in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 1 cup of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 12 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 2 to 3 times daily for 3 days.
5. Harvest when the sprouts are ¼ to ½ inch long.
6. Use in salads, soups, omelets, and casseroles.
peas,sprouting,vegetables,gardening
Sprouting Pumpkin Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Use 1½ cups of pumpkin seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 3 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 10 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds twice daily for 2 to 3 days.
5. Harvest when the...
Procedure:
1. Use 1½ cups of pumpkin seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 3 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 10 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds twice daily for 2 to 3 days.
5. Harvest when the sprouts are 1 to 1½ inches long; pick off hulls and rinse.
6. Use in sauces, dips, and baked goods.

Sprouting Rye Seeds

2. This will yield about 2 to 3 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 12 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds twice daily for 2 to 3 days.
5. The sprouts will be...
Procedure:
1. Use 1 cup of rye seeds in a 1-quart jar or tray sprout.
2. This will yield about 2 to 3 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 12 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds twice daily for 2 to 3 days.
5. The sprouts will be the length of the seeds.
6. Use in granola, salads, and baked goods.

4. Rinse the seeds 3 to 4 times daily for 3 days.
5. The sprouts...
Procedure:

2. This will yield about 2 cups of sprouts.
3. Soak the seeds for 8 to 10 hours.
4. Rinse the seeds 3 to 4 times daily for 3 days.
5. The sprouts will be the length of the seed.

3. Soak the seeds for 12 to 24 hours, changing the soaking water once.
4. Rinse the seeds 3 to 4 times daily...
Procedure:

2. This will yield about 1 quart of sprouts.

4. Rinse the seeds 3 to 4 times daily for 3 to 4 days.
5. Harvest when the sprouts are ½ to 2 inches long.

Planting Techniques (13) -

Plant Tomato Seedlings with Proper Spacing and Depth
Standard Source: almanac start a garden
Procedure:

• Plant seedlings two feet apart, pinching off a few of the lower leaves and planting the root ball deep enough so that the remaining lowest leaves are just above the surface of the soil.

Details:
• Dig a hole slightly larger...
Procedure:

• Plant seedlings two feet apart, pinching off a few of the lower leaves and planting the root ball deep enough so that the remaining lowest leaves are just above the surface of the soil.

Details:
• Dig a hole slightly larger than the root ball to allow for proper root expansion.
• Place the seedling in the hole, ensuring that the base of the stem is buried up to the first set of true leaves.
• Backfill with soil and gently firm around the roots to eliminate air pockets.
• Water thoroughly after planting to settle the soil and reduce transplant shock.
tomato planting,spacing,garden
Plant Tomato Seeds Indoors for Optimal Growth
Standard Source: almanac start a garden
Procedure:

• If planting seeds instead of purchasing transplants, start them indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the average last spring frost date.

Details:
• Use a seed tray or small pots filled with well-draining potting mix.
• Sow seeds at a...
Procedure:

• If planting seeds instead of purchasing transplants, start them indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the average last spring frost date.

Details:
• Use a seed tray or small pots filled with well-draining potting mix.
• Sow seeds at a depth of about 1/4 inch and keep the soil consistently moist until germination occurs.
• Place trays in a warm, sunny location or use grow lights to ensure adequate light for seedlings.
• Once seedlings have developed two sets of true leaves, they can be transplanted into larger containers.
tomato seeds,indoor planting,garden
Alternative Method of Budding
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Another Method of Budding:

1. Make Transverse Section: Make a transverse section in the bark of the stock.
2. Create Perpendicular Slit: Make a perpendicular slit beneath it.
3. Insert Bud: Push the bud down to give it the position...
Another Method of Budding:

1. Make Transverse Section: Make a transverse section in the bark of the stock.
2. Create Perpendicular Slit: Make a perpendicular slit beneath it.
3. Insert Bud: Push the bud down to give it the position it is to have. This operation is not always successful.
4. Inverse Method: It is better to employ an inverse or contrary method by making the vertical slit above the transverse section or cut, and pushing the bark containing the bud upwards into its proper position.
5. Benefits of Inverse Method: This method very rarely fails of success because as the sap descends by the bark, the kind placed above the transverse section receives abundance, whereas if it be placed below the section, very little sap can ever get to it to promote the growth of the bud.

Additional Tip: Oil rubbed upon the stems and branches of fruit trees destroys insects and increases the fruit. Used upon the stems of carnations, it guards them against the depredations of the ear-wig. The coarsest oil will suit, and only a small quantity is required.
alternative budding method, plant propagation, horticulture, tree cultivation
Budding with Double Ligatures (Mr. T. A. Knight's Method)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Bud with Double Ligatures:

1. Perform Standard Budding Steps: Follow the procedure as stated above for shield budding.
2. Apply Two Ligatures: Instead of one ligature, apply two: one above the bud inserted upon the transverse...
To Bud with Double Ligatures:

1. Perform Standard Budding Steps: Follow the procedure as stated above for shield budding.
2. Apply Two Ligatures: Instead of one ligature, apply two: one above the bud inserted upon the transverse section, through the bark; the other applied below in the usual way.
3. Remove Lower Ligatures: As soon as the buds have attached themselves, remove the lower ligatures but leave the upper ones intact.
4. Effect of Double Ligatures: The passage of the sap upwards is much obstructed, and the inserted buds begin to vegetate strongly in July (being inserted in June).
5. Remove Upper Ligatures: When these have afforded shoots about four inches long, remove the remaining ligatures to permit the excess of sap to pass on.
6. Secure Young Shoots: The young shoots are nailed to the wall and properly exposed to light. Their wood will ripen well and afford blossoms in the succeeding spring.
double ligatures budding, plant propagation, horticulture, tree cultivation
Expeditious Method for Planting Using a Spade Star Technique
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Make three cuts with a spade, each 12 to 15 inches long, crossing at the center at an angle of 60 degrees, forming a star shape.
2. Insert the spade across one of the rays, a few inches from the center on the side nearest...
Procedure:

1. Make three cuts with a spade, each 12 to 15 inches long, crossing at the center at an angle of 60 degrees, forming a star shape.
2. Insert the spade across one of the rays, a few inches from the center on the side nearest to the operator.
3. Bend the handle of the spade toward the operator and almost to the ground, causing the earth to open in fissures from the center in the direction of the cuts.
4. Insert the plant at the point where the spade intersects the ray, pushing it forward to the center and assisting the roots in spreading through the fissures.
5. Remove the spade, pressing the soil into a compact state with the heel.
6. Add earth with the grass side down, completely covering the fissures to retain moisture at the root and as a top-dressing, which encourages the plant to put fresh roots between the swards.
spade technique, planting method, star cuts, soil preparation, root encouragement
Shield Budding Procedure
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Shield Budding is performed as follows:

1. Select the Stock Location: Fix on a smooth part of the side of the stock, rather from than towards the sun. The height depends on whether dwarf, half, or whole standard trees are desired.
2. **Make...
Shield Budding is performed as follows:

1. Select the Stock Location: Fix on a smooth part of the side of the stock, rather from than towards the sun. The height depends on whether dwarf, half, or whole standard trees are desired.
2. Make Horizontal Cut: With the buddingknife, make a horizontal cut across the rind, quite through the firm wood.
3. Create Perpendicular Slit: From the middle of this transverse cut, make a slit downward perpendicularly, an inch or more long, going also quite through to the wood.
4. Take Off a Bud: Proceed with expedition to take off a bud. Hold the cutting or scion in one hand, with the thickest end outward, and with the knife in the other hand. Enter it about half an inch or more below a bud, cutting nearly half-way into the wood of the shoot.
5. Make Slanting Cut: Continue with one clean slanting cut, about half an inch or more above the bud, so deep as to take off part of the wood along with it. The whole should be about an inch and a half long.
6. Remove Woody Part: With the thumb and finger, or point of the knife, slip off the woody part remaining to the bud. Observe whether the eye or germ remains perfect. If not, and a little hole appears in that part, the bud has lost its root, and another must be prepared.
7. Position Bud: Place the back part of the bud or shield between the lips. With the flat heft of the knife, separate the bark of the stock on each side of the perpendicular cut clear to the wood for the admission of the bud.
8. Insert Bud: Slip down the bud close between the wood and bark to the bottom of the slit.
9. Trim Top of Shield: Cut off the top part of the shield even with the horizontal cut, in order to let it completely into its place and join exactly the upper edge of the shield with the transverse cut.
10. Bind Bud: Bind round the parts with a ligament of fresh bass, previously soaked in water, to render it pliable and tough. Begin a little below the bottom of the perpendicular slit, proceeding upward closely round every part, except just over the eye of the bud, and continue it a little above the horizontal cut. Not too tight, but sufficient to keep the hole close and exclude air, sun, and wet.

Notes: This method ensures that the descending sap may immediately enter the back of the shield, protruding granulated matter between it and the wood to effect a living union.
shield budding, grafting, plant propagation, horticulture, tree cultivation
Companion Planting for Space Optimization
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Objective: Maximize garden space by planting short-term crops between long-maturing plants.

Procedure:
1. Choose a long-maturing crop (e.g., tomatoes) that will occupy the same area for an extended period.
2. **Plant fast-maturing,...
Objective: Maximize garden space by planting short-term crops between long-maturing plants.

Procedure:
1. Choose a long-maturing crop (e.g., tomatoes) that will occupy the same area for an extended period.
2. Plant fast-maturing, short-term crops between the rows or around the base of the long-maturing plant (e.g., radishes).
3. Harvest the short-term crops before they interfere with the growth of the long-maturing crop.
4. Repeat as needed for other combinations that work well together (e.g., lettuce and carrots, onions and leeks).

Notes:
• This method is ideal for small gardens where space is limited.
• It ensures efficient use of garden area without sacrificing yield.
companion planting, space optimization, crop pairing, intercropping
Plant Kohlrabi Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Sow kohlrabi seeds in rows that are 18 to 24 inches apart.
2. Cover the seeds with a quarter to a half inch of soil.
3. Ensure consistent moisture until germination occurs.
4. Once seedlings emerge and are growing well,...
Procedure:

1. Sow kohlrabi seeds in rows that are 18 to 24 inches apart.
2. Cover the seeds with a quarter to a half inch of soil.
3. Ensure consistent moisture until germination occurs.
4. Once seedlings emerge and are growing well, thin them to five or six inches apart.
5. Transplant any thinnings if desired.

Details:
• Kohlrabi can be grown directly from seed in the garden.
• Avoid cultivating too deeply to prevent damage to shallow roots.
kohlrabi, planting, seed sowing, gardening
Plant Radish Seeds
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Plant radish seeds half an inch deep in rows or wide rows spaced 12 to 18 inches apart.
2. When seedlings are large enough to handle, thin them according to the variety:
- Small spring varieties: Thin to **one to...
Procedure:

1. Plant radish seeds half an inch deep in rows or wide rows spaced 12 to 18 inches apart.
2. When seedlings are large enough to handle, thin them according to the variety:
- Small spring varieties: Thin to one to three inches apart
- Winter varieties: Provide more space than spring varieties

Details:
• Radishes germinate quickly and can be used as markers for slower-growing seeds.
• Thinning is essential to prevent overcrowding and ensure healthy root development.
radish planting, seed depth, row spacing, thinning radishes
Planting Cauliflower Transplants
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Select healthy transplants that are four to six weeks old with four or five true leaves.
2. Check for legginess or crooked stems; if present, plant the transplant deeply (up to the first leaves) to prevent...
Procedure:

1. Select healthy transplants that are four to six weeks old with four or five true leaves.
2. Check for legginess or crooked stems; if present, plant the transplant deeply (up to the first leaves) to prevent top-heaviness.
3. Space transplants 18 to 24 inches apart in rows that are 24 to 36 inches apart.
4. Plant seedlings at the correct depth, ensuring that the roots are fully covered and the soil is firmly packed around the base of the plant.
5. Water thoroughly after planting to settle the soil and eliminate air pockets.
cauliflower, transplant planting, spacing, depth, watering
Planting Short Rows for Paced Harvest
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Objective: Avoid overproduction and ensure a steady supply of vegetables throughout the growing season.

Procedure:
1. Divide seeds into groups based on planting dates using labeled "budget" envelopes.
2. **Plant short rows (e.g., 10...
Objective: Avoid overproduction and ensure a steady supply of vegetables throughout the growing season.

Procedure:
1. Divide seeds into groups based on planting dates using labeled "budget" envelopes.
2. Plant short rows (e.g., 10 feet) instead of long, full rows to control yield per harvest.
3. Space out planting dates for crops that produce large quantities quickly (e.g., radishes) by planting them in intervals (e.g., every two weeks).
4. Adjust based on crop type:
- For high-yield crops like parsley or garlic, plant smaller rows to avoid excess.
- For fast-maturing crops like lettuce, use short rows and stagger planting dates for continuous harvests.

Notes:
• Excess produce can be preserved (e.g., freeze parsley, dry garlic).
• This method helps prevent gluts and ensures a steady supply of fresh vegetables.
short rows, paced harvest, planting intervals, seed budget envelopes, garden planning
Succession Planting for Continuous Harvests
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Objective: Maximize garden space by planting multiple crops in the same area over time.

Procedure:
1. Plant early-maturing cool-season crops (e.g., lettuce, spinach, cabbage) at the beginning of the season.
2. **Harvest and clear a...
Objective: Maximize garden space by planting multiple crops in the same area over time.

Procedure:
1. Plant early-maturing cool-season crops (e.g., lettuce, spinach, cabbage) at the beginning of the season.
2. Harvest and clear a portion of the garden after these crops are mature.
3. Replant with warm-weather crops such as corn, okra, squash, or chard in the cleared area.
4. Repeat the process in fall by replanting with cool-season crops (e.g., cole crops) or root vegetables like beets and turnips.
5. Use trellis space efficiently:
- Plant early peas on a trellis, then replace them with cucumbers after harvesting.
- After cucumber harvest, replant the same trellis area with peas for a fall crop.

Notes:
• This method is especially useful in small gardens.
• It ensures continuous use of garden space and provides variety throughout the year.
succession planting, garden space optimization, trellis use, crop rotation
Using Multiple Varieties for Staggered Harvests
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Objective: Spread out the harvest period of a single crop type by using different varieties.

Procedure:
1. Select multiple varieties of the same vegetable that mature at different rates (e.g., tomatoes).
2. **Plant all selected...
Objective: Spread out the harvest period of a single crop type by using different varieties.

Procedure:
1. Select multiple varieties of the same vegetable that mature at different rates (e.g., tomatoes).
2. Plant all selected varieties on the same day, but ensure they have varying maturity periods:
- Early variety: 60–70 days to maturity.
- Midseason variety: 75–80 days to maturity.
- Late variety: 80–90 days to maturity.
3. Monitor growth and harvest each variety as it matures, ensuring a continuous supply over a longer period (e.g., 30–50 days instead of 10–20 days).

Notes:
• This method is ideal for crops with long growing seasons.
• It helps avoid overwhelming the household with large quantities of produce at once.
multiple varieties, staggered harvests, tomato planting, maturity periods

Animal Husbandry (13) -

Avoid Feeding Spoiled or Contaminated Food to Rabbits
Standard Source: Keeping Rabbits
- Do not feed rabbits greens that have become heated, food sprayed with pesticides, spoiled food, or mouldy hay.

• These substances can cause poisoning, digestive problems, or long-term health issues in rabbits.

-...
- Do not feed rabbits greens that have become heated, food sprayed with pesticides, spoiled food, or mouldy hay.

• These substances can cause poisoning, digestive problems, or long-term health issues in rabbits.

• Always ensure the rabbit's food is fresh, clean, and free from contaminants.
rabbit diet, spoiled food, rabbit care, contaminated food
Determine the Breeding Readiness of Rabbits
Standard Source: Keeping Rabbits
- Female rabbits are ready to breed when they are 4 to 6 months old.

• Male rabbits are ready to breed when they are 5 to 6 months old.

• Ensure that both males and females have reached this age before attempting to breed them.
rabbit breeding, breeding readiness
Identify and Avoid Poisonous Plants for Rabbits
Standard Source: Keeping Rabbits
Important: Certain plants are poisonous to rabbits and should be avoided in their diet.

• Potato leaves, tomato leaves, and rhubarb are toxic to rabbits. Feeding these to rabbits can cause serious health issues or even...
Important: Certain plants are poisonous to rabbits and should be avoided in their diet.

• Potato leaves, tomato leaves, and rhubarb are toxic to rabbits. Feeding these to rabbits can cause serious health issues or even death.

Always ensure that the rabbit's environment is free from these plants, especially if they have access to outdoor areas.
rabbit care, poisonous plants, rabbit diet, toxic plants for rabbits
Optimal Breeding Seasons for Rabbits
Standard Source: Keeping Rabbits
- Female rabbits are more productive during springtime, summer, and early autumn.

• Breeding during the winter months is not recommended due to the cold climate, which can negatively impact reproductive success and the health of...
- Female rabbits are more productive during springtime, summer, and early autumn.

• Breeding during the winter months is not recommended due to the cold climate, which can negatively impact reproductive success and the health of both the mother and offspring.

• Ensure that breeding occurs during optimal seasons for better results.
rabbit breeding season, optimal breeding time
Proper Mating Technique for Rabbits
Standard Source: Keeping Rabbits
- Keep the male rabbit in a separate cage.

• Always put the female into the male’s cage to attempt mating.
