Garden Tools (1) -

Use the UrBin Grower for Efficient Organic Gardening
Reference Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
The UrBin Grower is a sub-irrigation system that allows for efficient and sustainable organic gardening:

Components of the UrBin Grower:
• Growing Container: Holds plants and soil.
• Water Reservoir: Stores water below the soil level to...
The UrBin Grower is a sub-irrigation system that allows for efficient and sustainable organic gardening:

Components of the UrBin Grower:
• Growing Container: Holds plants and soil.
• Water Reservoir: Stores water below the soil level to provide hydration through capillary action.
• Soil Separator Grid: Prevents waterlogging while allowing roots to access moisture.
• Seed Tray: Used for germinating seeds before transplanting into the growing container.
• Coir and Pumice: Provide aeration, drainage, and nutrient retention in the soil.

How It Works:
1. Plants grow downward through the soil separator grid and into the water reservoir below.
2. Capillary action pulls water upward from the reservoir into the soil above.
3. Roots absorb moisture and nutrients efficiently without overwatering.
4. The system allows for easy monitoring of plant growth and health.

Advantages:
• Prevents waterlogging by separating soil from the water reservoir.
• Reduces nutrient runoff, ensuring efficient use of fertilizers and compost.
• Eliminates the risk of overwatering when following the provided instructions.
urbin grower,sub-irrigation system,organic gardening,soil separator

Seed Starting (1) -

Coir as a Seed Starting Mix
Standard Source: Worm Factory 360 Manual
Using Coir for Seed Starting:

• Challenges in seed starting: Many gardeners find seed starting challenging due to the risk of failure.

Key Considerations from Garden Writers:
1. Moisture management: Use coir as a medium to retain...
Using Coir for Seed Starting:

• Challenges in seed starting: Many gardeners find seed starting challenging due to the risk of failure.

Key Considerations from Garden Writers:
1. Moisture management: Use coir as a medium to retain moisture and reduce the need for frequent watering.
2. Light requirements: Ensure that seeds receive sufficient light for germination and healthy growth.

Benefits of Coir for Seed Starting:
• Coir is unmatched in its ability to support seed starting due to its excellent water retention properties.
• It eliminates the need for wetting agents like polymers or frequent watering, making it an ideal medium for beginners.
coir, seed starting, moisture management, light requirements, wetting agents

Supplies Inventory (1) -

Prepare Gardening Supplies Inventory
Reference Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure for Preparing a Gardening Supplies Inventory:

1. List Required Items and Quantities:
- Black polyethylene: 1 unit
- Bleach: 5 gallons
- Clear polyethylene: 1 unit
- Garden hoses: 2 units
- Herb seeds (assortment):...
Procedure for Preparing a Gardening Supplies Inventory:

1. List Required Items and Quantities:
- Black polyethylene: 1 unit
- Bleach: 5 gallons
- Clear polyethylene: 1 unit
- Garden hoses: 2 units
- Herb seeds (assortment): as needed
- Hoe: 2 units
- Misters for seedlings: 2 units
- Miracle Gro: 2 units
- Non-hybrid seeds (assortment): 1 unit
- Organic fertilizers (assortment): 2 units
- Perennial flowerseeds (assortment): 3 units
- Pull wagon: 1 unit
- Rototiller: 1 unit
- Seed starting containers (assortment): 2 units
- Seed starting medium (assortment): 2 units
- Thermometers: 2 units
- Soil testing equipment: 1 unit
- Sprayer/Pumper (2-gallon size): 1 unit
- Styrofoam cups: 1000 units
- Watering can: 1 unit
- Wheelbarrow: 2 units
- Bug spray (Malathion, Sevin, Dursban, Diazanon): as needed
- Note: Dursban and Diazanon have severe side effects in humans; use outside of the house only. Sevin is safer for vegetables.

2. Set Purchase Priorities and Dates:
- Items with priority 1: Purchase by 10-1-08, planned duration 1 year (e.g., Black polyethylene, Bleach, Clear polyethylene, Non-hybrid seeds, Soil testing equipment).
- Items with priority 2: Purchase by 10-1-08, planned duration 1 year (e.g., Herb seeds, Misters for seedlings, Organic fertilizers, Perennial flowerseeds, Styrofoam cups).
- Items with priority 3: Purchase by 4-1-08 or 7-1-08, planned duration 30 days to 90 days (e.g., Garden hoses, Hoe, Pull wagon, Sprayer/Pumper, Wheelbarrow).

3. Organize and Store Supplies:
- Keep all items in a dry, accessible location.
- Label containers with the item name, quantity, purchase date, and expiration date where applicable.
- Store hazardous materials (e.g., bug sprays) separately from food and water sources.

4. Maintain Inventory:
- Regularly check supplies to ensure they are not expired or damaged.
- Replace items as needed based on usage and planned duration.

5. Use with Caution:
- When using chemical sprays, follow safety guidelines and wear protective gear (e.g., gloves, masks).
- Use Sevin for vegetables and other insecticides for flower gardens only.
- Keep all chemicals out of reach of children and pets.
garden supplies, inventory list, gardening preparation, seed storage, pest control, tool maintenance

Hydroponics (1) -

Using Hydroponics for Post-Nuclear Food Production
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Overview: Hydroponics is an effective method of growing food without soil, ideal for post-nuclear environments where traditional gardening may be difficult.

Steps:
1. Set up a hydroponic system: Use hobbyist hydroponic tanks that can be...
Overview: Hydroponics is an effective method of growing food without soil, ideal for post-nuclear environments where traditional gardening may be difficult.

Steps:
1. Set up a hydroponic system: Use hobbyist hydroponic tanks that can be adapted to automatic care systems in greenhouses or indoor spaces.
2. Choose suitable crops: Hydroponics is particularly useful for growing high-yield plants such as tomatoes, lettuce, and herbs in limited space.
3. Use artificial nutrient solutions: Provide plants with a balanced mix of nutrients through water-based solutions instead of soil.
4. Ensure proper lighting: Use grow lights to simulate sunlight if natural light is not available or is harmful due to UV radiation.
5. Maintain system cleanliness: Regularly clean the hydroponic system to prevent algae growth and ensure optimal plant health.
6. Monitor water pH and nutrient levels: Keep a close eye on the pH of the water and adjust nutrient concentrations as needed for different stages of plant growth.

Notes:
• Hydroponics can be adapted to both small-scale home use and large-scale post-nuclear food production.
• Refer to resources such as *Home Hydroponics and How to Do It* by Lem Jones or *Your Own Food Factory* by Jim McKeever for detailed guidance.
hydroponics, post-nuclear food production, nutrient solutions, grow lights, indoor gardening

Uv Protection (1) -

Protecting Crops from UV Radiation Post-Nuclear War
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Overview: Increased ultraviolet (UV) radiation after a nuclear event can damage sensitive crops. This procedure outlines how to protect plants from UV exposure.

Steps:
1. Identify UV-sensitive crops: From Table 9, note that peas,...
Overview: Increased ultraviolet (UV) radiation after a nuclear event can damage sensitive crops. This procedure outlines how to protect plants from UV exposure.

Steps:
1. Identify UV-sensitive crops: From Table 9, note that peas, tomatoes, beans, pumpkin, okra, onions, endive, watermelon, cucumber, turnip, beets, spinach, lettuce, eggplant, potato, and cabbage are extremely or moderately sensitive to UV radiation.
2. Use UV-resistant crops: Include resistant crops such as wheat, rye, soybeans, barley, alfalfa, and corn in your garden plan for long-term survival.
3. Build protective greenhouses: Construct wood frame, plastic-covered greenhouses that block out 80% or more of incoming UV radiation. These can be stored as precut wood parts and compact rolls of plastic.
4. Use hydroponics indoors: Grow sensitive crops like onions and peas in indoor hydroponic systems to avoid direct UV exposure.
5. Monitor UV levels over time: UV levels may decrease after the first few years post-nuclear war, making some previously sensitive crops viable again.
6. Supplement with resistant crops: Always include a surplus of UV-resistant crops in your seed stockpile as a backup for survival.

Notes:
• Greenhouses and hydroponics are essential for growing food in high-UV environments.
• The scientific data on UV sensitivity is based on studies from the National Academy of Sciences (1975) and M.G. Cline and F.B. Salisbury (1966).
UV radiation, greenhouse construction, UV-resistant crops, post-nuclear gardening, hydroponics

Soil Management And Crop Rotation (1) -

Crop Rotations for Arable Land Management on Various Soil Types
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure Overview:

There are multiple crop rotations tailored to different soil types, each with specific sequences and management practices:

1. Second Rotation (Loams and Clays):
• Sequence: Fallow with dung → Wheat → Drilled beans,...
Procedure Overview:

There are multiple crop rotations tailored to different soil types, each with specific sequences and management practices:

1. Second Rotation (Loams and Clays):
• Sequence: Fallow with dung → Wheat → Drilled beans, horse-hoed → Barley → Clover and rye-grass → Oats or wheat → Drilled beans, horse-hoed → Wheat.
• Soil Suitability: Loams and clays where the first rotation may not be advisable.
• Labor Distribution: Labor is more evenly distributed compared to other rotations.
• Grain Cultivation: Grains like wheat, barley, and oats are cultivated generally in this system.
• Manure Application: Dung must be administered on clover stubble for the rotation to succeed; otherwise, crops may be inferior in later years.

2. Third Rotation (Inferior Clays and Loams):
• Sequence: Fallow with dung → Wheat → Clover and ryegrass → Oats → Drilled beans, horse-hoed → Wheat.
• Soil Suitability: Designed for clays and loams of lower quality than those in the second rotation.
• Good Husbandry Practices: Rules of good husbandry are carefully followed to maintain soil health and produce high-value crops.
• Manure Application: Apply manure either on clover stubble or before sowing beans for optimal results.
• Rotation Effectiveness: One of the best rotations for specified soils if manure is properly applied.

3. Fourth Rotation (Thin Clays):
• Sequence: Fallow with dung → Wheat → Grass, pastured but not too early eaten → Grass → Grass → Oats.
• Soil Suitability: Suitable for thin clays where gentle husbandry is essential to avoid soil exhaustion.
• Grass Management: Allow grass to be pastured but not overgrazed; this helps maintain soil health while producing low-value crops temporarily.
• Manure Application: Additional manure must be procured and applied if the land does not improve sufficiently under grass.
• Rotation Flexibility: The rotation can be shortened or lengthened based on circumstances, but should never extend beyond when dung can be given to the fallow break.
• Critical Manure Use: Applying dung to the fallow break is essential for the success of this rotation; neglecting it renders the entire system ineffective.
crop rotation, soil management, agriculture, loams, clays, manure application, wheat, barley, oats, beans, clover, ryegrass

Soil Management & Crop Rotation (1) -

Implementing Rotations for Light and Sandy Soils
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Sixth Rotation Procedure (for Light Soils):

1. Crop Selection (Year 1): Plant turnips as the first crop in this rotation.
2. Grain Crop (Year 2): Follow with either spring wheat or barley as the second crop.
3. **Legume and...
Sixth Rotation Procedure (for Light Soils):

1. Crop Selection (Year 1): Plant turnips as the first crop in this rotation.
2. Grain Crop (Year 2): Follow with either spring wheat or barley as the second crop.
3. Legume and Grass Mix (Year 3): Sow a combination of clover and rye-grass to improve soil fertility.
4. Grain Rotation (Year 4): Plant either oats or wheat in the fourth year.

Extended Rotation Recommendations for Light Soils:
• Extend the rotation to eight years for better results, including:
- In the second year, sow seeds for pasture.
- Keep the land under grass for three years.
- Break up the land for oats in the sixth year.
- Drill with beans and peas in the seventh year.
- Sow wheat in the eighth year.

Notes: This extended rotation includes all branches of husbandry, provides variety in management, and is favorable to cultivators. If the land is only kept under grass for one year, a six-year rotation may be used, though this results in less optimal outcomes.


Seventh Rotation Procedure (for Sandy Soils):

1. Crop Selection (Year 1): Plant turnips that are consumed on the ground, ensuring they are fully utilized.
2. Grain Crop (Year 2): Follow with barley as the second crop in this rotation.
3. Grass Utilization (Year 3): Allow the land to be used for grass during the third year to improve soil structure and fertility.
4. Grain Rotation (Year 4): Plant either rye or oats in the fourth year.

Extended Rotation Recommendations for Sandy Soils:
• Extend the rotation by keeping the land under grass for three years, increasing the total duration of the rotation to six years, which is highly advisable.

Notes: Sandy soils require proper manuring with alluvial compost, marl, or clay to provide strength and body that they naturally lack. Barley, oats, and rye are more suitable crops on sandy soils than wheat, especially in favorable seasons.
crop rotation, light soil, sandy soil, turnips, barley, oats, rye, grass utilization, soil fertility

Soil Fertility And Crop Management (1) -

Utilize Guano as a Fertilizer
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Purpose: To use guano (bird excrement) to improve soil productivity and enhance crop growth.

Procedure:
1. Obtain guano from islands in the South Sea where sea-fowl excrements have accumulated over long periods.
2. Determine the appropriate...
Purpose: To use guano (bird excrement) to improve soil productivity and enhance crop growth.

Procedure:
1. Obtain guano from islands in the South Sea where sea-fowl excrements have accumulated over long periods.
2. Determine the appropriate quantity of guano based on the size of your field (e.g., 300 to 400 pounds per acre for potatoes).
3. Apply the guano to soils that are deficient in organic matter, particularly for nitrogen-rich crops such as wheat, rye, barley, and potatoes.
4. For potato cultivation, mix the guano with bone dust for better nutrient retention and long-term benefits.
5. Spread the guano evenly across the soil in a drill before planting, ensuring full coverage.
6. Mix the guano into the topsoil to enhance its fertility and productivity.
7. Water the area lightly to help the nutrients dissolve and reach the plant roots.

Notes:
• Guano contains ammonia, uric acid, phosphoric acid, oxalic acid, carbonic acid, and earthy salts.
• It is particularly effective for improving the growth of nitrogen-rich crops such as wheat, rye, barley, and potatoes, promoting healthy tuber development and increased yield.
• The addition of bone dust enhances the effectiveness of guano by providing additional phosphorus and calcium.
• Guano should be applied in appropriate quantities to avoid over-fertilization or nutrient imbalances in the soil.
guano, fertilizer, soil productivity, potatoes, wheat, rye, barley, bone dust

Silkworm Cultivation (1) -

Identify When Silk Worms Are Ready to Spin Cocoons and Prepare the Hedge for Cocoon Spinning
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To determine when silk worms are ready to spin their cocoons, look for these signs:

1. Behavioral Change: When leaves are placed on the feeding frames, the worms do not eat them but instead raise their heads as if searching for something...
To determine when silk worms are ready to spin their cocoons, look for these signs:

1. Behavioral Change: When leaves are placed on the feeding frames, the worms do not eat them but instead raise their heads as if searching for something else.
2. Transparency and Color: When viewed horizontally, light shines through the worms, giving them a whitish-yellow transparent appearance.
3. Movement Patterns: Worms that were previously fastened to the edges of the feeding frames now move slowly along them, showing an instinctive urge for change in position.
4. Migration Behavior: Worms begin to leave the center of the feeding frames and attempt to reach the edges or crawl onto them.
5. Color Change: The worms' rings draw inward, and their greenish color changes to a deep golden hue.
6. Physical Changes: Their skin becomes wrinkled around the neck, and their bodies feel softer than before, resembling soft dough.
7. Transparency Test: When held up to light, the

Preparation of the Hedge for Cocoon Spinning:

Materials Needed: Twigs from chestnut, hickory, oak, or birch (used in stable-brooms).

Steps:
1. Procure and Prepare Twigs: A week or ten days before the worms are ready to spin their cocoons, gather bundles of twigs.
2. Arrange Twigs: Place the twig bundles in feeding trays with approximately 15 inches between each bundle.
3. Positioning: Ensure that the top branches of the twig bundles touch the lower part of the tray above them, forming an arch. Angle the bundles slightly so that worms can climb without falling off.
4. Spread Out Twigs: Arrange the twigs in a fan-like shape to allow air circulation and ease for the worms as they work on their cocoons.
5. Create Openings: Leave openings at the tops of the curves where the worms can form their cocoons.

Additional Preparations:
• Clean the feeding frames thoroughly before placing the twigs.
• Ensure the room is well ventilated.
• Place the worms ready to spin near the prepared hedge.
• Provide a few le
silkworms, cocoon spinning, preparation for cocooning, identifying readiness, twigs for silkworms

Silk Production (1) -

Gather and Select Silk Worm Cocoons for Preservation
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to Gather and Choose Cocoons:

1. Timing of Harvesting:
- Strong, healthy silk worms complete their cocoons in three days and a half at farthest from the moment they first begin casting the floss.
- The period may be shorter if...
Steps to Gather and Choose Cocoons:

1. Timing of Harvesting:
- Strong, healthy silk worms complete their cocoons in three days and a half at farthest from the moment they first begin casting the floss.
- The period may be shorter if the silk worms spin the silk in higher temperatures or very dry air.
- It is better not to take off the cocoon before the eighth or ninth day, reckoning from when the silk-worm first rose. They may be taken on the seventh day only if the laboratory conditions are regular and precise enough to know with certainty.

2. Collect All Cocoons: Carefully gather all the cocoons produced by the silk worms.

3. Gathering Process:
- Begin gathering from the lower tier of hurdles.
- Take the cabins down gently and give them to those who will gather the cocoons.
- Place a basket between two gatherers to receive the cocoons.
- Another person should collect the stripped bushes, which may be laid by for another year.

4. Select for Egg Production: From the collected cocoons, choose those that are of high quality and suitable for preserving the eggs. Look for firm, well-formed cocoons without any signs of damage or disease.

5. Preserve Selected Cocoons: Ensure that the selected cocoons are stored in a clean, dry environment until the moths emerge from them.

6. Sorting Cocoons:
- All the cocoons that have a certain consistency and feel soft should be
silk worms, cocoons, harvesting, sorting, preservation

Livestock Management (1) -

Rearing Calves
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Take the calves from the cows in three weeks or a month.
2. In two or three days after they are calved, take the calves from the cows and put them in a house by themselves.
3. Give them nothing but a little fine hay until they...
Procedure:
1. Take the calves from the cows in three weeks or a month.
2. In two or three days after they are calved, take the calves from the cows and put them in a house by themselves.
3. Give them nothing but a little fine hay until they begin through necessity to pick a little.
4. Prepare a water gruel composed of about one-third barley and two-thirds of oats ground together very fine.
5. Sift the mixture through a very fine sieve.
6. Put it into the quantity of water below mentioned, and boil it for half an hour.
7. Take it off the fire and let it remain until it is milkwarm.
8. Give each calf about a quart in the morning and the same quantity in the evening.
9. Increase the amount as the calf grows older.
10. Cut some of the hay and mix it with bran or oats in a trough.
11. Slice some turnips about the size of a dollar, which they will soon by licking learn to eat.
12. After this, give them turnips enough.
13. After the calves have had this diet for about a week or ten days, tie up a little bundle of hay and put it in the middle of the house, which they will by degrees come to eat.
14. Put a little of the meal abovementioned in a small trough for them to eat occasionally.
15. Keep them in this manner until they are of proper age to turn out to grass, before which they must be at least two months old.
calves, rearing, feeding, hay, gruel, barley, oats, turnips

Forage Production (1) -

Select Blue Grama and Sideoats Grama Varieties for Forage Production
Reference Source: forage guide
Procedure:

Blue Grama Varieties:
1. Choose ‘Hachita’: A superior forage producer with better than average drought tolerance and good seed availability.
2. Consider ‘Lovington’: Offers good seedling vigor, leafiness, and drought...
Procedure:

Blue Grama Varieties:
1. Choose ‘Hachita’: A superior forage producer with better than average drought tolerance and good seed availability.
2. Consider ‘Lovington’: Offers good seedling vigor, leafiness, and drought establishment but has limited seed supply.
3. Select ‘Alma’: Provides similar forage productivity and crude protein content to ‘Hachita’ with better seedling emergence.

Sideoats Grama Varieties:
1. Choose ‘El Reno’: Offers good leafiness, vigor, production potential, and winter hardiness with common seed availability.
2. Consider ‘Vaughn’: Provides good seedling vigor and drought tolerance but has low palatability and is easy to establish.
3. Select ‘Niner’: Winter hardy with more even maturing than ‘Vaughn’, suitable for areas with limited seed supply.
4. Choose ‘Butte’: Long-lived, winter hardy, and earlier maturing, ideal for short growing seasons.
5. Consider ‘Pierre’: Exceeds in vigor and leafiness, weakly rhizomatous, and well-suited for areas north and east of Colorado.
Blue Grama, Sideoats Grama, forage production, variety selection, drought tolerance, seed availability

Forage Management (1) -

Plant and Manage White Clover for Pasture Use
Standard Source: forage guide
Purpose: Plant and manage white clover (including intermediate and small-type varieties) to optimize pasture quality, grazing efficiency, and reduce hazards.

Procedure:
1. Inoculation:
- Inoculate white clover seed with appropriate...
Purpose: Plant and manage white clover (including intermediate and small-type varieties) to optimize pasture quality, grazing efficiency, and reduce hazards.

Procedure:
1. Inoculation:
- Inoculate white clover seed with appropriate rhizobia before planting for best growth.
2. Seeding Timing:
- Seed four to six weeks before the dry, hot season arrives.
3. Mixing with Grasses:
- Mix white clover with irrigated grasses for best pasture use.
4. Grazing Management:
- More than 50% white clover in a pasture can cause bloat in cattle and sheep.
- Frequent grazing of cool-season grasses in the spring favors clover growth and maintenance in mixed stands.

Characteristics:
• White clover includes multiple types, such as intermediate (also known as common or White Dutch; height varies between tall and small) and small-type (also known as low-growing or wild type; commonly found in lawns).
• Intermediate white clover typically persists for two to three years.
• Small-type white clover regrows rapidly but is too short for hay fields and may be shaded out by tall grasses.

Varieties:
• ‘Grassland Huia’ is an improved intermediate variety from New Zealand with limited seed availability.
• ‘Kent Wild’ is an improved small-type variety from New Zealand with limited seed availability.

Notes:
• White clover contains estrogens that may induce female animal reproductive problems.
• Intermediate white clover is well-suited for mixed pasture systems but has a shorter lifespan than other varieties.
• Small-type white clover is not a heavy producer but persists well under grazing pressure.
white clover, pasture management, forage crops, grazing, inoculation, bloat prevention, variety selection

Seed Saving & Crop Production (1) -

Understand the In-Field Needs and Soil Preparation for Seed Crops
Standard Source: seed saving guide
To successfully grow seed crops:

1. Recognize that the in-field needs of seed crops are similar to those of vegetable crops.
2. Focus on the differences between seed crops and vegetable crops in terms of cultivation.
3. Consider the following...
To successfully grow seed crops:

1. Recognize that the in-field needs of seed crops are similar to those of vegetable crops.
2. Focus on the differences between seed crops and vegetable crops in terms of cultivation.
3. Consider the following key elements for successful seed production:
- Soil preparation
- Spacing
- Staking and trellising
- Managing disease
- Managing weeds

Soil Preparation for Seed Crops

Seed crops require fertile, nutritionally balanced soil with:
• Good drainage
• High organic matter content
• A friable (crumbly) texture

Since seed crops are often in the ground longer than vegetable crops, ensure a slow release source of fertility to nourish them throughout their entire life cycle.

Provide an adequate source of phosphorous, as it is crucial for good flowering and seed set.

Striking the right balance in nitrogen availability is also very important:
• Too little nitrogen: results in stunted plants and lower seed yields
• Too much nitrogen: can lead to:
- Excessive vegetative growth instead of flowers and seeds
- Increased susceptibility to lodging (falling over)
- Greater vulnerability to insect and disease pests, potentially reducing seed yield

In general, provide the same amount of nitrogen (or slightly less) that is commonly recommended for your crop as a vegetable. This will likely strike the right balance.
seed crops, soil preparation, fertility management, phosphorous, nitrogen balance, crop cultivation

Preservation And Storage (1) -

Selecting High-Quality Vegetables for Freezing
Reference Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Choose the best vegetables: Select vegetables that are fresh, firm, and free from blemishes or signs of decay.
2. Inspect thoroughly: Check for any visible damage, mold, or signs of insect infestation.
3. **Use immediately...
Procedure:

1. Choose the best vegetables: Select vegetables that are fresh, firm, and free from blemishes or signs of decay.
2. Inspect thoroughly: Check for any visible damage, mold, or signs of insect infestation.
3. Use immediately or store properly: If not using immediately, store in a cool, dry place until ready to freeze.

Important Notes:
• High-quality vegetables are essential for preserving flavor and texture during freezing.
• Always handle vegetables with clean hands and utensils to avoid contamination.

Additional Tips from Version 2:
• Choose vegetables that are tender, ripe but barely ready to eat, and as fresh as possible.
• Slightly under-mature vegetables are better than overripe ones.
• For best flavor, freeze vegetables within two hours of harvesting.

If you cannot freeze them immediately:
1. Cool the vegetables quickly in ice water.
2. Drain well after cooling.
3. Keep refrigerated until ready to prepare for freezing.

Ice:
• Use one pound of ice per pound of vegetables to maintain cold temperatures during cooling.
• Store ice in heavy-duty plastic bags or frozen milk cartons for future use.
freezing vegetables, vegetable selection, preserving vegetables, storing vegetables, ice water cooling

Pinks (1) -

Soil Preparation for Pinks (Carnations and Picotees)
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Soil Composition: Prepare a mix of three parts loam, one part cow manure, and one part sandy peat, with a small amount of sifted old plaster.
2. Planting Method: Pinks can be raised from layers, pipings, or seeds.
3....
Procedure:

1. Soil Composition: Prepare a mix of three parts loam, one part cow manure, and one part sandy peat, with a small amount of sifted old plaster.
2. Planting Method: Pinks can be raised from layers, pipings, or seeds.
3. Pipings Technique: Cut off superfluous shoots (pipings) and pot them in compost surrounded by moist sand.
4. Seed Sowing: In spring, sow seeds in similar pots or pans, or directly in open beds.
5. Northern States Consideration: In the Northern States, pinks should be potted for winter protection as early as October.
soil preparation, pinks, pipings, seed sowing

Composting Techniques (1) -

Sift Finished Compost for Uniformity
Reference Source: JG Complete Guide to Home Composting
Why Sift:
• Finished compost may contain larger pieces of woody material that haven’t fully degraded.
• Sifting ensures more of the compost works immediately in your garden.

How to Sift:
1. Use a compost sifter (commercial or homemade) to...
Why Sift:
• Finished compost may contain larger pieces of woody material that haven’t fully degraded.
• Sifting ensures more of the compost works immediately in your garden.

How to Sift:
1. Use a compost sifter (commercial or homemade) to screen finished compost.
2. Separate sifted compost from larger, undegraded pieces.
3. Return unsifted material back into the compost pile for further decomposition.

Benefits of Sifting:
• Creates a uniform, soil-like texture that is easier for plants to utilize.
• Maximizes the effectiveness of your compost in improving garden health.

Tip: Compost sifters are simple and inexpensive to make. Instructions can be found in supplemental information at the back of this guide.
compost sifter, finished compost, screening compost, composting tools

Planting Herbs (1) -

Plant Summer and Winter Savory Seeds
Standard Source: Vegetable Gardening Encyclopedia
To plant summer and winter savory seeds:

1. Choose well-drained soil for both varieties.
2. Work a complete, well-balanced fertilizer into the ground at a rate of one pound per 100 square feet before planting.
3. Plant seeds **half an inch...
To plant summer and winter savory seeds:

1. Choose well-drained soil for both varieties.
2. Work a complete, well-balanced fertilizer into the ground at a rate of one pound per 100 square feet before planting.
3. Plant seeds half an inch deep in rows that are 12 to 18 inches apart.
4. Ensure both varieties receive full sun for optimal growth.
5. When seedlings are four to six weeks old, thin summer savory plants to stand three to four inches apart.
6. Thin winter savory plants to 12 to 18 inches apart.

Note: Both types of savory belong to the mint family and have needle-shaped leaves and square stems that become woody as they develop.
summer savory,winter savory,planting herbs,gardening
